00 / Foundations

A bilingual media-monitoring platform for Palestine/Israel social media, documented end-to-end.

Two services — a data app (FastAPI, separate team) that ingests upstream content and runs the NLP pipelines, and a client app (Express + React) that surfaces the results to bilingual analysts. This reference compresses what each surface does, the methodology that justifies the claims it makes, how the prompts and pipelines are assembled, what the database actually holds versus what the schema promises, and where the contracts between the two teams sit.

02 / Foundations

Product.

A monitoring surface for people whose job is to know what the conversation is, not to consume it. Bilingual, RTL-first, daily cadence, deliberately narrow in scope.

What problem the product solves

The product gives a small group of practitioners a daily, structured read of what is being said in Palestinian and Israeli social media and news about the Palestine/Israel situation. It collapses a high-volume, mostly Arabic, mostly Telegram (with Facebook and Twitter mixed in) stream into a per-day set of named topics with engagement, sentiment, key actors, viral posts, and source breakdowns — so a working analyst can see what changed today without scrolling raw feeds.

It is not a news reader. It is a monitoring surface for people whose job is to know what the conversation is, not to consume it.

Who the user is

A bilingual (Arabic-primary, English-comfortable) analyst, researcher, or journalist whose work involves tracking Palestinian and Israeli media narratives day by day. They have a recurring information need (daily or weekly), not a one-off curiosity. Technically literate enough to understand they are reading the output of an NLP pipeline rather than editorial judgement. They know the domain well enough to recognize when an entity name is garbled or a topic is mis-classified — they want the system to be honest about its uncertainty rather than smoothing it over.

Daily and weekly workflow

Core daily

Today's picture

Open the app, land on Daily Reports, pick a date and category lane, scan topics (title, summary, sentiment, dominant platform, engagement, key actors, viral posts), drill into one for source breakdown, temporal pattern, related topics in the same day's reports.

Optionally: ask the AI assistant about a topic, run an X-monitor session against a keyword, open the Analytics Board for cross-topic statistics, or open the Posts Explorer to look at raw posts.

Core weekly

The aggregate

Receive (or generate) a weekly report covering the same monitoring surface, locale-filtered (PS / IL / INT). Read it in-app or export it.

Optionally collect findings into the Report Workbench (evidence basket) and assemble a draft for distribution.

Domain scope

Currently scoped to Palestine/Israel-focused media monitoring. The data-app upstream, the category taxonomy (PS_*, IL_*, INT_*), the language pair (Arabic + English), and the publisher mix all reflect that scope. Architecturally the system is not tied to that domain — the same shape of system would work for any geography/topic-pair — but everything currently operating in production is. Stated explicitly so a future change of scope is a deliberate decision and not drift.

What the product is NOT

Negative definitions are load-bearing here. Several past feature proposals — real-time alerting, sentiment-comparison features, AI narrative synthesis — were declined because they would have implicitly made the product into one of the things on this list.

Not

A sentiment-analysis platform

Sentiment is one signal among several. The product surfaces it; it does not make sentiment-quality claims.

Not

A general-purpose social-listening tool

Curated corpus, domain-specific categories, NLP pipelines tuned for one domain.

Not

A news aggregator for end consumers

The audience is practitioners; surfaces show NLP-derived structure, not editorial digests.

Not

A real-time alerting system

Reports are produced on a daily cadence by the data app. There is no streaming alert path.

Not

A fact-checking product

The system shows what is being said, not whether it is true. Verification labels in the taxonomy reflect publisher mix, not adjudication.

Not

An LLM narrative synthesizer

The workbench presents what the user has collected. AI assistance there is for finishing/formatting, not for inventing entities or claims not in the source.

Seven product principles

Each principle is a direct application of the methodology in section 04. The connection is noted on each.

  1. Integrity over polish. No feature ships presenting confidence the data can't support. Applied: the ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES flag gating off synthesis and shared-entities; rejecting bare percentage labels with no measurement basis; rejecting LLM-generated narrative that introduced entities not in source material. Methodology link: confidence-tier discipline (§5).
  2. Honest labeling. Every claim the UI makes must be verifiable in the data. The bilingual error fields (error / errorAr) and the "no shared sources" affordance on topic-connections are small expressions of the same rule. Methodology link: descriptive vs inferential distinction (§8).
  3. Client-side only, for client-app features. The data app is not a dumping ground for new feature complexity. If a client-side feature would require data-app changes, that decision is escalated separately and tracked in the data app's own queue.
  4. Bilingual parity. Arabic and English receive equal attention; RTL/LTR is handled at layout level, not patched per-surface. Methodology link: NLP/language bias mitigation (§3 Bias 7).
  5. Same-day product scope (Topic Connections specifically). Topic-connections is within-report, same-day. Cross-day linkage has been rejected on this basis; any future "linked across time" proposal is a redefinition of the surface, not an extension. Methodology link: temporal bias control (§3 Bias 3).
  6. Preserve the data plumbing when cutting a feature. When a feature is gated off because the data isn't ready, the underlying computation, types, and fields stay in place so reactivation is a one-line flip. This is in tension with "delete dead code" — when the deadness is upstream-conditional, we preserve.
  7. Surface degradation, don't paper over it. When a field is inconsistent (mixed casings, mixed languages, partial population), surface the uncertainty or hide the dependent feature; don't silently coerce values into a guessed canonical form. Methodology link: AAPOR transparency principle (§9).
00 / 28 · Overview Hub for all 28 sections — start here
01 / Foundations

Media Monitoring System — Product & System Reference.

The canonical product and system reference. Describes what the product does, who it serves, how the major pieces fit together, and the principles that govern feature decisions. Last updated 2026-05-21 (Task #516). Source: system-reference.md.

The three-doc canonical reference set

This document is the narrative companion to two more mechanical references. Where it touches a topic that lives in depth in one of the other two, it references that doc and stops; it does not duplicate.

  • This document (System Reference) — product, architecture, principles, glossary, open tensions.
  • §19 Pipeline C Architecture (docs/pipeline-c-architecture.md) — operational pipeline doc (stages, contracts, persistence shape, operator surfaces).
  • §16 Data Inventory (docs/data-inventory.md) — field-level reality check (what is in the data, how reliable each field is, what the client can lean on).

Read all three together when scoping new work.

§1 — Product overview

1.1 · What problem the product solves

The product gives a small group of practitioners a daily, structured read of what is being said in Palestinian and Israeli social media and news about the Palestine/Israel situation. It collapses a high-volume, mostly Arabic, mostly Telegram (with Facebook and Twitter mixed in) stream into a per-day set of named topics with engagement, sentiment, key actors, viral posts, and source breakdowns — so a working analyst can see what changed today without scrolling raw feeds.

It is not a news reader. It is a monitoring surface for people whose job is to know what the conversation is, not to consume it.

1.2 · Who the user is

A bilingual (Arabic-primary, English-comfortable) analyst, researcher, or journalist whose work involves tracking Palestinian and Israeli media narratives day by day. They have a recurring information need (daily or weekly), not a one-off curiosity. They are technically literate enough to understand they are reading the output of an NLP pipeline, not editorial judgement, and they know the domain well enough to recognise when an entity name is garbled or a topic is mis-classified — they want the system to be honest about its uncertainty rather than smoothing it over.

The product is not built for a general-public news consumer. The language, the density of topics, the assumption of prior context, and the RTL-first design all point at the practitioner archetype.

1.3 · What the user does with the product
Core daily workflow
  1. Open the app, land on Daily Reports.
  2. Pick a date (today by default). Pick a category lane or stay on "All Topics".
  3. Scan the day's topics — title, brief summary, sentiment, dominant platform, engagement, key actors, viral posts.
  4. Drill into a topic of interest: see its source breakdown, temporal pattern, related topics in the same day's reports.
  5. Optionally, branch out: ask the AI assistant about a topic, run an X-monitor session against a specific keyword, open the Analytics Intelligence Board for cross-topic statistics, or open the Posts Explorer to look at raw posts directly.
  6. Optionally, collect what they found into the Report Workbench (evidence basket) and assemble a draft for distribution.
Core weekly workflow

Receive (or generate) a weekly report covering the same monitoring surface, locale-filtered (PS / IL / INT). Read it in-app or export it.

The user leaves either with a sense of "today's picture" or with an artifact (a workbench draft, a weekly report export) they can share.

Pipeline C visibility — as of this writing

The unified-corpus pipeline currently surfaces only on the data-app's operator dashboard — admins can inspect runs, prompts, viewpoints, the parallel-run comparison window, and the publish-readiness checklist there (see §08 Admin Portals for the authoritative map). The client app does not yet expose a unified-report viewing path to end users; the client continues to render the segmented Pipeline B daily reports it has always rendered. The convergence work that will graduate Pipeline C onto the client side is queued under Plans A/B/C (see §7).

1.4 · Domain scope

Currently scoped to Palestine/Israel-focused media monitoring. The data-app upstream, the category taxonomy (PS_*, IL_*, INT_*), the language pair (Arabic + English, Arabic-primary), and the publisher mix all reflect that scope. Nothing in the client architecture is intrinsically tied to that domain — the same shape of system would work for any geography/topic-pair — but everything currently operating in production is. State this explicitly so a future change of scope is a deliberate decision and not a drift. This is scope of the product, not of the architecture.

1.5 · What the product is NOT

These negative definitions are load-bearing. Several past feature proposals (real-time alerting, sentiment-comparison features, AI narrative synthesis) were declined because they would have implicitly made the product into one of the things on this list.

  • Not a sentiment-analysis platform. Sentiment is one of several signals the data app produces. The product surfaces it; it does not make sentiment-quality claims.
  • Not a general-purpose social-listening tool. The corpus is curated, the categories are domain-specific, and the analysis pipelines are tuned for one domain.
  • Not a news aggregator for end consumers. The audience is practitioners; the surfaces show NLP-derived structure, not editorial digests for a general reader.
  • Not a real-time alerting system. Reports are produced on a daily cadence by the data app (Pipeline B per category; Pipeline C as a single whole-day unified run). The client surfaces the latest available report; there is no streaming alert path.
  • Not a fact-checking or verification product. The system shows what is being said, not whether it is true. (The category taxonomy includes verification labels because the corpus includes verification-oriented publishers, not because the product itself adjudicates claims. Pipeline C's Stage 8 post-hoc factuality check is a deterministic grounding check — does each claim trace back to a post in the cluster — not external verification.)

§2 — System architecture

2.1 · Major components
  • Data app — FastAPI + Streamlit, plus a worker layer. A separate service owned by the data team. It ingests social-media and news content, runs the NLP pipelines, and assembles per-day reports. The data app today runs two pipelines in parallel (see §3): the segmented legacy Pipeline B (per-category CSV → per-category reports) and the unified-corpus Pipeline C (whole-day clustering → unified topics). It exposes two distinct admin surfaces — Streamlit (port 5000) for CSV upload and per-topic review, FastAPI HTML (port 8000) for automation, pipeline kill-switches, the Phase 5 parallel-run window, the publish-readiness checklist, and the recovery sweep. §08 Admin Portals is the authoritative "where do I go to do X?" map. The client app never calls the underlying NLP — it consumes the data app's finished products.
  • Client app — backend (Express + TypeScript). A thin REST layer under /api/. Handles auth (sessions, 2FA, API-key for data-app webhooks), persistence via Drizzle ORM, scheduled jobs (node-cron for daily report sync and other recurring work), and the AI assistant / X-monitor sessions that talk to xAI Grok and OpenAI on the user's behalf. The backend is intentionally small: most "business logic" lives in the data app upstream or in the React client.
  • Client app — frontend (React 18 + Vite + Wouter + TanStack Query). The actual user interface. Bilingual, Arabic-primary, RTL by default, shadcn/ui + Tailwind, dark/light themed, mobile-responsive. Most derived state — overlap scoring, filtering, ranking, chart shaping — is computed here from the raw fields the backend serves.
  • Database (PostgreSQL on Neon). The system of record. On the data-app side it holds Pipeline B output in reports (with the per-day, per-category payload — including the topics array — living inside reports.report_data JSONB), raw_posts, the Pipeline C state (unified_reports, unified_topics, plus the supporting tables — see §19 Pipeline C Architecture and §16 Data Inventory for the physical inventory). On the client side it holds client-owned data (users, sessions, evidence baskets, drafts, workbench state, weekly subscriptions). Dev and production run on separate Neon databases.
Naming note — physical vs logical

Earlier versions of this doc (and surface code in the separate client repo) refer to daily_reports, topics, weekly_reports, and social_media_posts as if they were physical tables. Data Inventory §1.3 / §6 confirms those names do not exist on the surveyed DB: what the client calls "daily_reports" is the data-app's reports row, and what it calls "topics" is reports.report_data -> 'topic_analyses' inside that JSONB. When this doc uses the older logical names below (most notably in §4, which describes client-side surfaces), it is referring to the client-app's logical entities, not to physical tables. The data inventory is the source of truth for physical storage.

  • External AI services. OpenAI (o4-mini, GPT-5.2, GPT-4.1-mini) for the data app's analytical and synthesis calls; xAI Grok and OpenAI for the client app's AI assistant, X-monitor sessions, and workbench finishing. The data app's choice of model per call site is declared in the LLM_CALL_REGISTRY source of truth (see replit.md and docs/llm_analysis_prompts.md, surfaced in §17 LLM Prompts).
  • Email (Resend). Transactional client-app concerns (verification, weekly subscription delivery, OTPs). The data app also runs an email-thread watchdog for its own email-automation polling.
  • Object storage (AWS S3). The data app ingests dated-folder posts.csv files from S3 (scheduled poll plus manual triggers and backfill); freshness and concurrent-ingestion safety are guarded by dedicated smoke tests (s3-freshness-guard, ingestion-safety).
2.2 · Data flow diagram
social media + news (upstream) │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ data app │ │ (FastAPI + Streamlit + workers, separate team) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Pipeline B │ │ Pipeline C │ │ │ │ segmented │ │ unified corpus │ │ │ │ per-category │ ◀─ run │ whole-day cluster + │ │ │ │ CSV → reports │ in │ domain anchor + │ │ │ │ │ paral- │ bilingual analysis +│ │ │ │ │ lel │ viewpoints + │ │ │ │ │ ──────▶│ Stage 9 synthesis │ │ │ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ └────────┬─────────────────────┘ │ │ │ webhook (per-report; same │ │ │ endpoint contract, additive │ │ │ fields where Pipeline C extends │ │ │ — see §18 Pipeline C Architecture) │ └────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┘ ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────┐ │ client backend │ ←── reads ── │ PostgreSQL (Neon) │ │ Express, REST /api│ ── writes ─→ │ daily_reports, │ │ scheduled cron │ │ topics, raw_posts,│ │ AI session glue │ │ weekly_*, users, │ └─────────┬──────────┘ │ drafts, evidence │ │ └────────────────────┘ │ REST JSON ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────┐ │ React client │ ── calls ──→ │ xAI Grok, OpenAI, │ │ (SPA, bilingual, │ │ Resend (via server)│ │ RTL-primary) │ └────────────────────┘ │ derived state, │ │ charts, filters │ └─────────┬──────────┘ ▼ user

The mechanical detail of how Pipeline C stages chain (embeddings → HDBSCAN clustering → Jaccard merge → domain anchoring → dispatcher → synthesis → grounding check) and how it persists state lives in §19 Pipeline C Architecture. This diagram is deliberately the high-level box.

2.3 · What flows between components
  • Data app → client (Pipeline B): Complete daily-report payloads per category — a header plus a topics array plus per-topic viral posts and aggregated NLP. Push via webhook on approval; pull via admin sync endpoints.
  • Data app → client (Pipeline C): Unified daily payloads in a shape that is additive on top of the Pipeline B contract (per the convergence-round principle in §5); the mechanical translation layer is described in §19 Pipeline C Architecture and docs/pipeline-c-client-schema-parity.md. Webhook delivery is measured against a hard ~1 MB proxy ceiling — the trim and byte-budget policy lives in docs/webhook-payload-budget.md.
  • Data app → client (raw posts): raw_posts (currently Telegram-only) synced on a separate cadence, plus a daily-summary endpoint that exposes { date, unique_posts_by_platform, total_unique_posts } derived from raw_posts.
  • Client → data app: Heartbeat checks and admin-triggered sync pulls. No analytical work is sent upstream. The client never asks the data app to re-process or re-classify anything.
  • Client backend ↔ database: All reads and writes for both data-app payloads and client-owned tables.
  • Client frontend ↔ client backend: REST/JSON. TanStack Query manages the cache.
  • Client backend ↔ AI / email services: On-demand calls during AI assistant sessions, X-monitor sessions, workbench finishing, and email-triggered actions (subscription delivery, verification).
2.4 · Contractual boundaries (load-bearing)

These are the load-bearing rules. They have shaped most of our recent work and must be respected by future feature planning.

  • The client app does not add work to the data app. If a client-side feature would require a new field, a re-classification, a different aggregation, or any other data-app change, that work is scoped and tracked separately, escalated to the data-app team, and not committed to as part of the client task. Examples already on the books: the actor-enrichment investigation and the normalizedNameEn population-rate question that gates the topic-connections synthesis sentence (§7).
  • The client app is responsible for honesty about what the data supports. If a field is unreliable in production (see Data Inventory), the client must either degrade gracefully, surface the uncertainty, or hide the dependent feature behind a flag — never paper over the gap with synthesised output.
  • The data app owns analytical claims; the client owns presentation and workflow. The client may filter, sort, intersect, and chart what the data app emits. It may not invent new analytical claims (no client-side sentiment recomputation, no LLM-generated topic summaries that introduce entities not present in source material, no synthetic confidence numbers).
  • Pipeline B and Pipeline C are run in parallel; their payloads converge additively. Fields that Pipeline C adds extend the contract; they do not silently substitute Pipeline B fields. The client must be able to render either pipeline's payload without knowing which one produced it. See §5 ("no silent shape drift").

§3 — The data app

The data app is a separate service. The client app does not see its internals; it sees only the payloads the data app pushes (or the client pulls) and the operational implications of how those payloads are shaped.

What is universal across both pipelines:

  • The data app is the only component that touches raw social-media and news source material. The client never holds, fetches, or re-interprets a source post except via what the data app has already attached to a topic (viral_posts) or what it has synced into raw_posts.
  • Without the data app, the client has nothing to render. There is no fallback ingestion path.
  • The data app today runs two pipelines in parallel, both emitting per-day reports for the same source content. §3.1 and §3.2 describe each.
3.1 · Pipeline B — segmented legacy

The original pipeline. Per-category processing: each PS_* / IL_* / INT_* category CSV is treated as its own unit of analysis, run through topic clustering, per-topic LLM analysis (titles, briefs, narratives, per-topic geographic / actor / sentiment / platform / source-influence analyses), and emitted as one report per category per day. The bilingual outputs use the persona presets and category prompts catalogued in §17 LLM Prompts (which is the canonical prompt inventory for Pipeline B — that doc covers the topic_analysis, topic_analysis_retry, and executive_summary call sites, the LLM_CALL_REGISTRY, and the per-preset framework text).

Pipeline B is what the client app currently renders to end users on Daily Reports. Approvals on Pipeline B reports trigger webhook delivery to the client.

3.2 · Pipeline C — unified corpus

Pipeline C is the unified-analysis pipeline. Instead of processing each category CSV in isolation, it treats the whole day's posts as a single corpus, clusters across that corpus, anchors each cluster to one of seven canonical analytical domains (lenses), runs bilingual analysis per domain, derives viewpoints deterministically in Stage 9, and emits a single unified daily report.

Narrative stages
  1. Ingestion + embedding of the whole day's posts.
  2. HDBSCAN clustering across the unified corpus (with operator-tunable min_cluster_size, min_samples, and cluster_selection_epsilon).
  3. Deterministic keyword-Jaccard merge (Stage 5b) to collapse near-duplicate clusters.
  4. Domain anchoring — each cluster is bound to one of the seven canonical domain lenses (political, financial, social, health, security, religious, educational — see Glossary). (Source system-reference.md says "environmental"; §19 Pipeline C Architecture §4.5 cites pipeline_c_anchors.DOMAIN_KEYS at pipeline_c_anchors.py:55-63 which lists educational. The canonical key per the code is educational.)
  5. The Stage 7 per-domain dispatcher runs the eight per-domain bilingual analytical prompts; Stage 9 runs a daily synthesis prompt once per language. These nine prompts are exported for human review with a CI drift-check against the live code.
  6. Viewpoints derivation — deterministic, post-hoc, not an LLM call.
  7. Stage 8 deterministic post-hoc factuality grounding check (also not an LLM call) — verifying that each emitted claim traces back to a post in the cluster.
  8. Per-language key_voices lists with a normalised JSON shape contract.

The mechanical detail lives in §19 Pipeline C Architecture (docs/pipeline-c-architecture.md) and the cross-stage flow reference docs/pipeline-c-flow.md. The ADRs at docs/adr/ cover the individual design decisions (HDBSCAN floor, epsilon graduation, key-voices contract, etc.). Do not duplicate them here.

Pipeline C runs parallel to Pipeline B today

Both pipelines process the same day's content; both emit through the same webhook contract; the Phase 5 parallel-run comparison window — where the two pipelines' outputs are measured against the published comparison rubric — has not yet been opened (see §7). The convergence sequence on the client side is queued as Plans A/B/C (see Glossary and §7).

Prompt revision status, stated plainly

The eight per-domain analytical prompts and the Stage 9 synthesis prompt that ship with Pipeline C today are Replit-authored starter drafts pending an analyst-revision pass. They are exported to docs/pipeline-c-prompts.md precisely so that pass can review them. Until the analyst-revision pass runs (see §7), any reference to "Pipeline C's prompts" in product or roadmap discussion should carry that qualifier — they are not production-revised content. The authorship rules each revision must satisfy live in docs/prompt-authorship-guide.md, and the revision workflow itself is in docs/prompt-revision-protocol.md.

3.3 · Known limitations affecting the product experience

§16 Data Inventory is the canonical reference for field-level limitations; do not re-derive those here. The most consequential ones for product decisions are:

  • Actor enrichment is largely "unknown" and most normalizedNameEn values are empty. This has gated off the topic-connections card's synthesis sentence and shared-entities row (gated behind ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES = false), and it degrades the "Key Actors" card and "Actor Influence" list. The remediation is upstream.
  • Sentiment label and trend-direction values come back in mixed casing and mixed languages, breaking naive equality filters. sentiment_confidence is empty in practice.
  • raw_posts.nlp_actors is a JSON-encoded string, not the array the client expected — the post-exploration actor chips render silently empty until the upstream shape is fixed.
  • A handful of declared fields (viewpoints on the Pipeline B topics shape, several *_analysis text fields on legacy topics) are dead or unread on the client. They are preserved per the product principle in §5 ("preserve the data plumbing when cutting a feature").

For day-to-day product decisions, the right reflex is to consult the inventory before committing to a feature that depends on a specific field.

§4 — The client app surfaces

Surfaces, organised by user-facing meaning rather than component tree. Pages live in client/src/pages/ in the separate client repo.

Daily Reports (client-dashboard.tsx) — see §09

The product's home surface. Answers: "What's happening today (or on a chosen day) in Palestinian/Israeli social media?"

- Reads from daily_reports and topics. Data-app-fed (Pipeline B today). - Picks a date and category lane, lists the day's topics with per- topic visualisations (sentiment, key actors, viral posts, source / platform / temporal breakdowns, hashtag and word clouds). - Date navigation is unified across the recent-reports carousel, the calendar picker, and the archive sidebar; any date pick is a single canonical "go to this day" action. - The expanded topic-connections card (within-day, between two topics in the same report) shows shared keywords, locations, and sources. The synthesis sentence and shared-entities chip row are gated off pending data-app actor enrichment fixes (ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES = false). The reactivation hinges on upstream normalizedNameEn population rate (§7). - No unified-report viewing path is exposed here yet; Pipeline C output reaches end users only when the convergence work on the client (Plans A/B/C) lands.

Weekly Reports (weekly-report.tsx) — see §10

Answers: "What does the past week look like in aggregate?"

- Reads from weekly_reports. Generated by aggregateWeeklyData() with locale filtering (PS / IL / INT). - Weekly subscriptions are per-user with cadence (weekly / biweekly / custom interval), schedule day, optional auto-canvas draft generation, and HTML/JSON export. Templates store reusable configurations.

Analytics Intelligence Board (analytics-board.tsx) — see §11

Answers: "Across the corpus (or a filtered slice of it), what are the cross-topic statistical patterns?"

- Reads from raw_posts, with cross-module filter state shared across modules. - Modules: time-series activity monitor, topics heatmap, publisher leaderboard, sentiment + emotion gauge, amplification tracker, post exploration drawer, geographic-locations widget. - KPI scorecards: total posts, unique sources, % negative, originals / copies ratio.

Posts Explorer (raw-posts.tsx) — see §12

Answers: "Show me the actual posts behind the analytics."

- Reads from raw_posts. Currently Telegram-only because the raw-posts ingestion pipeline only sees Telegram (see data inventory; cf. §7). - Filters by platform, topic (boundary-aware LIKE), affiliation (ILIKE), sentiment, emotion. Watched-keyword chips actively filter and are highlighted in post content.

AI Assistant (ai-assistant.tsx) — see §13

Answers: "Help me reason about what I'm looking at."

- Wraps xAI Grok and OpenAI. Operates over topics + raw posts as context. Streaming responses with rich Markdown rendering. AI responses can be added to the evidence basket.

X Monitor (x-monitor.tsx) — see §14

Answers: "What's being said about a specific keyword right now?"

- Session-based monitoring against raw_posts plus any AI summary the user requests. Results can be added to the evidence basket.

Report Workbench (report-workbench.tsx) — see §15

Answers: "Help me assemble what I've collected into a report."

- The Evidence Basket layer collects typed blocks (AI responses, X monitor results, posts, chart snapshots, topic discoveries, narrative text) from any other surface and groups them by draft. - The canvas is a 3-zone block-based document editor (Topic Discovery on the left, central canvas, AI Report Finisher on the right) with inline editing, drag-and-drop reordering, exports (JSON / HTML / DOCX / PDF), preview, and an interactive guide. - Hard constraint: the workbench presents what the user has collected. It does not synthesise new claims; AI assistance here is for finishing/formatting, not for inventing entities or claims not in the source blocks.

Admin and account surfaces

Studio admin (studio-admin.tsx), admin dashboard (admin-dashboard.tsx), user management (user-management.tsx), user profile (user-profile.tsx), auth (auth-page.tsx, reset-password.tsx). These are operational rather than product surfaces; they exist so the product can be run, not because the analyst archetype thinks of them as part of their workflow.

Data-app operator surfaces (not part of the client app)

For completeness: the data app's own admin surfaces — the Streamlit admin on port 5000 and the FastAPI HTML operator dashboard on port 8000 — are where Pipeline C runs, prompts, viewpoints, the Phase 5 parallel-run window, the publish-readiness checklist, the kill- switches, and the recovery sweep are inspected and controlled. End users do not see them. docs/admin-portals.md is the authoritative map of which surface owns which task and how they cross-link.

---

§5 — Product principles

These have emerged organically across prior work. They are explicit here so they can be referred to in scoping rather than re-derived each time. Each one below has been re-validated against current practice.

Original seven
  • Integrity over polish. No feature ships presenting confidence the data can't support. Applied examples: the ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES flag gating off synthesis and shared- entities; rejecting bare percentage labels with no measurement basis; rejecting LLM-generated narrative that introduced entities not in source material.
  • Honest labeling. Every claim the UI makes must be verifiable in the data. No synthesised narrative that introduces entities or claims absent from the source. The bilingual error fields (error / errorAr) and the "no shared sources" affordance on topic-connections are small expressions of the same rule.
  • Client-side only, for client-app features. The data app is not a dumping ground for new feature complexity. If a client-side feature would require data-app changes, that decision is escalated separately and tracked in the data app's own queue.
  • Bilingual parity, RTL-primary. Arabic and English receive equal attention; RTL/LTR is handled at layout level, not patched per- surface. Arabic is the primary locale assumption — when a choice is forced, it is forced toward Arabic readers.
  • Same-day product scope (Topic Connections specifically). Topic- connections is within-report, same-day. Cross-day linkage has been rejected on this basis; any future "linked across time" proposal is a redefinition of the surface, not an extension.
  • Preserve the data plumbing when cutting a feature. When a feature is gated off because the data isn't ready, the underlying computation, types, and fields stay in place so reactivation is a one-line flip. This is in tension with "delete dead code" — when the deadness is upstream-conditional, we preserve.
  • Surface degradation, don't paper over it. When a field is inconsistent (mixed casings, mixed languages, partial population), surface the uncertainty or hide the dependent feature; don't silently coerce values into a guessed canonical form.
Pipeline-C-era additions
  • Audit-first for any change to the parallel-pipeline contract. Changes that affect how Pipeline B and Pipeline C emit to the client are surveyed and reviewed before they ship; the LLM_CALL_REGISTRY and the exported prompt inventory (docs/pipeline-c-prompts.md, docs/llm_analysis_prompts.md) are the audit surfaces. CI drift-checks catch silent prompt edits.
  • Honest about prompt revision status. Replit-authored starter drafts are labeled as such — in this doc, in docs/pipeline-c-prompts.md, and in any roadmap discussion — until the analyst-revision pass runs.
  • No silent shape drift between Pipeline B and Pipeline C payloads. Convergence-round fields are additive, not substitutive. The client must be able to render either payload without knowing which pipeline produced it.
  • OTPs stay on the transactional email pipeline. If a CRM or marketing email layer is ever added alongside the existing Resend setup, one-time-password and verification traffic remains on the transactional path. This is a stated rule even though no CRM layer is currently in scope, because the historical drift mode is exactly that "mixed-purpose pipeline" mistake.

§6 — Glossary highlights (Pipeline-C-era terms)

The full glossary lives in §24 Glossary & reference. The complete set of 21 entries from system-reference.md §6 is reproduced below so the canonical terminology is colocated with this narrative reference.

Report — disambiguated (daily-segmented / daily-unified / weekly)
Report (daily, segmented — Pipeline B)
A per-day, per-category envelope produced by Pipeline B. One day generates many reports (one per category; also bilingual — language is ar or en, roughly 50/50). A report contains a header (title, narrative, dates) and an array of topics. Stored in daily_reports.
Report (daily, unified — Pipeline C)
A per-day, single- envelope unified report produced by Pipeline C from the whole day's posts, with topics derived from unified-corpus clustering and analyses produced via the per-domain dispatcher. Lives in the data app's Pipeline C state today; reaches the client only via the parallel-run / convergence path (Plans A/B/C). When this doc says "report" without a qualifier, the surfaced end-user artefact today is the Pipeline B daily report.
Report (weekly)
A locale-filtered (PS / IL / INT) aggregation of a week's daily reports, with narrative sections, ranked stories, top entities, category breakdown, sentiment overview, and platform breakdown. Generated by aggregateWeeklyData(). Stored in weekly_reports.
Pipeline-C-era terms (Pipeline B, Pipeline C, parallel run, domain anchor, viewpoints, key voices, reportable cluster, convergence round, analyst revision)
Pipeline B (segmented legacy)
See §3.1. The original per- category pipeline; still the source of the daily reports the client renders today.
Pipeline C (unified corpus)
See §3.2. The unified-analysis pipeline that clusters the whole day's posts together, anchors each cluster to a domain lens, and runs bilingual per-domain analysis + Stage 9 synthesis. Runs parallel to Pipeline B today.
Parallel run
The current operational mode in which Pipeline B and Pipeline C both process every day's content. The Phase 5 parallel-run *window* (where the two outputs are compared against the rubric) has not yet been opened. The rubric is drafted at docs/unified-pipeline-phase-5-rubric.md.
Unified report
Synonym for "daily report (Pipeline C)".
Domain anchor
In Pipeline C, the single canonical analytical lens that each cluster is bound to. The seven canonical domains are political, financial, social, health, security, religious, and educational. The eight per-domain Stage 7 prompts in docs/pipeline-c-prompts.md correspond to these lenses (one carries an additional cross-domain specialisation — see that doc).
Viewpoints
The Pipeline C Stage 9 deterministic derivation that summarises the discourse positions inside a cluster. Not an LLM call; computed from the cluster's per-post and per-section signals.
Key voices
A per-language, per-cluster list of named accounts with short distinctive quotes (≤ 12 words each) emitted by the per-domain prompts. Has a normalised JSON shape contract with a recovery normaliser; the split between Arabic and English key-voices lists is the relevant subject of the Plan C round of client-side convergence.
Reportable cluster (vs noise)
A Pipeline C cluster that clears the editorial threshold to be surfaced as a topic. The HDBSCAN floor (min_cluster_size / min_samples / cluster_selection_epsilon) and the soft top-N reportable cap decide what counts; everything else is treated as noise.
Convergence round (Plans A/B/C)
The queued sequence of client-side work that brings Pipeline C output into the user- facing surfaces. Sequence: **Plan A → Plan B → Plan C**. The detailed scope of each plan lives outside this doc; the relevant property here is that they are additive (per the "no silent shape drift" principle) and Plan C in particular addresses the per- language key-voices split.
Analyst revision
The not-yet-run pass on the nine Pipeline C starter-draft prompts (eight per-domain Stage 7 prompts + Stage 9 synthesis prompt) that takes them from Replit-authored drafts to analyst-revised production prompts. Governed by docs/prompt-authorship-guide.md (rules) and docs/prompt-revision-protocol.md (workflow).
Data, post, and taxonomy terms (Source, raw vs. legacy posts, viral post, distinctive keyword, watched keyword, evidence block, category, dashboard section)
Source
Disambiguate by context: Publishing source — the upstream account/outlet that made a post (e.g. a Telegram channel, a Facebook page).; Data source — the data app, as the system that provides payloads to the client.; Signal source — within an analytical surface, the field a chart is reading from (e.g. topics.source_analytics_data).;
Post (raw vs. legacy social_media_posts)
- raw_posts — the live ingestion surface, currently Telegram- only, with NLP fields. Backs Posts Explorer, Analytics Board, AI assistant context. - social_media_posts — a residue of two earlier pivots (CSV upload + in-process LLM, then per-report posts payload). Historical rows are preserved; the table is product-irrelevant.
Viral post
A pre-baked, per-topic snippet (viral_posts JSONB on topics) chosen by the data app. Includes platform, content, URL, engagement, views. The only place Facebook/Twitter content surfaces, since raw_posts is Telegram-only.
Distinctive keyword
In topic-connections, a keyword that appears in one topic but not the other being compared (the complement of "shared keywords").
Watched keyword
A user-tracked term that drives filtering on Posts Explorer and influences report-generation context. Stored in watched_keywords.
Evidence block
A typed unit collected into the workbench (AI response, X monitor result, post, chart snapshot, topic discovery, narrative text). Stored in evidence_blocks, linked to a report_drafts row.
Category
A taxonomy slug like PS_Media, IL_Government, INT_Solidarity, plus legacy slugs that route through LEGACY_CATEGORY_MAP in shared/categories.ts.
Dashboard section
A separate, looser taxonomy on raw_posts.dashboard_section. Mixes registry slugs with values not in the registry and a known typo (INT_Verificaiton). ---

§7 — Open questions and known tensions

Resolved in the 2026-05-21 regeneration (dropped from this section)
  • The social_media_posts "still receiving inserts" question — resolved by the data inventory regeneration.
  • "Option D not yet landed, topic-connections polish held" — Option D has landed; held polish is no longer blocked by that decision.

Items that still hold

  1. Actor-enrichment limitation gates entity-driven features. A large share of actor records still come back as type "unknown" and most normalizedNameEn values are empty. This blocks the topic-connections synthesis sentence and shared-entities chip row (gated behind ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES), and it degrades the "Key Actors" card and "Actor Influence" list. The remediation is upstream; the client cannot fix this without inventing data.
  2. Sentiment-comparison and confidence-gated features remain infeasible. topics.sentiment_confidence is empty in practice and sentiment_label is mixed-language / mixed-case; any sentiment-confidence-thresholding or naive sentiment-equality feature would silently drop most rows.
  3. The product describes itself as multi-platform but live ingestion is largely Telegram. raw_posts is effectively Telegram-only; non-Telegram content reaches the user only through the pre-baked viral_posts and dominant_platform fields on topics. Whether to surface this honestly (e.g. a "Telegram only" affordance on Posts Explorer) is an open product call.

Items added in this revision

  1. Analyst-revision pass on the nine Pipeline C starter-draft prompts has not run. The eight per-domain Stage 7 prompts and the Stage 9 synthesis prompt currently in production are Replit-authored drafts; the analyst-revision pass governed by docs/prompt-revision-protocol.md is queued and not yet executed. Until it runs, anything that depends on "production-revised Pipeline C analysis" is on uncertain footing.
  2. Phase 5 parallel-run comparison window has not opened. Pipeline B and Pipeline C are both running on every day's content, but the formal comparison window — where outputs are measured against the rubric — has not yet been opened. Until it does, the parallel-run is operational, not evaluative.
  3. Plans A/B/C convergence work is queued. The client-side convergence sequence that brings Pipeline C onto end-user surfaces is queued in the order Plan A → Plan B → Plan C. None has shipped to the client yet. Any client feature that assumes "Pipeline C is the source" is premature.
  4. Topic Connections synthesis sentence (Option D reactivation) hinges on upstream normalizedNameEn population rate. Option D has landed; the synthesis sentence stays gated until normalizedNameEn population is measured and clears the threshold the feature needs. The population rate at last check was unmeasured.

Per the standing rule on this regeneration pass, no follow-on tasks are initiated from this section.

§8 — Update cadence

Like the data inventory and the pipeline-c architecture doc, this document is a living reference but requires a human trigger to stay current. Revisit when:

  • A major new surface ships (something a user would name when describing what the app does) — including any client-side graduation of Pipeline C content under Plans A/B/C.
  • A core product principle changes or a new constraint is adopted (e.g. a new "we don't do X" decision, or a change to the parallel-pipeline contract).
  • The system architecture changes materially — a new component, a removed component, a new external integration, a meaningful shift in the data-app ↔ client contract, or the resolution of the Phase 5 parallel-run window into a single-pipeline future.
  • A previously-blocked tension in §7 is resolved (the analyst-revision pass runs; normalizedNameEn is measured; Plan A lands; etc.) — strike or rewrite the item rather than letting it linger.
  • Otherwise, quarterly review for staleness.

If you read this and notice it has drifted from current reality, flag it rather than silently working around it.

§9 — Changelog

2026-05-21 · v3.5 — §04 Methodology illustrated with 9 SVG diagrams

The methodology section (§04) was already populated with the SSA framework's 10-section structure (representability problem → validity gap → 7 biases → polling theory → error margins → sampling methods → confidence labels → report language → AAPOR → references), but contained zero visual aids. Added 9 inline SVG figures to clarify the workflow:

  1. Iceberg (§1) — 1% creators / 9% engagers / 90% silent majority, with explicit waterline marking what's scrape-able vs. structurally invisible.
  2. Validity spectrum (§2) — six claim types from "issue salience" (high validity) to "population prevalence" (very low) with the containment-boundary marked between cards 4 and 5.
  3. Seven biases radial (§3) — all seven structural biases at-a-glance with each one's direction of distortion.
  4. Polling-vs-listening (§4) — five-row side-by-side comparison highlighting non-response and error model as the hard non-transfer dimensions.
  5. MoE worked example (§5) — the ±Z × √(p̂(1−p̂)/n) formula computed for n=2,000, p̂=0.63, 95% confidence, with the 60.9–65.1% CI band shown on a horizontal scale.
  6. NLP accuracy ladder (§5) — bars showing English (85–92%) → MSA Arabic (75–85%) → Dialectal Arabic (60–75%) → Code-switched (55–70%), with the practical consequence ("63% finding may reflect 55–71% true negative").
  7. Four sampling methods (§6) — Stratified Purposive / Temporal Random / Triangulation / MRP as 2×2 cards, each with theory, application, and best-for note.
  8. Confidence label decision tree (§7) — the Appendix C ASCII tree rendered as a flowchart: cross-validation gate → criteria gate → anomaly gate → one of four colored leaves (🟢 Confirmed / 🔵 Detected / 🟡 Emerging / 🔴 Artifact).
  9. AAPOR transparency wheel (§9) — the six AAPOR-aligned methodology-appendix fields as a hexagonal radial with right-side description cards.

All diagrams use the docsite's cream/teal/terracotta palette via inline SVG (no external assets, no runtime dependencies). Total added: ~45 KB of SVG; source file ssa-social-listening-methodology.md v2.1 (2024) imported in full and added to project knowledge.

2026-05-21 · Phase 5 source-fidelity pass — §5 Product Principles restored to verbatim

Seven of the eleven bullet bodies in §5 Product Principles had drifted into paraphrased / abbreviated form during prior assembly passes. Restored verbatim from 3-system-reference.md §5 under the Phase 5 Hard Rules (no paraphrasing drift). Specifically: Integrity over polish (applied examples restored — ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES, bare percentage labels, LLM-narrative-with-invented-entities); Client-side only, for client-app features (title qualifier + escalation rule restored); Same-day product scope (Topic Connections specifically) (title qualifier + redefinition-vs-extension framing restored); Preserve the data plumbing ("types, and fields" + "in tension with delete dead code" restored); Surface degradation ("mixed casings, mixed languages, partial population" parenthetical restored); Audit-first for the parallel-pipeline contract (LLM_CALL_REGISTRY, exported prompt inventory paths, CI drift-checks restored); OTPs stay transactional (rationale about historical drift mode restored).

No structural changes; no claims removed; only specificity restored. JSDOM harness re-verified. The other five source files (1-_pipeline-c-architecture.md, 2-data-inventory.md, admin-portals.md, 4-llm-prompts-reference.md, client-pipeline-c-spec.md) were re-audited under the stricter rules and required no edits.

2026-05-21 — Full regeneration pass (Task #516)

Section-level changes vs the 2026-04-23 version:

  • §1 — Refreshed the workflow walk; added the §1.3 paragraph explicitly stating that Pipeline C output is admin-only today and the client has no unified-report viewing surface yet. Added Pipeline C cadence note to the §1.5 "not real-time" bullet; clarified that Pipeline C's grounding check is not external fact-checking.
  • §2 — Architecture refreshed to show two parallel pipelines inside the data-app box. Components list expanded with the Streamlit/FastAPI two-portal model, S3 ingestion, the model registry, and dev/prod database separation. Data-flow diagram redrawn. Contract-boundary list preserved and extended with the parallel-pipeline additive-fields rule. Added an explicit physical-vs-logical naming note on the DB bullet reconciling this doc's client-side logical names with the data-app's physical schema.
  • §3 — Split into §3.1 (Pipeline B), §3.2 (Pipeline C), §3.3 (known limitations). Pipeline C subsection states explicitly that the nine prompts are Replit-authored starter drafts pending analyst revision.
  • §4 — Surface list refreshed; added an explicit "data-app operator surfaces are not part of the client app" pointer to docs/admin-portals.md. Removed the social_media_posts item from the per-surface text.
  • §5 — Existing seven principles preserved and re-validated. Added four Pipeline-C-era principles: audit-first for the parallel-pipeline contract, honest about prompt revision status, no silent shape drift, OTPs stay transactional.
  • §6 — Added ten Pipeline-C-era glossary entries (Pipeline B, Pipeline C, parallel run, unified report, domain anchor, viewpoints, key voices, reportable cluster, convergence round Plans A/B/C, analyst revision). Disambiguated "report" into daily-segmented, daily-unified, and weekly.
  • §7 — Re-validated item by item. Dropped the resolved social_media_posts-writes item and the closed Option-D-blocked topic-connections item. Kept actor-enrichment, sentiment-confidence/label, and Telegram-mostly ingestion. Added analyst-revision pass not run, Phase 5 window not opened, Plans A/B/C queued, and the normalizedNameEn-rate dependency for Option D reactivation.
  • §8 — Triggers extended for Pipeline-C-era events.
  • Companion regenerations: Data Inventory (Task #512) and Pipeline C Architecture (Task #511). The three docs are synchronised as of this date.
2026-04-23 — Initial publication

Derived from the codebase state as of this date, the data inventory, and the prior plan / task files in .local/tasks/. Captured the seven prescribed sections with the seven user-facing surfaces from client/src/pages/, the six product principles that emerged from prior work, the glossary disambiguating the raw_posts / social_media_posts and report (daily vs weekly) splits, and five open tensions.

01 / 28 · System Reference Source: system-reference.md · Task #516 · 2026-05-21 · narrative companion to §18 and §15
03 / Foundations

Architecture & data flow.

Six components, one push and one pull path between data app and client, two database connection pools, three external services. The contractual boundary that "the client app does not add work to the data app" is the constraint that has shaped most recent work.

The six components

Upstream

Data app

FastAPI service, owned by a different team. Ingests social-media and news content, runs NLP pipelines (entity resolution, sentiment, keyword extraction, topic clustering, temporal/source aggregation), assembles per-day reports. The client never calls the underlying NLP — it consumes finished products.

Client

Backend (Express + TypeScript)

A thin REST layer under /api/. Handles auth (sessions, 2FA, API-key for data-app webhooks), persistence via Drizzle ORM, scheduled jobs (node-cron for daily report sync), and the AI assistant / X-monitor sessions.

Client

Frontend (React 18)

Vite + Wouter + TanStack Query. Bilingual, RTL-first, shadcn/ui + Tailwind, dark/light themed, mobile-responsive. Most derived state — overlap scoring, filtering, ranking, chart shaping — is computed here from raw fields.

Storage

PostgreSQL on Neon

System of record. 36 tables. Holds data-app payloads (daily_reports, topics, raw_posts, weekly_reports, social_media_posts legacy) plus all client-owned data. Two connection pools separate user-facing reads from heavy batch sync work.

External

AI services

xAI Grok and OpenAI, used only by the client app's AI assistant and X-monitor surfaces, and for some report-finishing in the workbench. The data app does not depend on these — they are client-side conveniences.

External

Email (Resend)

Transactional only — verification, weekly subscription delivery, OTP, security mail. A future marketing/CRM platform is contemplated for campaign mail; OTPs and security mail will not move off Resend regardless.

Data flow

social media + news (upstream) │ ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ │ data app │ NLP pipelines, entity resolution, topic │ (FastAPI, owned │ clustering, sentiment, aggregation │ by other team) │ └─────────┬──────────┘ │ push: POST /api/receive-report (webhook, x-api-key) │ pull: client polls /api/data-app/sync* on demand ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ client backend │ ─── reads ── │ PostgreSQL (Neon) │ │ Express, REST /api │ ─── writes ─→ │ daily_reports, │ │ scheduled cron │ │ topics, raw_posts, │ │ AI session glue │ │ weekly_*, users, │ └─────────┬──────────┘ │ drafts, evidence │ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ REST JSON ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ React client │ ─── calls ──→ │ xAI Grok, OpenAI, │ │ (SPA, bilingual) │ │ Resend (via server) │ │ derived state │ └──────────────────────┘ │ charts, filters │ └─────────┬──────────┘ ▼ user

What flows between components

  • Data app → client: Complete daily-report payloads (header + topics array + per-topic viral posts and aggregated NLP). Also raw posts (currently Telegram-only) on a separate sync. Push via webhook; pull via admin sync endpoints. Same field shape both ways.
  • Client → data app: Heartbeat checks and admin-triggered sync pulls. No analytical work is sent upstream. The client never asks the data app to re-process or re-classify anything.
  • Client backend ↔ database: All reads and writes for both data-app payloads and client-owned tables.
  • Client frontend ↔ client backend: REST/JSON. TanStack Query manages the cache.
  • Client backend ↔ AI / email services: On-demand calls during AI assistant sessions, X-monitor sessions, workbench finishing, and email-triggered actions.

Where derivations happen

ComputationWhere it runsWhy
NLP (sentiment, entity, keyword, topic, geo)Data appHeavy, batch, owned by upstream team
Topic clustering (HDBSCAN) and per-topic aggregationData appSame — needs the embedding matrix
IDF-weighted Jaccard topic overlapClient (in browser)Same-day only; cheap; avoids upstream coupling
Filtering, sorting, ranking, chartingClient (in browser)User-facing interactivity; no upstream cache to invalidate
AI assistant / X-monitor / workbench finishingClient backend → external AIUser-initiated only; never in the daily-report path

Three contractual boundaries

This is the constraint that has shaped most recent work and that must be respected by future feature planning.

Boundary 1

The client app does not add work to the data app. If a client-side feature would require a new field, a re-classification, a different aggregation, or any data-app change, that work is scoped and tracked separately, escalated to the data-app team, and not committed to as part of the client task.

Boundary 2

The client app is responsible for honesty about what the data supports. If a field is unreliable in production, the client must either degrade gracefully, surface the uncertainty, or hide the dependent feature behind a flag — never paper over the gap with synthesised output.

Boundary 3

The data app owns analytical claims; the client owns presentation and workflow. The client may filter, sort, intersect, and chart what the data app emits. It may not invent new analytical claims.

03 / 28 · Architecture & Data Flow Source: System Reference §2 · Audit Briefing §2–§3
04 / Foundations

SSA Platform — Top-Level Flow & Pipeline C Internals.

Two reference diagrams with accompanying notes. Generated 2026-06-16. Scope: data-app ↔ client architecture and the Pipeline C stage chain. The figures are self-contained and theme-independent — they render on a fixed light surface regardless of the host page theme. Imported verbatim from ssa-platform-flow.html (portable section). Companion to §03 Architecture & Data Flow, §20 Pipeline C Architecture, and §21 Embedding Topology.

1. Platform overview

Upstream sources feed the data app via S3. The data app exposes two operator surfaces and runs both pipelines in parallel, then crosses a one-way contract boundary into the client app. The constraints that are easy to lose at this altitude — the second operator surface, the separate raw_posts path, the additive webhook budget, and the admin-only status of Pipeline C — are drawn explicitly rather than collapsed.

Accompanying points

  • The two converging lines under the pipelines reflect the real contract: both pipelines emit per-day reports against the same webhook endpoint, with Pipeline C's fields layered additively on top of the Pipeline B shape rather than substituting them. The client renders either payload without knowing which produced it.
  • Today only Pipeline B reaches end users. Pipeline C output is admin-only, gated behind the Plans A/B/C convergence work.
  • raw_posts sync is a distinct path on its own cadence (currently Telegram-only) with its own kill-switch; Pipeline C does not depend on it for its corpus (Stage 1 reads S3 directly), but the client daily-summary endpoint does.
  • The contract is one-directional: data flows down only. The client never asks the data app to re-process, re-classify, or add a field — any such need is scoped and escalated separately.
  • The webhook payload is measured against a hard ~1 MB proxy ceiling, which drives the trim and byte-budget policy.

2. Pipeline C internals

The stage chain that Figure 1 collapses into a single purple box, with the known gaps flagged where they actually occur. The headline is domain routing: the anchor-centroid gate is near-dormant and the LLM fallback does essentially all the disambiguation.

Accompanying points

  • Stage-order drift between two canonical docs. The route-before-merge ordering shown here follows the code-grounded architecture doc, where domain routing is Stage 4 and the Jaccard merge is Stage 5b. The narrative system-reference lists them the other way round — merge before routing. This is a live discrepancy to reconcile against the orchestrator code before anyone relies on the order.
  • unclassified is a failure sentinel, not a domain. It is the LLM-failure-only sentinel (ADR 0007), set when the fallback returns an invalid response — not a first-class cross-lens domain.
  • Eight prompts, not nine. The shipped set is eight (seven canonical domains plus the unclassified fallback), enforced by the assertion in pipeline_c_prompts.py, even though the count is sometimes referred to as nine.
  • Routing reality. The anchor-centroid gate fires on roughly 0.2% of clusters; the gpt-4.1-mini fallback performs nearly all domain disambiguation. This is either intentional tight calibration or an anchor-drift signal — it has not been adjudicated.
  • Merge dormant. The Stage 5b Jaccard merge folded zero clusters across the 14 days ending 2026-05-13 (confounded by the sub-floor coverage period). It remains a safety net, not a load-bearing quality lever.
  • Prompts are starter drafts. The eight per-domain prompts and the synthesis prompt that ship today are Replit-authored starter drafts pending the analyst-revision pass.

Color key (both figures). Overview: amber = operator/admin surfaces, teal = Pipeline B, purple = Pipeline C, coral = the one client app, blue = external services, gray = neutral I/O. Internals: purple = Pipeline C stages, gray = output, red/amber = flagged gaps.

04 / 28 · Platform Flow Source: ssa-platform-flow.html · 2 SVG figures · imported verbatim, diagrams unaltered
05 / Methodology

The science of social listening.

Theory, practice, and scientific grounding for the kind of work this product enables. Social media data is not a random sample of any population — it is a convenience sample of the vocal, connected, and platform-active subset of any given group. This section is the epistemic backbone of every claim the product surfaces: what social listening can capture reliably, what it cannot capture without correction, and the disciplines that keep findings honest. Source: SSA Methodology Framework v2.1 (2024).

Why this matters here

The product principles in section 02 — integrity over polish, honest labeling, surface degradation, don't paper over it — are direct applications of the methodology in this section. The 7-bias inventory below is what justifies the rejection of population-prevalence claims; the AAPOR transparency frame is what justifies the methodology appendix every report carries; the confidence tiers are what the velocity badges and momentum classifications report against.

In this section
  1. §1 — The representability problem
  2. §2 — The validity gap
  3. §3 — Seven structural biases
  4. §4 — Polling theory applied
  5. §5 — Error margins & statistical theory
  6. §6 — Scientific sampling methods
  7. §7 — The four-tier confidence label system
  8. §8 — Report language standards
  9. §9 — AAPOR transparency framework
  10. §10 — Key academic references

§1 — The representability problem

The core question

Can social media listening reflect on-ground public perspectives? Conditionally yes, but never without explicit qualification. Social media data is not a random sample of the public. It is a convenience sample of the vocal, connected, and platform-active subset of any given population — and that subset differs systematically from the general public in measurable ways.

Key structural facts

DimensionFigureImplication
Global social media penetration~62% (2024)38% of the world is structurally absent from social data
Active vs. passive user ratio~9:1 (lurker rule)91% of registered users never post publicly
Demographic overrepresentationYounger, urban, educated, digitally literateOlder, rural, lower-income populations systematically underrepresented
Platform self-selectionUsers choose platforms by identity and valuesPlatform choice is itself a data point, not neutral

What social listening is and is not

Social listening is a form of non-probability sampling of public digital expression. It cannot be treated as equivalent to a probability-based survey or poll. However, it generates valuable and valid data when the scope of claims is kept within its descriptive power.

Reliably captures

What social listening does well

  • The shape, volume, and trend direction of online conversation
  • Narrative frames and language patterns in use around a topic
  • Relative salience of issues and themes within a defined platform population
  • Speed and spread of emerging topics
  • Network structure of who is speaking to whom
Cannot reliably capture

Without explicit correction

  • Point-in-time prevalence of an opinion in the general population
  • The views of non-posting users (the silent majority)
  • Demographic-accurate sentiment splits
  • Predictions of offline behavior
  • Ground-truth public opinion

§2 — The validity gap

Two types of validity

The fundamental epistemological challenge of social media research is the distinction between two types of validity.

Ecological

How accurately the data reflects real-world cultural dynamics

Agenda-setting and narrative formation within online communities. Social listening has high ecological validity. It accurately describes what is being said, by whom, where, and when — within the captured population.

External

How accurately findings generalize

From the study population (social media users who post) to the target population of interest (the general public, all consumers, all citizens). Social listening has limited external validity by default, and any inferential claim requires explicit qualification and ideally cross-validation.

The validity spectrum

Claims made from social listening data exist on a spectrum from high to low validity:

Validity levelClaim typeExample
High — reliableIssue salience and volume"Mentions of [topic] increased 340% over Q3"
High — reliableNarrative and framing"The dominant frame is economic, not political"
High — reliableTrend direction"Negative sentiment has been declining consistently for 6 weeks"
Medium — qualifiedSentiment direction"The online conversation skews negative on this issue"
Low — needs cross-validationDemographic sentiment"Women aged 18–34 express more negative sentiment"
Very low — explicit caveatPopulation prevalence"63% of the public opposes this policy"

The academic consensus

The peer-reviewed literature is consistent and clear on this point:

Olteanu et al. (2019) identified five classes of data bias in social media research, concluding that "social media data is neither a representative nor a random sample of the population at large."

Hargittai (2020) in Whose Voices Are Represented in Social Science Research? demonstrated that digital trace data systematically overrepresents younger, urban, educated, and politically engaged populations — and that this skew varies significantly by platform.

The implication for practice: social data has high value as a cultural lens and limited value as a population survey — unless corrected through weighting, triangulation, or post-stratification.

§3 — Seven structural biases

Every piece of social media data carries structural biases that must be identified and disclosed. Each engagement runs through this taxonomy. Cards below give the mechanism, the direction the bias pulls findings, and the available mitigation.

Bias 1

Population bias

Mechanism

The population of social media users differs systematically from the general population. Platform users skew younger, more urban, more educated, and more digitally literate. Elderly, rural, and lower-income populations are structurally underrepresented.

Direction of distortion

Findings will overrepresent the perspectives of younger, urban, educated users. Sentiment from older demographics, rural communities, or lower-income groups is structurally absent.

Mitigation

Demographic reweighting where user-level data permits; triangulation with probability-based polls; explicit disclosure in methodology note.

Bias 2

Self-selection bias

Mechanism

Only motivated, emotionally engaged users post publicly. The vast majority — the 91% who register but never post — are invisible in social data. Those who do post are disproportionately driven by strong positive or negative emotion.

Direction of distortion

Sentiment polarity is inflated. The moderate, satisfied, or indifferent majority is structurally silent. Social data tends to make opinions look more polarized than they actually are in the full population.

Mitigation

Analyze the shape of sentiment distribution (bimodal vs. unimodal), not just the mean sentiment score. Flag when polarity skew is unusually high. Never extrapolate polarity figures to general population without cross-validation.

Bias 3

Temporal bias

Mechanism

Conversation volume spikes dramatically around news events, controversies, and viral moments. These spikes can represent 60–90% of a month's data, drowning out the organic, steady-state conversation that existed before and after the event.

Direction of distortion

Event-driven data distorts the baseline. A brand crisis, a celebrity mention, or a political moment can make it appear that public sentiment has shifted dramatically, when what has actually shifted is the volume of expressive attention — not the underlying opinion.

Mitigation

Rolling baseline windows (28-day smoothed trend); event normalization windows; isolation of event-driven spikes in reporting. Clearly distinguish event-driven from organic data periods.

Bias 4

Platform bias

Mechanism

Each platform has distinct cultural norms, demographic compositions, and discourse styles. These are not interchangeable lenses on the same public conversation — they are fundamentally different ecosystems.

PlatformCultural normDiscourse style
X (Twitter)Real-time reactionOutrage, political, breaking news
LinkedInProfessional identityOptimism, career, B2B
TikTokEntertainment and identityYouth culture, trends, humor
RedditEnthusiast communitiesIn-depth discussion, niche expertise
FacebookSocial connectionPersonal, community, local
InstagramAspiration and lifestyleVisual, brand-positive, emotional
Direction of distortion

Platform choice determines the cultural window through which a topic is viewed. Monitoring only X will produce a different — and more negative and polarized — picture than monitoring Facebook.

Mitigation

Multi-platform monitoring with documented platform weights; platform-stratified reporting; avoid single-platform findings being presented as general public opinion. Direct relevance: see Posts Explorer — current ingestion is Telegram-only.

Bias 5

Algorithmic amplification bias

Mechanism

Social media algorithms surface emotionally engaging, outrage-inducing, and shareable content. A post with 500 original authors but 5 million impressions has been algorithmically amplified — it does not represent 5 million independent opinion holders.

Direction of distortion

The reach of content is systematically skewed toward negative, extreme, and emotionally provocative content. Volume (number of posts) and reach (number of impressions) tell different stories, and conflating them produces a more negative and more extreme picture than reality.

Mitigation

Report volume (post count) and reach (impressions/engagements) as separate metrics. Clarify which figure is being used for sentiment calculations. Note high amplification ratios when present.

Bias 6

Content moderation bias

Mechanism

Platform moderation removes posts, suspends accounts, and — through shadow-banning — reduces the visibility of certain content before it is ever collected. The researcher's dataset is therefore not a sample of all expressed opinion, but of all expressed opinion that survived moderation.

Direction of distortion

Certain viewpoint categories — particularly extreme positions, conspiracy content, and coordinated inauthentic behavior — are partially censored before data collection begins. The direction of distortion depends on the platform's moderation philosophy.

Mitigation

Acknowledge in the limitations section that collected data reflects post-moderation expression. For topics where moderation is likely to be active (misinformation, extremism, coordinated campaigns), cross-check with lightly moderated platforms when relevant.

Bias 7

NLP & language bias

Mechanism

Most commercial sentiment analysis models were trained predominantly on English-language, Western-cultural text. Performance degrades significantly on: non-English languages, dialectal variation within a language, code-switching (mixing languages within a post), sarcasm, irony, cultural humor, and informal registers.

Direction of distortion

Systematic misclassification of non-standard or non-English content. For Arabic-language social data specifically: Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) vs. Egyptian, Levantine, and Gulf dialects are processed very differently by most commercial models, with dialectal Arabic producing significantly higher misclassification rates.

Mitigation

Use language-specific NLP models where available (CAMeL-Tools for Arabic, dialect-aware BERT models); conduct human validation on a minimum 5% stratified random sample (10% minimum for Arabic-primary reports); document model name and version in every report; flag known coverage limitations for non-English content. Direct relevance: see Integrity — sentiment_label mixed Arabic/English casing produces this exact failure mode in production.

§4 — Polling theory applied

The theoretical relationship

Classical probability-based polling and social media listening are complementary methodologies, not competitors. Understanding what polling theory offers — and where it fails to transfer — allows the practice to be positioned correctly and more rigorous studies to be designed.

Principles that transfer from polling

1. Define your target population explicitly

In traditional polling, the target population is specified precisely: "registered voters in the UK," "adults aged 18+ in Egypt," "users of [brand] product category." This same discipline must apply to social listening.

Application: Every report must define the social listening population — which platforms, which languages, which geographic filters, which account types (individual users only vs. including media/brand accounts), and which date window.

2. Establish and document your sampling frame

In polling, the sampling frame is the list from which respondents are drawn — voter rolls, phone directories, opt-in panels. In social listening, the sampling frame is defined by the keyword query set: the Boolean queries, hashtags, account handles, and URL patterns that determine which posts are collected.

An undocumented query is an undocumented sampling frame — an invisible source of sampling bias. Queries must be version-controlled and documented.

3. Distinguish descriptive from inferential claims

Polling generates inferential statistics — findings that, by virtue of random sampling, can be generalized to the target population within a calculable margin of error.

Social listening generates descriptive statistics — findings that accurately describe the captured dataset, which is not a random sample of any target population.

These two types of claims must never be conflated in client reporting without explicit correction language.

4. Apply post-collection weighting

Pollsters weight raw survey data by demographic variables (age, gender, region, education) to match known census distributions. This corrects for differential non-response and overrepresentation.

Social listening data can be subjected to analogous weighting — by platform share, language share, account type distribution, or geographic distribution — to improve alignment with the target population. The challenge is that reliable census benchmarks for social media populations are limited and platform-dependent.

Where polling theory does not transfer

DimensionClassical pollingSocial listening
Sampling methodRandom — each member has known, calculable probability of selectionNon-random convenience sample — selection probability unknown and non-uniform
StimulusControlled, closed-ended questions applied uniformlyOpen, unprompted, self-directed expression in naturally occurring contexts
Non-responseTracked, modeled, and adjusted forStructural silence is untracked and unmeasurable — we cannot know why people do not post
Time frameSingle point in time with defined recall windowContinuous data stream with analyst-defined collection windows
Error modelMargin of error calculable from sample size and probability theoryStatistical error floor only — does not capture systematic biases
RepresentativenessDesigned-in through sampling strategyMust be constructed post-hoc through weighting or triangulation

§5 — Error margins & statistical theory

The margin of error problem

Unlike probability-based polling — where the margin of error is a mathematically precise function of sample size and sampling design — social listening has no universally accepted margin of error standard. This is a fundamental limitation acknowledged in the academic literature and should be disclosed in all client work. There are two distinct components to uncertainty in social listening data:

  • Statistical error (calculable) — the variance attributable to sample size, assuming the sample were representative.
  • Systematic bias error (non-calculable with precision) — the additional uncertainty introduced by all seven structural biases above. This cannot be reduced to a single number; it must be disclosed qualitatively.

The statistical error floor

Even though social data is not a probability sample, the classical margin of error formula can be applied to calculate the minimum statistical uncertainty — the floor below which confidence cannot go, even in the best case:

MoEstat = ±Z × √( p̂(1 − p̂) / n )

Where Z = Z-score for desired confidence level (1.645 for 90%, 1.96 for 95%, 2.576 for 99%), p̂ = observed proportion (e.g. 0.63 for 63% negative sentiment), n = number of posts/mentions analyzed. This formula gives the statistical lower bound on uncertainty only. It assumes the sample is representative, which social data is not. The true uncertainty is always greater than this figure.

Worked example

n = 2,000 posts p̂ = 0.63 (63% negative sentiment) Z = 1.96 (95% confidence) MoE = ±1.96 × √(0.63 × 0.37 / 2,000) = ±1.96 × √(0.0001166) = ±1.96 × 0.0108 = ±2.1% 95% CI: 60.9% to 65.1%

Even under optimal conditions, there is a ±2.1% statistical uncertainty in this proportion. With real-world systematic biases, the true uncertainty is considerably wider.

Confidence tiers based on evidence quality

In the absence of a universal MoE standard, the qualitative confidence tier system below covers evidence quality factors:

TierConditionsAppropriate claim strength
HighMulti-platform (3+), n ≥ 10,000, stable over 30+ days, cross-validated with survey/poll"Strong and consistent signal across platforms"
Moderate2–3 platforms, n = 1,000–9,999, 14–30 day window, no cross-validation"Data indicates a probable trend; validation recommended"
LowSingle platform, n < 1,000, event-driven spike, no validation"Early signal observed; interpret with caution"

The NLP accuracy dimension

Confidence in sentiment findings is also bounded by the accuracy of the NLP model used for classification. Most commercial sentiment models achieve:

Content typeClassification accuracy
English general text85–92%
MSA Arabic75–85% (model-dependent)
Dialectal Arabic60–75% (significant variation by dialect)
Code-switched content55–70%

These rates translate directly into additional uncertainty on top of sampling uncertainty. A 63% negative sentiment finding from a model with 80% accuracy may reflect anywhere from 55% to 71% true negative sentiment.

§6 — Scientific sampling methods
