Four methodologically grounded approaches exist for improving the representativeness and validity of social listening data.

Method 1 — Stratified Purposive Sampling Hsieh & Shannon (2005); Sloan & Morgan (2015)
Theory

Rather than collecting all available data from a keyword stream (which allows dominant subpopulations to define the sample), divide the potential data into strata defined by meaningful variables, then sample from each stratum proportionally.

Social media application

Define strata by: platform × language × account type (individual / media / brand) × region. Estimate the proportional contribution of each stratum to the target population. Weight the sample accordingly.

Strengths

Reduces dominance by any single demographic, platform, or subgroup. Allows subgroup-level analysis with adequate per-stratum sample sizes. Conceptually aligned with classical stratified random sampling.

Limitations

Requires estimating population stratum proportions, which are not always available for social media populations. Stratum-level data collection may require platform API access beyond standard listening tool capabilities.

Best for

Multi-platform reports with defined geographic scope; clients requiring subgroup analysis by region or language.

Method 2 — Temporal Random Sampling Standard epidemiological methodology adapted for streams
Theory

Rather than capturing the entirety of a keyword stream over a time period (which gives disproportionate weight to high-volume event periods), randomly select time windows for data collection. This distributes sampling evenly across time, reducing event-spike dominance.

Social media application

Define random sampling windows — e.g. 4 randomly selected 2-hour windows per day across 30 days — rather than continuous collection. Posts captured within these windows constitute the analytical sample.

Strengths

Reduces temporal bias and event-spike dominance. Computationally lighter than full-stream capture. Produces better baseline estimates for longitudinal tracking.

Limitations

May miss important event-driven conversation if the random window does not coincide with the event. Best combined with a separate event-capture collection when monitoring high-salience topics.

Best for

Always-on brand monitoring; longitudinal sentiment tracking; baseline measurement studies.

Method 3 — Triangulation Denzin (1978); Pew, YouGov, Reuters/Ipsos hybrid studies
Theory

No single methodology can provide complete, unbiased insight into a phenomenon. Triangulating across multiple independent data sources allows the strengths of one to compensate for the weaknesses of another. Where sources agree, confidence is elevated. Where they diverge, the divergence itself is informative.

Social media application

Combine social listening data with at least one probability-based source — a survey, a published poll, an official statistic, or a client's proprietary panel data.

  • Social listening provides: narrative texture, real-time speed, thematic depth, emerging topic identification, verbatim language analysis
  • Probability polling provides: population-representative prevalence estimates, demographic breakdowns, inferential validity
Strengths

The most scientifically defensible approach for making inferential claims about general public opinion from social data. Used by the world's leading research institutions. Significantly elevates the confidence tier of any finding where both sources agree.

Limitations

Requires access to a probability-based data source, which typically adds cost and time. Interpretation of divergence between sources requires methodological expertise.

Best for

Strategic client work; reports where population-level inferential claims are required; premium hybrid research products; reports that will inform significant business or policy decisions.

Method 4 — MRP (Multilevel Regression with Post-stratification) Gelman & Little (1997); Wang et al. (2015)
Theory

MRP is a two-stage statistical technique. In stage one, a multilevel regression model is built using demographic variables to predict opinion. In stage two, the model's predictions are weighted (post-stratified) to match the known demographic distribution of the target population from census data. This allows non-representative datasets to produce population-representative estimates.

The Wang et al. (2015) demonstration

Using data from 750,000 Xbox gaming platform users — a dataset radically unrepresentative of the US voting population (young, male, gamer-skewing) — Wang et al. applied MRP to accurately forecast the 2012 US presidential election outcome. The study demonstrated that even severely biased convenience samples can produce reliable population estimates when sufficient demographic information is available for reweighting.

Social media application

Infer user demographics from social media signals (language, location, account characteristics, behavioral patterns). Build a multilevel regression model predicting sentiment or opinion from demographic variables. Post-stratify against census or known population distributions.

Strengths

The theoretically most powerful approach for converting social data into population-representative estimates. Enables inferential claims with documented statistical uncertainty. Increasingly used in academic computational social science.

Limitations

Requires sufficient demographic signal in the dataset for reliable inference. Computationally and statistically intensive. Requires specialized expertise to execute correctly. Quality is bounded by the accuracy of demographic inference from social signals.

Best for

Research-grade deliverables; government and academic partnerships; high-budget campaigns where population-level inferential claims are the central deliverable.

Minimum sample size thresholds

Claim typeMinimum nRecommended nNotes
Sentiment direction (positive vs. negative)4002,000+Below 400, CIs exceed ±5% even ideal
Topic/theme breakdown1,0005,000+Smaller themes underrepresented below 5,000
Demographic subgroup analysis500/group1,500+/groupSubgroup n calculated independently
Longitudinal trend (per period)2001,000+Per measurement period, not total
Platform-specific analysis300/platform1,000+/platformEach platform analyzed as a sub-sample

Beyond ~2,000 posts, statistical MoE improvements are marginal (law of diminishing returns). After this threshold, resources are better invested in: multi-platform coverage, temporal stability analysis, cross-validation with an external source, and human validation of NLP output.

§7 — The four-tier confidence label system

Every quantitative finding in a research-grade report carries one of four standardized confidence labels. The product's velocity badges and momentum classifications follow this same calibration discipline.

🟢 Signal Confirmed

Supported by multiple independent data sources; meets all criteria for high-confidence designation.

Criteria (all must be met)
  • Monitoring across 3 or more platforms
  • Total n ≥ 10,000 posts
  • Trend stable across 30+ consecutive days
  • Cross-validated with at least one probability-based source
Report language

"Strong and consistent signal across platforms, corroborated by [source]. The finding is robust to platform-level variation."

Appropriate for

Executive summary claims; strategic recommendations; findings informing significant business decisions.

🔵 Signal Detected

Consistent across the available data but not cross-validated against an external probability-based source.

Criteria
  • 2 or more platforms
  • n = 1,000 to 9,999 posts
  • Observed across a 14 to 30-day window
  • No external cross-validation available
Report language

"Data indicates a probable trend. Further validation against survey or polling data is recommended before drawing population-level conclusions."

Appropriate for

Most standard client reporting; operational and tactical findings; directional guidance.

🟡 Signal Emerging

The pattern is visible in the data but the evidence base is too sparse, too recent, or too event-driven to be confident of its durability or representativeness.

Criteria
  • Single platform, or sparse data across platforms
  • n < 1,000 posts
  • Observed over less than 14 days
  • High probability of event-driven distortion
Report language

"Early signal observed. Interpret with caution — the data window is short and the sample is limited. Monitor for development over the next 2–4 weeks."

Appropriate for

Early-warning monitoring alerts; trend-watch outputs; flagging topics for deeper investigation.

🔴 Artifact — Verify

The pattern detected is anomalous and may reflect data quality issues rather than genuine sentiment or opinion.

Triggers
  • Unusual spike inconsistent with preceding trend
  • Suspected bot or coordinated inauthentic behavior
  • High NLP model flag rate or known misclassification patterns
  • Single-source domination (one account or one publisher)
  • Data collection or query anomaly
Report language

"Anomalous pattern detected. Manual review recommended before this finding is acted upon. Possible explanations include [bot activity / event spike / NLP error / data collection issue]."

Appropriate for

Flagging in monitoring dashboards; bringing to client attention before final delivery; initiating investigation.

Confidence label decision tree (Appendix C)
Is the finding cross-validated with a probability-based source? ├── YES → Are 3+ platforms represented with n ≥ 10,000 and 30+ days? │ ├── YES → 🟢 SIGNAL CONFIRMED │ └── NO → 🔵 SIGNAL DETECTED (with cross-validation noted) └── NO → Is n ≥ 1,000 across 2+ platforms over 14+ days? ├── YES → 🔵 SIGNAL DETECTED └── NO → Are there anomalies (spikes, bots, NLP flags)? ├── YES → 🔴 ARTIFACT — VERIFY └── NO → 🟡 SIGNAL EMERGING
What this means in this product

The four-tier system above is the SSA house standard. The product does not currently implement it. The fields the product surfaces — topics.velocity_status, topics.momentum, topics.trend_direction, topics.velocity_score — encode directional movement (rising / stable / declining), not evidence quality (n / platform-breadth / temporal stability / cross-validation). They are not a mapping to Signal Confirmed / Detected / Emerging / Artifact–Verify, and they should not be presented to users as if they were. Adopting the four-tier system would require:

  • A per-topic n_posts measure that the UI can read alongside the existing fields (already present as cluster_size in unified_topics; not yet surfaced on Daily Reports).
  • A platform-breadth count (currently degraded by the Telegram-only raw_posts reality — see Posts Explorer).
  • A temporal-stability check across consecutive days (Pipeline C's cross-day continuity surface — topic_stable_id, is_carried_forward, prior_topic_post_count — supplies the underpinnings, but the four-tier classifier does not yet exist).
  • A cross-validation gate against an external probability-based source (no infrastructure exists for this).

The off-limits list in Open Questions includes "the integrity-over-polish data principle"; the corollary is that adopting the four-tier system is a real product decision, not a labeling change. It is in scope for a future Phase 2 conversation, not for incremental UI work.

§8 — Report language standards

The language used in deliverables determines how findings will be interpreted, cited, and potentially misused. The most important rule: descriptive language for what the data shows; inferential language only when cross-validated.

Required language by claim type

Claim typeRequired language elements
Descriptive"Posts mentioning..." / "In the analyzed conversation..." / "Within the study window..."
Directional"The online conversation trends toward..." / "Sentiment direction is..."
Inferential (cross-validated)"Cross-validated with [source], data suggests..." / "Both social and survey data indicate..."
Inferential (not cross-validated)Must include explicit caveat: "If this online conversation is representative of broader public opinion..."

Before / after language examples

❌ Avoid

"63% of users are unhappy with the product."

✅ Use

"63% of analyzed posts mentioning [product] within the study window expressed negative sentiment (n=2,847; 95% CI: ±1.8%). [Signal Detected] — this reflects the active online conversation, not a population-representative survey."

❌ Avoid

"Sentiment improved by 12 points."

✅ Use

"Net sentiment index rose from −34 to −22 across the study period, a +12-point directional improvement. Trend is consistent across all three platforms monitored. [Signal Detected]"

❌ Avoid

"Public opinion on [topic] has shifted strongly negative."

✅ Use

"Online conversation about [topic] has shifted toward more negative framing over the study period. To assess whether this reflects broader public opinion, cross-validation with survey data is recommended. [Signal Detected]"

❌ Avoid

"Consumers will likely reduce spending on this category."

✅ Use

"Online discourse shows rising concern about [category] pricing and value (consistent across 4 weeks, 3 platforms). If this sentiment is representative of broader consumer sentiment, it may signal reduced purchase intent. Cross-validation recommended before strategic action. [Signal Emerging]"

Mandatory methodology appendix

Every research-grade report includes a methodology note addressing all six AAPOR-aligned fields:

Data collected from: [list of platforms] Collection window: [start date] to [end date] [time zone] Query version: [version number — see Query Library] Total posts analyzed: [n] Language coverage: [primary language(s)] [% of non-primary language content] NLP model: [model name and version] Human validation: [% of sample reviewed; analyst name] Known limitations: [brief bullet list from bias inventory] Confidence classification: [four-tier label system — see Methodology Standard v2.1]
What this means in this product

The mandatory methodology appendix above is the SSA standard for research-grade client reports. The product does not emit it. Daily reports and weekly reports ship with the topic envelope only — no methodology note, no query version, no NLP model name & version, no human-validation rate, no n / collection-window framing. Architectural pieces exist that could populate it: LLM_CALL_REGISTRY records model name and version, the daily-report payload carries coverage_metadata and unique_posts_by_platform, the data app emits job_runs and spending_logs. None of this currently reaches the user-facing surface. Adopting the AAPOR-aligned appendix is a Phase 2 product decision; the data needed is largely already in the system.

§9 — AAPOR transparency framework

The American Association for Public Opinion Research (AAPOR) transparency standards represent the closest formally endorsed framework applicable to social listening data disclosure. AAPOR has extended guidance to non-probability and digital data sources.

Six core principles (adapted for social data)

1

Population definition

Who is the population of interest? What is the social media population being monitored? Be explicit about inclusions and exclusions.

2

Sampling and data collection

How was data collected? Which platforms, APIs, and collection methods were used? What were the query parameters?

3

Sample size and design

How many posts were analyzed? Over what time period? What stratification or sampling strategy was applied?

4

Non-response and coverage

What is not captured? What are the known gaps in coverage? For social listening: what % of the true conversation does the query likely capture?

5

Questionnaire / instrument

For social listening: the keyword query set is the instrument. It must be documented and disclosed.

6

Data processing

What NLP model was used? What pre-processing was applied? What was the human validation rate?

Reports that aspire to research-grade status should be fully disclosable under AAPOR standards: if a client or regulator asked for the complete methodology documentation, it could be provided. Methodological opacity is both a scientific weakness and a commercial risk.

§10 — Key academic references

Foundational works
Olteanu, A., Castillo, C., Diaz, F., & Kiciman, E. (2019)

Social Data: Biases, Methodological Pitfalls, and Ethical Boundaries. Frontiers in Big Data, 2, 13. The definitive taxonomy of social media data biases. Identifies five classes of bias and their implications for research validity. Required reading for methodology work.

Hargittai, E. (2020)

Whose Voices Are Represented in Social Science Research? Political Communication, 37(1), 152–158. Demonstrates systematic demographic skew in digital trace data and its implications for representativeness claims. Provides the empirical basis for population bias disclosure.

Wang, W., Rothschild, D., Goel, S., & Gelman, A. (2015)

Forecasting elections with non-representative polls. International Journal of Forecasting, 31(3), 980–991. The Xbox study. Demonstrates that MRP can recover population-representative estimates from severely biased convenience samples.

Sloan, L., & Morgan, J. (2015)

Who Tweets with Their Location? PLOS ONE, 10(11). Empirical data on demographic variation within Twitter's posting population. Supports stratified sampling methodology design.

Hsieh, H. F., & Shannon, S. E. (2005)

Three approaches to qualitative content analysis. Qualitative Health Research, 15(9), 1277–1288. Methodological foundation for purposive and stratified sampling approaches adapted for social media content analysis.

Methods and statistical theory
Gelman, A., & Little, T. C. (1997)

Poststratification into many categories using hierarchical logistic regression. Survey Methodology, 23(2), 127–135. The original MRP paper. Establishes the statistical theory for post-stratification from non-representative samples.

Denzin, N. K. (1978)

The Research Act: A Theoretical Introduction to Sociological Methods. McGraw-Hill. Establishes the theoretical basis for triangulation as a validity-enhancement strategy in social research.

Standards and guidelines
AAPOR (2023)

Transparency Initiative Standards for Online and Non-Probability Surveys. American Association for Public Opinion Research. The closest formally endorsed disclosure standard applicable to social media listening methodology.

Pew Research Center (2018)

Analyzing Social Media Data: A Practical Guide. Pew Research Internet & Technology. Practical guidance from a leading research institution on applying rigorous standards to social media data analysis.

Bias inventory form (Appendix A)

Complete for every engagement during project briefing.

Bias typeApplicable?Direction of distortionMitigation appliedDisclosed?
Population biasY / NY / N
Self-selection biasY / NY / N
Temporal biasY / NY / N
Platform biasY / NY / N
Algorithmic amplificationY / NY / N
Content moderation biasY / NY / N
NLP / language biasY / NY / N
05 / 28 · Methodology Source: SSA Methodology Framework v2.1 (2024) — full doc faithfully reproduced
06 / Methodology

The monitoring footprint.

Who the system is listening to, organized by the three taxonomy layers the data app emits. This section reconstructs the watchlist as observed in production — through the row distributions in raw_posts.dashboard_section, raw_posts.profile_classification, and raw_posts.profile_affiliation. The source documents do not contain a named-account list; that lives in the data-app team's seed/admin layer and is documented separately (see §7). Distributions cited here are from 168,614 raw_posts rows as of the data inventory snapshot, 2026-04-23.

Methodology connection · Bias 1 — Population & AAPOR Principle 5

The list of monitored accounts is the sampling frame for everything downstream. Per methodology §9, AAPOR's instrument-disclosure principle applies: "For social listening, the keyword query set is the instrument. It must be documented and disclosed." For an account-anchored monitoring system the account list is part of the instrument too. An undocumented account list is an undocumented sampling frame — visible to the team that maintains it, invisible to the methodology audit.

§1 — The three taxonomy layers

Every monitored post carries three orthogonal "who" labels. Each layer answers a different question, and each has a different source of truth.

Layer 1

dashboard_section

Question: which monitoring lane does this publisher belong to? Granularity: geographic + topical, e.g. PS_Media, IL_Government, INT_Solidarity. Source: assigned upstream (data-app side), likely per-account at ingestion. Field: text. Coverage: 100% populated on raw_posts.

Layer 2

profile_classification

Question: what kind of publisher is this? Granularity: role / publisher-type, e.g. Politician, Journalist, Media outlet, Government Official, NGO/CBO. Source: assigned upstream. Field: text. Known issue: mixed conventions in the same column — comma-separated multi-values vs ampersand-joined single labels (see §3).

Layer 3

profile_affiliation

Question: which political space does this publisher occupy? Granularity: political-faction, e.g. West Bank Government, Hamas, Gaza Government, Jerusalem. Source: assigned upstream. Field: text. Carries the political-faction granularity that profile_classification does not (per Labeling Quality Note).

§2 — Dashboard-section distribution

The observed footprint of the system, broken down by monitoring lane. Counts are raw_posts rows (Telegram-only ingestion); a high-volume lane represents either many monitored accounts in that bucket or fewer high-activity accounts.

LanePostsShareReading
PS_Media96,97357.51%Palestinian media outlets — largest single lane by a wide margin
News33,73020.00%Legacy generic-news bucket — not in the formal category registry
IL_Media9,7275.77%Israeli media outlets
PS_Factions8,8595.25%Palestinian factions / political organizations
INT_Media8,4585.02%International media coverage
PS_Gaza7,1964.27%Gaza-focused accounts (the geographic granularity within PS)
IL_Government1,7591.04%Israeli government accounts
PS_Government9040.54%Palestinian government accounts
Government3480.21%Legacy generic bucket — not in registry
PS_Education1990.12%Palestinian educational institutions
PS_NGOs1230.07%Palestinian NGOs
IL_Officials960.06%Named Israeli officials
INT_Verificaiton940.06%typo should be INT_Verification — fact-checking accounts
INT_Solidarity790.05%International solidarity accounts
IL_Factions250.01%Israeli factions / political movements
PS_Culture240.01%Palestinian cultural accounts
PS_Verification190.01%Palestinian fact-checking
PS_Youth10.00%Single-row lane — likely a seed entry never followed up
Total168,614100%—

What the distribution tells us about scope

  • Palestinian-media-dominant. The PS_* lanes plus the generic News bucket account for ~88% of all monitored content. The system's center of gravity is the Palestinian media space, with Israeli and international coverage as comparatively thin context streams.
  • Government coverage is sparse. Combined *_Government + IL_Officials = 2,759 rows (1.6%). Either few government accounts are monitored or they post relatively rarely vs. media accounts.
  • The "long tail" lanes are thin. Eight of the eighteen lanes have fewer than 200 rows. PS_Youth with a single row suggests a stale seed entry. PS_Culture, PS_NGOs, INT_Solidarity, IL_Factions are all monitored in concept but produce little volume.
  • Two lanes are legacy / not in the registry. News (33,730 rows) and Government (348 rows) are not in CATEGORY_REGISTRY — they appear to be a separate, looser legacy of dashboard sections that pre-date the PS_/IL_/INT_ taxonomy. They have not been migrated; the system silently accepts them.

§3 — Profile-classification vocabulary

The publisher-type layer. This is what shows up in the per-topic "profile types" breakdown on the source-analytics surface (e.g. {"NGO / CBO": 2, "Journalist": 1, "Politician": 1, "Media outlet": 2} for one topic).

Sampled values that appear in production

The Labeling Quality Note documents the following observed values; the full enumeration is not in the source documents (Open Questions Q1).

Person-type
  • Politician
  • Journalist
  • Political Figure
  • Government Official
  • Author
  • Professor
  • Community
Organization-type
  • Media outlet · News outlet
  • NGO / CBO
  • Government & Official Institutions
  • Fact-Checking & Verification (94 rows; the only *verif* value in this field)

Known data-quality issues

Three different conventions in one field

From the Labeling Quality Note, profile_classification mixes:

  • Comma-separated multi-values, no space: Community,News outlet or Government Official,Professor,Author,Political Figure — implies multi-role accounts.
  • Ampersand single label: Government & Official Institutions — implies one categorical role.
  • Plain single label: Politician, Journalist — the conventional case.

The renderer treats whatever is in the column as an opaque string — three conventions in one field means client-side filtering and grouping silently does the wrong thing on at least two of them.

§4 — Profile-affiliation values

The political-faction layer. Per the Labeling Quality Note, this is the only layer that carries faction granularity — profile_classification does not distinguish a West-Bank-Government account from a Gaza-Government account, but profile_affiliation does.

Named values that appear in the source

The Labeling Quality Note names these explicitly as examples of "the political-faction granularity":

ValueNotes
West Bank GovernmentPalestinian Authority & associated bodies
HamasMovement-level affiliation
Gaza GovernmentDistinct from West Bank Government per the taxonomy
JerusalemGeographic-political designation

Other values exist in production — the source documents enumerate these four by name as the canonical examples. A full distribution table would require a fresh data-app query (see §7 open questions).

§5 — How the taxonomy is used downstream

What the system actually does with these three labels.

Filtering the Posts Explorer surface

Posts Explorer's filter row reads from profile_affiliation via ILIKE (case-insensitive substring match), dashboard_section via boundary-aware LIKE, and watched-keyword chips that highlight matched terms in post content. See §12 Posts Explorer.

Source-influence-tier bucketing (Pipeline C)

Pipeline C's _source_influence_tiers(topic_df) at pipeline_c_processing.py:638-660 buckets posts by profile_classification and persists the result to unified_topics.source_influence_tiers (JSONB). This is the only documented downstream consumer of profile_classification in the pipeline.

Source-attribution in per-topic narrative (Pipeline C, §6.3)

The per-domain prompts (8 lenses) reference dashboard_section in their instruction text ("read each dashboard_section in turn") and require named-account quotes via author_display_name. profile_affiliation is not directly consumed by any prompt path — rg "profile_affiliation" pipeline_c_*.py returns no hits. See Pipeline C §1.5.

Per-topic profile-types breakdown

The data app rolls up profile_classification values per topic into topics.source_analytics_data.profileTypes. Sample shape (one production topic):

{ "topSources": [ {"name":"Bezazelsmotrich","posts":1,"classification":"Politician","totalEngagement":235} ], "profileTypes": {"NGO / CBO":2,"Journalist":1,"Politician":1,"Media outlet":2}, "sourceDiversity": 6 }

Rendered on the source-analytics visualization on the topic card. 99.07% populated on topics (12,645 / 12,764).

Per-domain prompt routing (8 anchors)

Pipeline C clusters routes to one of 8 domain anchors (political / financial / social / health / security / religious / educational / unclassified) which are themselves built from the 19-category seed in seed_categories.py. The category taxonomy and the dashboard-section taxonomy are related but not identical surfaces — categories drive prompt selection, dashboard-sections drive filtering. See §17 LLM Prompts.

§6 — Platform coverage

The watchlist exists across platforms in concept; in practice the raw_posts ingestion is single-platform.

In raw_posts

Telegram only

168,614 of 168,614 rows on raw_posts are Telegram. The Posts Explorer platform-filter dropdown shows one option in production. Cross-platform monitoring is not represented at the raw-post level.

In viral_posts

Facebook + Twitter + Telegram

topics.viral_posts JSONB carries pre-baked snippets from Facebook and Twitter alongside Telegram. The data-app emits these directly from a pipeline the client does not see. topics.dominant_platform ranges over the full multi-platform set (with the casing inconsistency documented in Data Inventory).

Methodology connection · Bias 4 — Platform

This split — Telegram for raw-post drill-through, multi-platform for aggregated viral posts — is the source of the platform-bias issue called out in methodology §3 Bias 4 and Posts Explorer. The watchlist's intended cross-platform scope is not currently observable at the raw-post layer.

§7 — What's NOT in the source documents

Honest framing: the section above reconstructs the watchlist's observable shape through the data the system has emitted. The following are not in any of the nine source documents reviewed:

MissingWhere it would liveStatus
Named account list (handle-by-handle inventory of what is monitored)Data-app seed file, admin UI, or a monitoring_profiles row inventoryUndocumented
Per-account assignment of dashboard_section / profile_classification / profile_affiliationLikely a seed YAML / admin form, data-app sideUndocumented
Add / remove workflow for the watchlistData-app admin layerUndocumented
Governance — who decides which accounts are added?Process documentation that does not exist on the client sideUndocumented
Account-level coverage health (no posts in N days, recently silenced channels)Would be a derived monitoring surface; does not currently existNot built
The monitoring_profiles table schema and contentsThe 36-table inventory in Data Inventory §1.3 lists it but does not deeply audit it (out of scope for the data-app-fed surface)Out of audit scope
What the monitoring_profiles table likely holds Inferred — not directly documented

The table appears in the 36-table inventory but is excluded from the deep field-by-field reality check in Data Inventory §2 with this scope note:

"The deep field-by-field reality check below covers the data-app-fed tables. Internal-only tables (auth, admin, AI assistant) are not deeply sampled because they're not the subject of this inventory and don't suffer from upstream NLP-pipeline contract drift."

Inference (not verified): monitoring_profiles is likely either (a) the client-side per-user monitoring configuration (custom watchlists, scheduled queries, "monitor my interests" features) — which would mean it does not hold the canonical site-wide watchlist — or (b) a mirror of the data-app's monitored-account inventory that the client reads for some surface. The endpoint group catalog in Data Inventory §1.2 lists /api/monitoring-profiles under the unprotected critical-security findings (S6) but does not state what the table holds. Phase-2 audit candidate.

§8 — Methodology implications

The watchlist as sampling frame

From methodology §4: "In polling, the sampling frame is the list from which respondents are drawn. In social listening, the sampling frame is defined by the keyword query set and account list." The de-facto watchlist documented above is the system's sampling frame. Two consequences:

  • Any descriptive claim about "what is being said in Palestinian media" is precise within the monitored set. Generalizing to all Palestinian media discourse requires the population assumption that the monitored set is representative of the whole — which is not currently verified.
  • Changes to the watchlist (additions, removals, account suspensions) shift the sampling frame. Without a documented account list, frame-shifts are not auditable after the fact. A spike or drop in coverage on some topic cannot be cleanly attributed to "the world changed" vs. "the monitored accounts changed."

What honest reporting would require

Per methodology §9 AAPOR Principle 5 (questionnaire / instrument):

  • The monitored-account list should be versioned — every add/remove timestamped.
  • Reports should carry the account-list version they were generated against (akin to query_version in the AAPOR-aligned appendix).
  • Longitudinal claims should be checked against frame-stability between the periods compared.

None of this is currently implemented at the client side. The data-app side may have the versioning primitives; the client does not surface them.

§9 — Open questions

  1. What's the canonical authority for the monitored-account list? An admin UI, a seed file in the data-app repo, both? Where does an analyst go to see "what are we monitoring right now"?
  2. How is the assignment of dashboard_section / profile_classification / profile_affiliation performed? Manual per-account at onboarding, NLP-inferred per-post, hybrid? The casing inconsistencies and registry-vs-legacy mix suggest at least two regimes have run over time.
  3. How many accounts are actually monitored? Posts per account is observable; account count is not derivable from the inventory without a fresh query.
  4. Is profile_affiliation ever used to gate or weight downstream computation? The audit found zero direct references in Pipeline C. Is this a feature gap or by design?
  5. What is the contents and schema of monitoring_profiles? The Phase-2 audit candidate above.
  6. Are there silenced / no-recent-posts accounts in the watchlist? PS_Youth with a single row is the visible flag; there are likely others. No per-account "last seen" surface exists.
  7. What's the cross-platform plan? Per Posts Explorer and §6 above, raw-post ingestion is Telegram-only. Is there a documented intent to ingest Facebook + Twitter raw posts, or is the multi-platform appearance via viral_posts the intended final state?
06 / 28 · Watchlist & Scope Reconstructed from raw_posts distributions in Data Inventory + Labeling Quality Note. Named-account inventory not in source docs.
07 / The Two Apps

Data app.

A separate FastAPI service, owned by another team. Does the only thing the client cannot do for itself: turns raw upstream content into a per-day, per-category envelope of named topics with NLP signals attached. The client sees its outputs, never its internals.

What the data app does that wouldn't otherwise happen

  • Ingests raw upstream content. Telegram for sure; other platforms apparently — since topics.viral_posts carries Facebook and Twitter posts that never reach raw_posts (data-inventory finding #7).
  • Runs the NLP pipelines: sentiment labelling, keyword extraction, geo-location extraction, entity resolution into actors_structured, topic clustering, dashboard-section classification, copy-vs-original detection on raw posts, and per-topic temporal/source/platform aggregation.
  • Assembles the daily-report payload — a per-day, per-category envelope containing the day's topics with all the aggregated signals.

Without the data app, the client has nothing to render. There is no fallback ingestion path; the data app is the upstream.

Two pipelines on the same plumbing

Legacy / Pipeline B

Per-category pipeline

The historical path. Per-category fixed-window CSVs feed in; topics are clustered within a single category at a time. Has the well-known per-category prompts (PS_Government, IL_Media, INT_Solidarity, ~23 in total — see section 17). Writes to topics; topics.viewpoints is left empty.

Republishing / Pipeline C

Unified pipeline

Same-day, all-source unified clustering. Single HDBSCAN call on the whole-day L2-normalised embedding matrix. Writes to unified_topics with separate viewpoints_ar / viewpoints_en JSONB columns. New per-topic momentum ∈ {emerging, rising, stable, declining} and delta_post_count derived from a strict D-1 lookup against unified_topic_centroids. See Pipeline C audit.

Pipeline C — key constants

ConstantValueSource
min_cluster_size8pipeline_c_processing.py:90-94
min_samples3pipeline_c_processing.py:90-94
cluster_selection_methodeompipeline_c_processing.py:734
distance metriceuclidean ≡ cosine on L2-normalised inputpipeline_c_processing.py:733
CONTINUITY_DISTANCE_THRESHOLD0.30cross-day continuity (pgvector cosine), L75-76
ANCHOR_SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD0.55domain routing gate, L66-67
LLM_FALLBACK_DELTA0.05margin before LLM fallback, L135-139
LLM_FALLBACK_ABS_FLOOR0.45absolute floor for fallback
embedding modeltext-embedding-3-small v1via env EMBEDDING_MODEL_ID

Inputs at the door

Pipeline C inherits the legacy contract: content, external_url, platform. Five “source-distinguishing” columns — author_display_name, author_username, dashboard_section, profile_affiliation, profile_classification — are not in required_columns at processing.py:1508 and are read opportunistically downstream. A posts.csv missing those columns will not be rejected at the door — it will silently degrade source-attribution depth.

Outputs (what reaches the user, via the client)

Effectively everything analytical: topic titles, summaries, sentiment labels, keyword frequencies, viral-post snippets, dominant-platform calls, trend-direction tags, source breakdowns, temporal data, per-topic engagement totals, weekly-report narrative sections, ranked stories, and category breakdowns. Section 07 catalogs where each is presented.

Known limitations affecting product decisions

The Data Inventory is the canonical reference. The most consequential ones:

  • Actor enrichment is 81.57% unknown and most normalizedNameEn values are empty.
  • Sentiment label and trend-direction values come back in mixed casing and mixed languages, breaking naive equality filters.
  • raw_posts.nlp_actors is a JSON-encoded string, not the array the client expected — actor chips render empty.
  • A handful of declared fields (viewpoints, sentiment_confidence, the *_analysis text fields) are dead or unread.
07 / 28 · Data App Source: System Reference §3 · Pipeline C Audit §1
08 / The Two Apps

Client app.

An Express backend so thin most “business logic” lives upstream or in the React client. A SPA where overlap scoring, filtering, ranking, and chart shaping are computed from the raw fields the backend serves.

Backend

Runtime
Node.js · Express · TypeScript
ORM
Drizzle (typed against shared schema in shared/schema.ts)
Scheduled work
node-cron for daily report sync and other recurring jobs
Auth
Session-based, 2FA available, API-key for data-app webhooks
AI integration
xAI Grok & OpenAI, only on user-initiated sessions (assistant, X-monitor, workbench finishing)
Email
Resend (transactional only — verification, weekly subscriptions, OTP)
Database pools
Two — separating user-facing reads from heavy batch sync work (server/db.ts)

Frontend

Framework
React 18
Bundler
Vite
Router
Wouter (lightweight, hooks-based)
Server state
TanStack Query
UI primitives
shadcn/ui + Tailwind
Internationalization
Bilingual, RTL-first; layout-level, not patched per-surface
Theming
Dark/light, mobile-responsive

Seven user-facing surfaces

Pages live in client/src/pages/. Each answers a distinct question and reads from a specific slice of the database.

SurfaceFileQuestion it answers
Daily Reportsclient-dashboard.tsxWhat's happening today?
Weekly Reportsweekly-report.tsxWhat does the past week look like?
Analytics Boardanalytics-board.tsxWhat are the cross-topic patterns?
Posts Explorerraw-posts.tsxShow me the actual posts.
AI Assistantai-assistant.tsxHelp me reason about this.
X Monitorx-monitor.tsxWhat's being said about this keyword now?
Report Workbenchreport-workbench.tsxHelp me assemble what I've collected.

Database — 36 tables

Data-app-fed surface tables (bolded), plus client-owned auth, session, AI, evidence, draft, subscription, and admin tables.

_migrations · admin_audit_log · ai_assistant_messages · ai_assistant_sessions · conversations · custom_reports · daily_reports · email_logs · email_verification_codes · evidence_blocks · feedback_submissions · invitations · job_runs · login_history · messages · monitoring_profiles · prompt_logs · prompt_presets · raw_posts · report_drafts · report_templates · scheduled_queries · social_media_posts · spending_logs · sync_state · system_settings · topics · user_sessions · users · waitlist_surveys · watched_keywords · weekly_reports · weekly_settings · weekly_subscriptions · x_monitor_messages · x_monitor_sessions

Endpoint groups

GroupReads fromPages consuming
/api/reportsdaily_reports + topicsclient-dashboard, report-workbench, topic-card
/api/topics-by-date/:datetopics + daily_reportsclient-dashboard
/api/weekly-reports*weekly_reportsweekly-report
/api/x-monitor/*x_monitor_sessions + raw_postsx-monitor
/api/ai-assistant/*ai_assistant_* + topics + raw_postsai-assistant, ai-report-finisher
/api/raw-posts*raw_postsraw-posts, analytics-board
/api/evidence-blocks*evidence_blocks + report_draftsreport-workbench, evidence basket
/api/studio/*admin tablesstudio-admin, admin-dashboard, user-management
08 / 28 · Client App Source: System Reference §4 · Data Inventory §1.2
09 / Operator Surfaces

Admin Portals — Where to do what.

The data app ships two distinct admin user interfaces: Streamlit on port 5000 and the FastAPI HTML operator dashboard on port 8000. Each owns a different set of operational tasks. Source: admin-portals.md. Includes the four task additions for Pipeline C operator surfaces (Tasks #452, #459, #463, #502).

This project ships two distinct admin user interfaces as part of the dual-server architecture documented in replit.md's System Architecture section. New operators routinely miss the second one and conclude that operational tooling — Phase 5 parallel-run measurements, the publish readiness checklist, the pipeline-control kill-switches, the recovery sweep tile, prompt content-hash inventory, and so on — is missing. It is not. It lives on the FastAPI HTML dashboard.

This document is the canonical "where do I go to do X?" reference. It deliberately contains no passwords, JWT secrets, or hardcoded admin email addresses beyond the documented @socialstudio.me domain restriction.

The two portals at a glance

Portal Default port Process Primary purpose Who uses it
Streamlit admin 5000 streamlit run app.py Manual CSV upload, per-topic admin review, archive browsing, processing log inspection Editors / analysts during the daily review pass
FastAPI HTML operator dashboard 8000 (root URL /) uvicorn api:app Automation, pipeline control, ingestion / S3 / email automation, readiness checks, sync, AI inventory, sources / clustering health, Pipeline C report Operators running the system day-to-day

Both surfaces sit behind the same JWT-based authentication, restricted to @socialstudio.me email addresses (see "Authentication" below). Auth state is not shared across the two portals' sessions; an operator moving from one to the other may need to re-authenticate, which is expected.

Streamlit admin (http://<host>:5000)

Code: app.py. Started by run_servers.py as the first child process.

The page is organised as four tabs:

Tab Purpose
📈 تقرير جديد Upload a labelled CSV, sample / weight posts, and generate the bilingual analyses for a new daily report (the legacy segmented Pipeline B path).
📁 التقارير المؤرشفة Browse archived reports from the database, open a topic-by-topic admin review surface, edit fields in place, toggle topic visibility, and approve / reject.
🧭 الموضوعات الموحدة / Unified Topics Read-only consumer view of the most recent unified Pipeline C report (whole-day clustering output).
📋 سجل المعالجة Inspect the per-job structured logs emitted by JobLogger (per-date, per-job-id, per-report-id).

A persistent left-hand sidebar block ("🛠️ لوحة العمليات / Operations Dashboard") links over to the FastAPI HTML operator dashboard. The link opens in a new browser tab so the operator does not lose their Streamlit session.

FastAPI HTML operator dashboard (http://<host>:8000/)

Code: api.py (the HTML response served on the root path). Started by run_servers.py as the second child process.

The dashboard is organised as a left-hand sidebar with the following sections (the exact set visible to a given user depends on their role — viewer / admin / superadmin):

Overview - 🏠 لوحة التحكم — main dashboard, including the Phase 5 parallel-run strip, the publish-readiness pre-publish gate, the recovery-sweep tile, draft-report queue, and the pipeline kill-switches panel. - 📁 أرشيف التقارير — full report archive with sync / delivery state. - 📅 تقويم التقارير — calendar view of report coverage.

Activity - 📋 سجل المعالجة — processing log mirror (same data as Streamlit). - 📤 رفع الملفات — admin-only file upload surface. - 📊 مراقب النظام — system monitor (disk, queues, workers).

Automation - 📧 أتمتة البريد — email-ingestion automation. - ☁️ أتمتة S3 — S3 polling, dated-folder backfill, recovery sweep, per-folder file-details drawer, abandoned-folder visibility.

Settings - ⚙️ إدارة الفئات — category management (CRUD on the database-driven category prompts). - 📡 Client Sync — push / pull approved reports between dev and prod. - 🤖 نماذج الذكاء / AI Models — LLM_CALL_REGISTRY view and swap-safety inventory, including the prompt content-hash inventory. - 🔎 المصادر / Sources — Sources Transparency Monitor. - 🧬 صحة التجميع / Clustering — clustering health and per-post → cluster drill-through. - 🧭 تقرير خط الأنابيب الموحد / Pipeline C Report — Pipeline C admin surface (per-domain prompts, viewpoints, observability tiles). - 🔄 مزامنة / Sync — production sync modal.

A small reciprocal back-link in the dashboard's top header (above the sidebar / main content area) points at the Streamlit admin. It opens in a new tab.

"Where do I find X?" quick lookup

Need Portal Tab / section
Upload a labelled CSV for the segmented pipeline Streamlit 📈 تقرير جديد
Approve or reject a draft segmented report (in-place edits) Streamlit 📁 التقارير المؤرشفة
Read the latest unified Pipeline C report Streamlit 🧭 الموضوعات الموحدة / Unified Topics
See per-job structured processing logs Either (mirrored) 📋 سجل المعالجة
See Phase 5 parallel-run measurements (Pipeline B vs Pipeline C) FastAPI dashboard 🏠 لوحة التحكم → Phase 5 strip
Open the pre-publish readiness checklist (10-item greenlight gate) FastAPI dashboard 🏠 لوحة التحكم → readiness panel
Stop / start the segmented pipeline kill-switch FastAPI dashboard 🏠 لوحة التحكم → pipeline control
Stop / start the Pipeline C kill-switch FastAPI dashboard 🏠 لوحة التحكم → pipeline control
Stop / start the raw_posts ingestion kill-switch FastAPI dashboard 🏠 لوحة التحكم → pipeline control
Trigger or inspect a missed-day recovery sweep FastAPI dashboard ☁️ أتمتة S3 → recovery sweep tile
Check today's ingestion-snapshot status (ready / in-progress / stuck-folder) FastAPI dashboard 🧭 Pipeline C Report → "📸 Ingestion snapshot — today" tile
Review / edit category prompts FastAPI dashboard ⚙️ إدارة الفئات
Review prompt content hashes / LLM call registry FastAPI dashboard 🤖 نماذج الذكاء / AI Models
Inspect per-account engagement (Sources Transparency) FastAPI dashboard 🔎 المصادر / Sources
Inspect cluster health / per-post → cluster drill-through FastAPI dashboard 🧬 صحة التجميع / Clustering
Push approved reports from dev to prod FastAPI dashboard 🔄 مزامنة / Sync
Manage admin users / role mutation (superadmin only) FastAPI dashboard (superadmin endpoints under /api/admin/...)

If a needed widget is on the FastAPI dashboard, do not duplicate it into Streamlit. The FastAPI dashboard is the operator console by design and duplicating its tiles would mean maintaining two copies of every operational surface forever. If a specific operator signal would genuinely help mid-review (for example, a single Publish-Readiness pill inside the Pipeline C Report tab), open a separate, narrowly scoped task for that signal.

Authentication

Both portals use the same auth implemented in auth.py:

- Login — POST /api/auth/login with email + password. The email must end in @socialstudio.me (auth.ALLOWED_DOMAIN); requests from other domains are rejected with 403. - Token — on success the server issues a JWT (HS256, 24-hour expiry) signed with the secret loaded from the JWT_SECRET environment variable. The actual secret value is never committed to the repo and never logged. If JWT_SECRET is unset the process fails to start with a clear error. - Storage — the JWT is set as a httponly samesite=lax cookie named admin_token and returned in the JSON response so SPA / API clients can also use a Bearer header. - Roles — viewer < admin < superadmin (see ROLE_RANK in api.py). Most endpoints require admin; user-management endpoints require superadmin. - Rate limiting — 5 failed login attempts per IP per 5 minutes (auth.RATE_LIMIT_MAX_ATTEMPTS / RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_SECONDS). Successful logins reset the counter. - Lockout recovery — wait out the rate-limit window or restart the server to clear the in-memory rate-limit store. If an operator has forgotten their password, a superadmin can reset it through the admin-users management endpoints; see the audit trail in admin_audit_log. The exact recovery procedure intentionally lives outside this repo.

Local development

run_servers.py starts both processes from one entry point:

  • Streamlit on port 5000 — streamlit run app.py --server.port 5000
  • FastAPI on port 8000 — uvicorn api:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
  • API docs (Swagger) on http://0.0.0.0:8000/docs

The cross-links between the two portals are configurable via environment variables so the same code works in both local and deployed topologies:

- FASTAPI_ADMIN_URL — the URL that the Streamlit sidebar link points at. Defaults to http://0.0.0.0:8000 when unset (matches local run_servers.py). - STREAMLIT_ADMIN_URL — the URL that the FastAPI dashboard header back-link points at. Defaults to http://0.0.0.0:5000 when unset.

In a Replit .replit.dev deployment the two ports map to two distinct public hostnames; set both env vars to the corresponding public URLs so the cross-links actually resolve outside the container. The Streamlit sidebar shows a small "default URL" note when FASTAPI_ADMIN_URL is unset, as a hint that the operator is looking at a local-only link.

Out of scope for this doc

- The external client app referenced in webhook configuration (CLIENT_APP_URL, WEBHOOK_API_KEY) is a separate codebase outside this repo and is not covered here. Its URL and API key are deployment configuration, not documentation. - This doc does not describe the auth implementation in detail beyond what an operator needs to know to log in and recover from a lockout. See auth.py for the implementation.

Pipeline C "Unified Report" tab — carried-cluster actions (Task #452)

The FastAPI admin /admin → Unified Report tab now makes the carried (non-reportable) clusters from each Pipeline C run actionable rather than purely decorative:

- Inline cheap signals on every carried card — domain, cluster size, cohesion, top keywords, dominant platforms, and a single canonical carry_reason chip (below_soft_domain_quota / below_cohesion_threshold / below_top_n_cap). The reason is derived by pipeline_c_reportable.derive_carry_reason with this priority: soft per-domain quota displacement > cohesion floor > default top-N cap. - Domain filter chips — apply to BOTH the reportable and carried sections with live per-section counts (Nr/Nc). Click to hide a domain from the view; click again to bring it back. Client-side only; nothing is persisted. - Collapsible carried cards — default to collapsed. Opening a card lazy-loads up to 25 posts (composite engagement DESC) via GET /api/admin/pipeline-c/topics/{topic_id}/posts. The drill- through is left-joined against raw_posts so 90-day-pruned posts still surface with NULL metadata and engagement=0. - "Promote & analyze" button per carried card — operator-level override that calls POST /api/admin/pipeline-c/topics/{topic_id}/promote. The endpoint flips is_reportable=TRUE, appends the row to the reportable rank tail (MAX(rank)+1, never re-ranks existing rows), runs single-topic LLM enrichment via pipeline_c_dispatch.run_single_topic_analysis, writes an admin_audit_log row tagged pipeline_c_promote_topic with an over_cap_override boolean (true when the new reportable count exceeds the run's configured cap), and re-fires the webhook only if the report has already been successfully delivered. The webhook re-send is idempotent at the client end via the deterministic report id.

Task #459 correctness contract. The endpoint is hardened against three durable-correctness gaps surfaced by the May 2026 integrity audit:

1. Concurrency — A session-level Postgres advisory lock keyed on (report_id, cluster_label) (acquired via db.try_acquire_promote_lock, released in finally via db.release_promote_lock) wraps the full critical section (LLM → flip → webhook → audit). A second concurrent click for the same cluster gets 409 promotion already in flight for topic_id={id} immediately — no LLM bill, no audit row, no duplicate webhook. Session-level (not _xact_) so the lock can outlive the 10-30 s LLM call without pinning a transaction. 2. Bilingual-required gate — Both Arabic AND English must succeed in per_lang_success before is_reportable flips. Single-language failure returns 502 with the failed language surfaced in the detail; is_reportable stays FALSE so the operator can retry from the carried section once the upstream model issue clears. The orchestrator's any-language-wins fallback for the batch reportable path is intentionally unchanged — manual promote is the only path under this stricter policy because the operator is explicitly intervening. 3. Audit row records the webhook outcome — The pipeline_c_promote_topic audit row is written after the webhook re-send returns, and its details payload now carries webhook_redelivered (bool), webhook_error (str|null), and last_successful_delivery_at (ISO string|null). A failed wire delivery is recoverable from the audit log alone.

Where to go to do X: - "I want to see what a carried cluster is about." → expand the card. - "I want to know why it didn't make the report." → carry_reason chip. - "I want to bring it into the report anyway." → Promote & analyze.

Webhook payload — compact vs verbose quota_swap_log (Task #463)

The unified-report webhook (webhook.build_unified_report_dict) ships the soft per-domain quota swap log in a compact shape, intentionally narrower than what the backend persists:

"quota_swap_log": [ {"promoted_domain": "gendered_dimension", "displaced_domain": "political"}, {"promoted_domain": "youth", "displaced_domain": "media"} ]

The per-swap score fields (promoted_score, displaced_score, promoted_label, displaced_label, the active floor) are deliberately stripped from the wire payload. This is a byte-budget choice — unified-reports payloads already push the client app's ingestion budget on high-coverage days, and the per-swap scores are internal to the editorial selector (operators reading the report do not act on a raw composite score). The compact list is enough to tell the client which domains were rebalanced and in what order. The client team's contract reference for this is docs/client-pipeline-c-spec.md (the row for quota_swap_log); the swap rationale (ADR 0016) lives in docs/adr/0016-pipeline-c-soft-domain-quota.md.

The verbose form — including every score and the floor at the moment of the swap — is persisted server-side in the unified_reports.quota_swap_log JSONB column, written by pipeline_c_quota.apply_domain_quota and never mutated thereafter. An operator who needs the full audit (for a client-team question "why was X swapped in over Y on 2026-05-14?") can fetch it directly:

SELECT quota_swap_log FROM unified_reports WHERE report_id = 'unified-2026-05-14-<suffix>';

quota_swap_count and quota_floor ship on the wire in both shapes (they are scalar and cheap); only the per-entry score detail is backend-only. If the client team ever needs the verbose shape on the wire, that is a deliberate contract change — open a separate task rather than widening the compact builder, because the wire payload budget needs to be re-validated end-to-end first.

Analyzed Clusters tab (Task #502)

The Pipeline C Report tab has historically been the only admin surface for the day's reportable clusters. As cluster-card content grew (bilingual brief / narrative / viewpoints / key voices + every persisted metadata axis), that single tab became hard to scan when an operator just wanted "show me what made it into today's report and let me drill into one." Task #502 lifts that workflow into its own page.

Where do I go?

🔬 Analyzed Clusters — the new dedicated admin tab. Sidebar button sits immediately below "Pipeline C Report". Lands on the most recent day with at least one reportable cluster. Date picker at the top lists every day the system has at least one unified_topics.is_reportable=TRUE row for. List view shows one card per reportable cluster, with the shared metadata panel (reportable axes, top keywords, dominant platforms, hashtags, typed actors, source-influence tiers, factuality check, representative post URLs, IDs). Open → opens an in-place deep-dive showing the full bilingual brief / narrative / viewpoints / key voices for that cluster, the shared metadata panel, AND the top 50 posts in the cluster's corpus (ranked by composite engagement) with author name, dashboard section, profile classification / affiliation, and per-post view / reaction / comment / share counts. 🧭 Pipeline C Report — still the home for promote / un-promote flows and for the carried-cluster review. Reportable section is now a compact one-row-per-cluster strip that deep-links to the Analyzed Clusters tab (preserving the operator's date selection and pre-opening the same cluster). * Carried section is unchanged in semantics (still viewpoints-only, still gated by "Promote & analyze"); it now embeds the same shared metadata panel so an operator can see every persisted field before deciding to promote.

Endpoints

All three endpoints are admin-gated (require_admin) and pure read-only — no LLM call, no DB write, no schema change. They render columns the Pipeline C orchestrator has already persisted into unified_topics / unified_post_cluster_assignments / raw_posts.

Endpoint Returns
GET /api/admin/pipeline-c/analyzed-clusters/dates { available_dates: ["YYYY-MM-DD", …] } — every date with at least one reportable topic.
GET /api/admin/pipeline-c/analyzed-clusters?date=YYYY-MM-DD { date, available_dates, report, topics } — full reportable list for the chosen date (defaults to most-recent). Each topic is augmented with top_keywords / dominant_platforms / cohesion so the metadata panel can render without a follow-up fetch.
GET /api/admin/pipeline-c/topics/{topic_id}/detail { topic, report, posts } — the augmented topic row, the parent run header, and the first 50 posts of the cluster's corpus (composite-engagement ranked) including author_display_name / dashboard_section / profile_classification / profile_affiliation / views_count / reactions_count / comments_count / shares_count.

The HTML fragment that backs the tab body is served by GET /api/admin/dashboard/analyzed-clusters-html and lazy-loaded by loadAnalyzedClustersTab() on first activation, matching the existing unified-report / clustering lazy-load pattern.

Single source of truth for the metadata panel

The shared metadata block (axes pills + keywords + platforms + hashtags + typed actors + source-influence tiers + factuality + repr. URLs + IDs) is rendered by the JS function _renderSharedMetaHtml defined in the Pipeline C Report fragment. The Analyzed Clusters fragment reuses it directly — there is intentionally no second copy. Adding a new persisted field requires changing exactly one place. Tests live in tests/test_task_502_analyzed_clusters_page.py.

09 / 28 · Admin Portals Source: admin-portals.md · 2026-05-21 with Tasks #452, #459, #463, #502 merged
10 / User Surfaces

Daily Reports.

The product's home surface. Date, category lane, list of named topics — sentiment, key actors, viral posts, source/platform/temporal breakdowns, hashtag and word clouds.

File
client/src/pages/client-dashboard.tsx
Reads from
daily_reports · topics (data-app-fed)
Endpoint
/api/reports · /api/topics-by-date/:date
Picks
A date (today by default) and a category lane (or “All Topics”)
Methodology connection · confidence labels

The methodology's four-tier confidence label system (🟢 Signal Confirmed / 🔵 Signal Detected / 🟡 Signal Emerging / 🔴 Artifact–Verify) is the house standard for communicating calibrated uncertainty. The product's existing fields — velocity_status, momentum, trend_direction — are not a mapping to those tiers. They report directional movement, not evidence quality. Reality gap: no per-topic field today encodes platform breadth × n × stability × cross-validation. A topic shown on a Daily Report carries velocity but not confidence-tier context — the reader cannot tell whether to read it as a Signal Detected or an Artifact–Verify.

What renders per topic

ComponentReadsState
Topic card headertitle · brief_summary · sentiment_label · dominant_platform · categorycasing-sensitive on platform; getCategoryDef() handles legacy slugs
Sentiment dashboardsentiment_label · sentiment_scores · sentiment_trend.dailySentimentEnglish-only filter drops ~50% of rows
Key Actors cardactors_structured[].type, .originalName, .count81.57% unknown — “Unknown” group dominates
Actor names (topic card fallback)actors_structured[].normalizedNameEn ‖ .originalNamefalls back to Arabic original when EN empty — masks the empty-normalized-name failure
Viral postsviral_posts[]91.6% populated · cross-platform
Source analyticssource_analytics_data.{topSources,profileTypes,sourceDiversity}~99% populated
Platform analyticsplatform_breakdown · dominant_platformcasing inconsistency 84 rows have capital-F “Facebook”
Activity timelinetemporal_data.dailyDistribution · hourlyDistribution · peakHours~99% populated
Velocity badgetrend_direction · velocity_status · velocity_scorefour casings/decorations for three values
Word cloudkeywords_with_counts98.9%
Hashtags cloudhashtags_datareliable
Emerging topics alerttrend_direction · velocity_*same casing trap as velocity badge

Date navigation

Unified across the recent-reports carousel, the calendar picker, and the archive sidebar (see .local/tasks/sync-date-navigation.md). Any date pick is a single canonical “go to this day” action.

Topic Connections card

Within-day, between two topics in the same report. Shows shared keywords, locations, and sources. The synthesis sentence and shared-entities chip row are gated off pending data-app actor enrichment fixes (ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES = false). Cross-day linkage has been rejected as a v1 redefinition, not an extension.

Same-day scope

Topic-connections is within-report, same-day. Any future “linked across time” proposal is a redefinition of the surface, not an extension. The held visual-polish pass on this card (Task #76) is paused pending whether the card should be rethought given how much is gated off.

10 / 28 · Daily Reports Source: System Reference §4 · Data Inventory §4
11 / User Surfaces

Weekly Reports.

Locale-filtered (PS / IL / INT) aggregation of a week's daily reports. Narrative sections, ranked stories, top entities, category breakdown, sentiment overview, platform breakdown.

File
client/src/pages/weekly-report.tsx
Reads from
weekly_reports
Generator
aggregateWeeklyData() with locale filtering
Endpoint
/api/weekly-reports · /api/weekly-reports/:id
Methodology connection · sample size thresholds

The methodology's minimum sample size table (§6) calls for ≥1,000 posts for topic/theme breakdowns and ≥200 per measurement period for longitudinal trends. The current weekly aggregations operate on whatever a week's daily reports produced — usually well above these floors at corpus scale, but no per-section n is surfaced to the user. Reality gap: a weekly section reporting "rising negative sentiment around X" carries no n, no CI, no platform breadth indicator.

Locales

PS

Palestine

Domestic Palestinian-language and PS_*-categorized content.

IL

Israel

Israeli-language and IL_*-categorized content.

INT

International

International coverage and INT_*-categorized content.

Subscriptions

Per-user with cadence (weekly / biweekly / custom interval), schedule day, optional auto-canvas draft generation, and HTML/JSON export. Templates store reusable configurations. Stored in weekly_subscriptions + weekly_settings.

Render fields

  • narrative_sections — bilingual prose summary blocks
  • ranked_stories — ranked top stories of the week
  • top_entities — most-mentioned actors across the week
  • category_breakdown — share of weekly volume per category
  • sentiment_overview — weekly sentiment distribution
  • platform_breakdown — weekly per-platform totals
Small-N caveat

33 weekly reports total at the last inventory snapshot; not deeply sampled. Flagged for a future sampling pass before any feature gate depends on per-field reliability here.

11 / 28 · Weekly Reports Source: System Reference §4 · Data Inventory §4
12 / User Surfaces

Analytics Intelligence Board.

Cross-topic statistical patterns over the corpus, or a filtered slice of it. Time-series, heatmaps, leaderboards, gauges, geographic widgets — with cross-module filter state shared across modules.

File
client/src/pages/analytics-board.tsx
Reads from
raw_posts primarily (Telegram-only in current DB)
Filter state
Shared across all modules
Methodology connection · descriptive vs inferential

The methodology's descriptive/inferential distinction (§8) requires that quantitative findings carry the language of what they actually measure. Analytics Board scorecards aggregate over the captured corpus — they describe the dataset, not a population. Reality gap: KPI numbers render bare ("63 topics this week", "+12% engagement vs last week") without the descriptive framing the methodology prescribes ("63 topics in the captured corpus", "Within the study window…"). Honest framing would not change the numbers; it would change how a reader could quote them.

Modules

Time-series activity monitor

Volume over time, with per-platform overlay and selectable bucketing.

Topics heatmap

Cross-topic intensity visualisation across categories or time bins.

Publisher leaderboard

Top publishers by volume, weighted by profile classification.

Sentiment + emotion gauge

Aggregate sentiment distribution with emotion overlay.

Amplification tracker

Originals-to-copies ratio over time, derived from copy_or_original.

Post exploration drawer

Drill-through into individual raw posts. Per-post actor chips silently empty (see callout).

Geographic-locations widget

Locations from nlp_analysis.geo_locations on raw posts; coordinates only on a small fraction of records.

KPI scorecards

Total posts · Unique sources · % negative · Originals/copies ratio. All respect the cross-module filter state.

Bug · Data Inventory §6

Per-post actor chips silently empty. raw_posts.nlp_actors is always a JSON-encoded string in this DB (166,233 of 166,233 populated rows), but parseActorItem in post-exploration-panel.tsx:243 expects an array of strings or objects. The array branch is dead code. Contract clarification needed from data-app team — see section 23.

12 / 28 · Analytics Board Source: System Reference §4 · Data Inventory §3.5, §6
13 / User Surfaces

Posts Explorer.

Show me the actual posts behind the analytics. Filter by platform, topic, affiliation, sentiment, emotion. Watched-keyword chips actively filter and are highlighted in post content.

File
client/src/pages/raw-posts.tsx
Reads from
raw_posts (Telegram-only in current DB)
Filters
platform · topic (boundary-aware LIKE) · affiliation (ILIKE) · sentiment · emotion · watched keywords
Methodology connection · Bias 4 — Platform

This is the textbook case of Platform Bias. The methodology prescribes "multi-platform monitoring with documented platform weights; platform-stratified reporting; avoid single-platform findings being presented as general public opinion." The product's raw_posts ingestion is Telegram-only — 168,614 of 168,614 rows. Facebook and Twitter content reaches the user only through pre-baked viral_posts and dominant_platform, with no per-platform raw-post drill-through. Reality gap: the surface implies platform diversity it cannot back up at the post level. The platform filter UI shows only one option in production.

The Telegram reality

The product describes itself as multi-platform but the live ingestion is largely Telegram. raw_posts is 100% Telegram; non-Telegram content reaches the user only through pre-baked viral_posts and dominant_platform fields on topics. Posts-Explorer and Analytics-Board UIs that imply platform diversity are accurate-but-thin in practice.

Open product call

Whether to surface this honestly (e.g. a “Telegram only” affordance on Posts Explorer) is an open product call. The platform filter UI shows only one option in current production.

Watched keywords

User-tracked terms that drive filtering on Posts Explorer and influence report-generation context. Stored in watched_keywords. Highlighted in post body when matched.

Per-post NLP fields read

FieldTypeReliability
platformtext NOT NULL100% “telegram”
nlp_actorsjsonb (always a string in DB)contract drift
copy_or_originaltextpopulated
original_post_idtextpopulated where applicable
dashboard_sectiontextmixed taxonomy + 1 typo (INT_Verificaiton, 94 rows)
profile_classificationtextpopulated
nlp_sentiment_labeltext1,345 rows literal “{}”
13 / 28 · Posts Explorer Source: System Reference §4 · Data Inventory §2 (raw_posts)
14 / User Surfaces

AI Assistant.

Help me reason about what I'm looking at. Wraps xAI Grok and OpenAI. Operates over topics + raw posts as context. Streaming responses with rich Markdown rendering. Responses can be added to the evidence basket.

File
client/src/pages/ai-assistant.tsx
Backing tables
ai_assistant_sessions · ai_assistant_messages
Reads context from
topics · raw_posts
Endpoint
/api/ai-assistant/*
Providers
xAI Grok · OpenAI
Methodology connection · the no-invention rule

The product's hard rejection of LLM narrative generation in v1 (one of the off-limits redesigns in Open Questions) is the operational expression of the methodology's no inferential claim without cross-validation rule. The AI Assistant grounds answers in topics and raw_posts retrieved from the database, not in model priors. Honest framing: the assistant performs lookup and summarization within a captured corpus, never claims-about-the-population. When it cites a topic or post, that is the descriptive surface; when it generalizes, the generalization is a Signal Emerging at best until cross-validated externally.

What it does

  • Operates over user-selected topics or post selections as conversational context.
  • Streaming token-by-token responses with rich Markdown rendering.
  • Per-session message history persisted in ai_assistant_messages.
  • Any AI response can be added to the evidence basket as a typed block (type: ai_response).
Honest labeling

The assistant does not synthesise new analytical claims about the corpus. It reasons over topics and posts the user has selected; it does not invent entities or claims absent from source material. Same hard rule that gates the workbench AI Report Finisher.

Difference from the data-app LLM stack

The data app's LLM calls (topic_analysis, executive_summary — see section 17) run during pipeline execution and produce structured outputs the data app then ships in payloads. The client's AI assistant is a user-facing convenience; it never writes back to topics or any data-app-fed table.

14 / 28 · AI Assistant Source: System Reference §4
15 / User Surfaces

X Monitor.

Session-based monitoring against raw_posts plus any AI summary the user requests. Results can be pinned to the evidence basket.

File
client/src/pages/x-monitor.tsx
Backing tables
x_monitor_sessions · x_monitor_messages · raw_posts
Endpoint
/api/x-monitor/*

Session model

Each monitoring session is anchored to a keyword (or keyword set). Within the session, the user can re-run the search, refine, request an AI summary, and pin specific posts or summaries to the evidence basket.

Constraint inherited from Posts Explorer

Because the monitor reads from raw_posts, it inherits the Telegram-only ingestion constraint. A session anchored on a keyword that's prominent on Facebook or Twitter will return thinner results than the underlying conversation warrants. Non-Telegram content reaches the user only through pre-baked topics.viral_posts.

15 / 28 · X Monitor Source: System Reference §4
16 / User Surfaces

Report Workbench.

Help me assemble what I've collected into a report. The Evidence Basket layer collects typed blocks from any other surface and groups them by draft. The canvas is a 3-zone block-based document editor.

File
client/src/pages/report-workbench.tsx
Backing tables
evidence_blocks · report_drafts · report_templates
Exports
JSON · HTML · DOCX · PDF

Evidence Basket — typed block kinds

  • ai_response — output from AI Assistant
  • x_monitor_result — output from X Monitor
  • post — a raw post pinned from Posts Explorer
  • chart_snapshot — a snapshot of an Analytics Board chart
  • topic_discovery — a topic pinned from Daily Reports
  • narrative_text — free-form text the user has written

Three-zone canvas

Left

Topic Discovery

Browse pinned topics, search the corpus, drag into canvas.

Center

Canvas

Block-based document editor with inline editing and drag-and-drop reordering.

Right

AI Report Finisher

Formatting and finishing assistance. Hard constraint: it does not synthesise new claims.

Honest labeling

The workbench presents what the user has collected. AI assistance here is for finishing/formatting, not for inventing entities or claims not in the source blocks. This is the rule that previously caused us to reject LLM-generated narrative reporting in v1.

Outputs

Drafts can be exported as JSON (machine-readable, archivable), HTML (preview-equivalent, printable), DOCX (sharable Word file), or PDF. Preview and an interactive guide are built in.

16 / 28 · Report Workbench Source: System Reference §4
17 / Data & Quality

Data Inventory — Pipeline B + Pipeline C.

Field-level reality check of every data surface the client and operator touch. Last regenerated 2026-05-21 (Task #512) against the current production schema. Source: data-inventory.md. Companion to §01 System Reference (narrative) and §19 Pipeline C Architecture (operational).

Snapshot date: 2026-05-21 Task: #512 (regenerates the 2026-04-23 snapshot; no on-disk predecessor — see Changelog) Query log: [.local/docs/data-inventory-queries.sql](../.local/docs/data-inventory-queries.sql) Author: Replit Agent (read-only documentation pass)

This is a code-grounded, query-backed survey of the data surfaces the data-app produces and the client-app consumes. Every number below traces to a labeled query (Q#.#) in the SQL log. No code, schema, or data was modified; the only on-disk side effects are this file and the query log.

---

§0 Environment caveat (read first)

* DB queried: heliumdb @ helium — the database the running app reads/writes via DATABASE_URL (Q0.1, Q1.1). Server identifies as PostgreSQL 16.10. From inside the app we cannot distinguish prod-of-record vs prod-shadow vs staging. DATABASE_URL is just an opaque connection string; there is no environment label on the DB itself. Treat every count below as "what this DB currently holds" — the shape and the verdicts transfer to other environments, the absolute counts* may not. pg_stat_user_tables.n_live_tup is stale on this Neon-backed DB (off by 4+ orders of magnitude on raw_posts). Every count in this document comes from COUNT(*), not estimates (Q1.2). * The prior 2026-04-23 inventory file referenced in Task #512's brief is not present in git history — the only on-disk remnant of the 2026-04-23 audit is docs/client-data-quality-response-2026-05.md, which carries the fourteen original Section 6 verdicts as a verdict table. Section 6 below re-derives every one of those fourteen verdicts against the current DB.

---

§1 Sources and shapes

§1.1 Surfaces (where data crosses a network boundary)

Data-app endpoints the client consumes (webhook delivery is the only producer-initiated surface; the rest are pulled on demand):

Endpoint Surface Source code
POST {client_webhook_url} full report payload on approval webhook.py:_build_payload
GET /api/client/reports/daily-summary {date, unique_posts_by_platform, total_unique_posts} api.py (sourced from raw_posts)
GET /api/client/reports/... per-report client mirror api.py

Admin surfaces (operator-facing, not in the public client contract): Streamlit on :5000 (CSV upload, per-topic review) and FastAPI HTML on :8000 (kill-switches, Phase 5 measurements, publish-readiness, recovery sweep). Cross-linked via FASTAPI_ADMIN_URL / STREAMLIT_ADMIN_URL env vars (api.py:967, api.py:20257, app.py:1741).

§1.2 Public tables present

27 tables in public (Q1.1). Bolded = directly read by client-facing endpoints. Italicised = Pipeline-C-specific (introduced after the 2026-04-23 baseline).

  • reports — Pipeline B per-category report JSONB store
  • raw_posts — verbatim mirror of upstream posts.csv rows
  • unified_reports — Pipeline C run header (Phase 1+)
  • unified_topics — Pipeline C per-cluster row (incl. Stage 7 narrative + Stage 9 viewpoints)
  • unified_topic_centroids — pgvector HNSW table for day-over-day continuity (Phase 1)
  • unified_post_cluster_assignments — per-post → cluster_label (ADR 0013)
  • unified_ingestion_snapshots — Phase 0/1 corpus snapshot (ADR 0015)
  • unified_llm_calls — per-call LLM audit trail (Task #319)
  • pipeline_c_prompt_versions — content-hash inventory of every live prompt
  • pipeline_control_state — three independent kill-switches (ADR 0011)
  • post_embeddings — pgvector store (shared by Pipelines B and C since Phase 0)
  • topic_diagnostics — per-topic cohesion / single-author / coherence (Pipeline B path; predates Pipeline C)
  • report_clustering_summary — Pipeline B per-report clustering summary
  • webhook_deliveries — per-attempt webhook history
  • embedding_worker_telemetry — async embedding-worker cycle records
  • phase5_measurements, phase5_window, phase5_window_archive — Pipeline B/C parallel-run rubric
  • admin_audit_log, admin_users — admin auth + audit
  • category_prompts, prompt_presets — DB-backed prompt content
  • s3_scan_history, s3_automation_state, raw_posts_config — S3 ingestion plumbing
  • email_automation_state, email_automation_processed — email-thread ingestion plumbing
§1.3 Tables the 2026-04-23 inventory assumed exist but do not

Q1.3 confirms zero rows in information_schema.tables for: topics, daily_reports, weekly_reports, social_media_posts, custom_reports. Every per-topic field in the prior inventory's topics discussion actually lives inside reports.report_data -> 'topic_analyses' (JSONB; Q2.2, Q2.3). This resolves the prior Task-#71-shaped question — see §6.

§1.4 Row counts (Q1.2)
Table Rows
raw_posts 3,186,762
post_embeddings 503,595
unified_post_cluster_assignments 91,104
topic_diagnostics 11,145
embedding_worker_telemetry 1,880

reports 1,146

unified_llm_calls 802

unified_topic_centroids 350

report_clustering_summary 82
s3_scan_history 72
