unique_posts_by_platform object {platform: int, …} report.unique_posts_by_platform (or derived from topics) {"twitter": 12480, "facebook": 6231, "tiktok": 4112} Stat tiles, one per platform. Keys are lowercase platform strings; "unknown" is a valid bucket. May be {}.
topics array of merged bilingual topic objects merged_topics from _merge_topic(topic_ar, topic_en) [ {…}, … ] The only topic list on the wire — see §3. Each entry carries both languages via field (AR) and field_en (EN) sibling keys. There is no topics_ar / topics_en on the wire.
status string report.status (set to "approved" by build_unified_report_dict) "approved" Always "approved" for a successfully delivered Pipeline C envelope. The run-level status (succeeded / degraded) is not forwarded to the wire today (it lives in the internal dict only).
approved_at string ISO timestamp report.approved_at (= unified run's finished_at) "2026-05-13T05:42:11+03:00" Show as "Generated at" caption. May be "".
bilingual bool report.bilingual true Always true for Pipeline C. If false, fall back to whichever language has content.
no_data bool report.no_data false If true, render a friendly empty-state instead of the topic grid.
pipeline_run_id string (additive, present only when set upstream) report.pipeline_run_id (alias of id) "unified-2026-05-12-deadbeef" Same value as id; surfaced so a client can disambiguate without inferring from the unified-… prefix. Safe to ignore in UI.
coverage_metadata object (additive, present only when set upstream) report.coverage_metadata {"embedding_model_id": "text-embedding-3-small", "embedding_model_version": "v1"} Render in a small "run quality" caption when present. Sub-keys may be null on legacy rows.
forced_low_coverage bool (additive, Task #450) report.forced_low_coverage (sourced from unified_reports.run_flags.forced_low_coverage) false When true, an operator force-ran the day under sub-floor coverage (ADR 0012). The page SHOULD mark the report visibly as a forced run (small badge in the report header — wording recommendation: "Forced run — degraded coverage"). These runs are excluded from the project's internal quality rubric and skip centroid carry-forward. Default false on legacy / clean runs.
coverage_at_trigger number \ null (additive, Task #450) report.coverage_at_trigger (sourced from unified_reports.run_flags.coverage_at_trigger) 62.4 Observed embedding coverage % at the moment the operator confirmed the override. Only meaningful when forced_low_coverage is true. Render alongside the forced-run badge as the operative coverage number (e.g. "62.4% coverage at trigger"). null on non-forced runs and on legacy rows.
quota_floor number \ null (additive, Task #450) report.quota_floor (sourced from unified_reports.quota_floor) 0.482 Adaptive composite-score percentile floor the soft per-domain quota was evaluated against (ADR 0016). null when the quota was disabled or the run produced no reportable selector output (carried-forward / empty runs). Safe to ignore in UI; surface only in an operator-facing "run provenance" caption.
quota_swap_count int (additive, Task #450) report.quota_swap_count (sourced from unified_reports.quota_swap_count) 2 Number of swaps the quota applied. 0 on a balanced keep-set, on legacy runs predating the quota, and on disabled-quota runs. The page MAY surface this as a small chip ("⚖ quota balanced" when 0 and quota_floor != null, "⚖ 2 quota swaps" when > 0).
quota_swap_log array of {promoted_domain, displaced_domain} (additive, Task #450) report.quota_swap_log (compact summary of unified_reports.quota_swap_log; verbose per-swap fields — labels, scores, floor — are NOT on the wire) [{"promoted_domain": "gendered_dimension", "displaced_domain": "political"}] Ordered list of swaps the quota applied (same length as quota_swap_count). [] on a balanced keep-set, on legacy runs, and on disabled-quota runs. The page MAY render the compact list as a tooltip on the quota_swap_count chip; rendering it inline in the topic grid is discouraged because the swap rationale is internal to the editorial selector.

Internal-dict fields NOT forwarded to the wire today (do not write client code that depends on these from the webhook — they are absent from build_webhook_payload):

topics_ar, topics_en, executive_summary_ar, executive_summary_en, executive_summary, pipeline, pipeline_run_status, total_topic_count, reportable_topic_count, preview_includes_unreportable, factuality_summary.

These exist in the in-memory dict that build_unified_report_dict returns, but build_webhook_payload only copies the curated set above. If a future task adds them to the wire it must do so additively, and this spec must be updated in the same change.

---

3. Per-topic schema (entries in topics)

Source of truth: webhook._merge_topic (webhook.py ~lines 142–227). Each entry in the envelope's topics array is the result of merging one (topic_ar, topic_en) pair from _unified_topic_to_segmented_pair. The wire shape is bilingual within a single object — AR text lives under the unsuffixed key and EN text lives under the _en-suffixed sibling. Anything not in the table below is NOT on the wire today, even if it appears in the intermediate per-language dicts.

Topic ordering in topics (Task #452 — "appended, not re-ranked"). The entries in topics arrive in a deliberate order set by build_unified_report_dict (webhook.py ~lines 929–946): reportable topics first, sorted by reportable_rank ascending (NULLS LAST, tied on cluster_label); any topic an operator promotes after the daily run has finalised carries reportable_rank = MAX + 1 and is therefore appended at the tail of the array — not interleaved by score, not re-ranked into the head. The client SHOULD render topics in the order received and MUST NOT re-sort the array by postCount or any other score unless it has an explicit UX reason to do so; re-sorting client-side will silently break the appended-promote contract and confuse operators who expect a post-finalise promotion to land at the bottom of the page. Note that reportable_rank itself is not on the wire today (see §3.4) — the ordering is the contract.

3.1 Bilingual text pairs (per-language)
Wire key (AR) Wire key (EN) Type Source (in _merge_topic) What the page should do
title title_en string topic_ar.title / topic_en.title (EN falls back to AR if missing) Card heading. May be "" on a degraded topic — fall back to original_topic.
brief brief_en string topic_ar.brief / topic_en.brief (EN falls back to AR) One-paragraph summary directly under the title. May be "".
narrative narrative_en string topic_ar.narrative / topic_en.narrative (EN falls back to AR) Longer prose body. Render as expandable. May be "".
detailed_analysis detailed_analysis_en string topic_ar.detailed_analysis / topic_en.detailed_analysis (EN falls back to AR) Legacy long-form analysis field. Pipeline C does not populate it today — typically "". Render only if non-empty.
temporal_analysis temporal_analysis_en string topic_ar.temporal_analysis / topic_en.temporal_analysis (EN falls back to AR) Render in a "Temporal" analysis section. May be "".
platform_analysis platform_analysis_en string topic_ar.platform_analysis / topic_en.platform_analysis (EN falls back to AR) Render in a "Platform" analysis section. May be "".
source_analysis source_analysis_en string topic_ar.source_analysis / topic_en.source_analysis (EN falls back to AR) Render in a "Source / influencer" analysis section. May be "".
location_analysis location_analysis_en string topic_ar.location_analysis / topic_en.location_analysis (EN falls back to AR) Legacy singular alias of geographic_analysis; same content. Render geographic_analysis if present, fall back to location_analysis. Do NOT render both.
geographic_analysis geographic_analysis_en string topic_ar.geographic_analysis / topic_en.geographic_analysis (EN falls back to AR) Render in a "Geographic" analysis section. May be "".
actor_analysis actor_analysis_en string topic_ar.actor_analysis / topic_en.actor_analysis (EN falls back to AR) Render in an "Actors" analysis section. (Pipeline C mirrors the plural actors_analysis text into this key at the wire layer; the plural form itself is NOT on the wire.)
sentiment_summary sentiment_summary_en string topic_ar.sentiment_summary / topic_en.sentiment_summary (EN falls back to AR) One-line sentiment headline. Render under brief or alongside the sentiment chart. May be "".
key_voices key_voices_en array of {account, quote} topic_ar.key_voices / topic_en.key_voices (no cross-language fallback) Render as quoted cards (one per voice): account as small label, quote as the body. Quotes are ≤ 12 words by design. May be [].
viewpoints viewpoints_en array of {perspective, description} topic_ar.viewpoints / topic_en.viewpoints (no cross-language fallback) Render as a labelled accordion keyed by perspective (one of the canonical dashboard sections such as PS_Government, INT_Media, IL_Individuals). description is the per-section commentary text. Entries appear in the canonical section order. May be [].
3.2 Language-agnostic per-topic fields (single value, same across AR and EN view)
Wire key Type Source (in _merge_topic) What the page should do
original_topic string topic_ar.original_topic (= topic_stable_id or domain_primary) Stable cluster identifier. Use as React key for topic cards.
enabled bool topic_ar.enabled (always true on Pipeline C wire) If false, hide the topic.
postCount int topic_ar.stats.total_posts Headline stat on the topic card ("Posts in this cluster").
sentiment object topic_ar.sentiment Sentiment label-to-share object. Render as a donut / stacked bar. May be {}.
sentiment_trend object topic_ar.sentiment_trend Sub-keys may include overall_sentiment (string), sentiment_trajectory (string), daily_sentiment ({date: score, …}), sentiment_shift (string/number). Render as a small sentiment-over-time spark element. May be {}.
keywords object {keyword: count, …} topic_ar.keywords Top-N tag cloud / chip row. May be {}.
locations object {location: count, …} topic_ar.locations Geographic chip row. May be {}.
actors object {actor: count, …} topic_ar.actors Legacy flat actor list. Prefer actors_typed for rendering.
actors_typed array of {name, type, count} topic_ar.actors_typed Typed actor list — render grouped by type. May be [].
actors_structured array topic_ar.actors_structured (or built from actors_typed) Same actors organised for structured rendering. May be [].
hashtags object {tag: count, …} topic_ar.hashtags.trending (the .trending sub-object only — not the full hashtags dict) Top trending hashtags. May be {}.
emotions object topic_ar.emotions Emotion label-to-share. May be {}.
source_analytics object topic_ar.source_analytics Includes a by_section sub-object ({section_key: {count, engagement, …}}) — the data behind the dual-narrative dashboard-section surface. Render in an "Engaged sections" card.
mentions object topic_ar.mentions Mention network (account → count). May be {}.
source_urls array of strings topic_ar.source_urls (= unified_topics.representative_post_urls) Up to ~40 representative URLs. Show as a "view source posts" expandable list.
posts array of post objects _trim_posts_for_wire(topic_ar.example_posts) — capped to WEBHOOK_EXAMPLE_POSTS_PER_TOPIC (default 5) and the duplicate content field stripped when equal to text Render 1–5 sample post cards per topic. Each post object carries the usual external_url / url, text, platform, posted_at, engagement counters, etc. Render text as the body. May be []. The wire key is posts, not example_posts.
domain_routing_method string \ null topic_ar.domain_routing_method "centroid" (anchor-only) or "llm_fallback" (LLM disambiguated) or null (legacy / non-Pipeline-C). Internal diagnostic — fine to ignore in UI, or surface in a small operator tooltip.
engaged_sections array of section keys topic_ar.engaged_sections Ordered list of dashboard section keys (e.g. ["PS_Government", "PS_Media", "INT_Media"]). Render as chips. Always an array — never null.
3.3 No stats / temporal / velocity block on the wire today

_merge_topic does not copy the per-topic stats object onto the wire (it only pulls stats.total_posts into postCount). Per-topic temporal histograms, per-platform breakdowns, velocity metrics, and the bilingual engagement sub-block exist in the intermediate _unified_topic_to_segmented_pair dict but are dropped before transport. If you need a per-topic platform breakdown, the only source on the wire is the envelope-level unique_posts_by_platform, summed across all topics.

3.4 Internal per-topic fields NOT forwarded to the wire today

Documented here so a future task can ship them additively without re-discovery. None of the keys below appear in _merge_topic's output and therefore none arrive at the client today:

- Engagement totals (Task #396 produced these upstream but the wire merge drops them): total_reactions, total_shares, total_comments, total_views. - Plural narrative-analysis aliases: actors_analysis, sentiment_analysis. (Only the singular actor_analysis and the plural geographic_analysis survive on the wire; actor_analysis is mirrored from the upstream plural.) - Pipeline-C identity fields: domain_primary, momentum, is_carried_forward, topic_stable_id, source_influence_tiers. - stats (and its platforms, temporal, velocity, engagement sub-objects), admin_edited, visualization (the entire object — dropped in Task #405).

A client built against this spec MUST NOT assume these keys are present. If you want a "Continuing" badge or a domain pill, you will need a follow-up to extend _merge_topic; this spec calls that gap out in §6 task 11.

---

4. Rendering rules

4.1 Pipeline scoping

- Filter the incoming feed by category == "unified-pipeline-c". Pipeline B reports use different category keys and may have a slightly different per-topic shape — do not mix them on the same page. - pipeline_run_id is an additive optional alias of id. Use it only if you need to disambiguate without parsing the unified-… prefix.

4.2 Bilingual layout

- The page MUST support an AR (RTL) view and an EN (LTR) view. Use IBM Plex Sans for both. Brand palette: Teal, Orange, Dark Blue, Yellow, Green, Red-Orange (match the existing admin dashboard by eye). - Day-level executive summary: AR view shows overallNarrative; EN view shows overallNarrativeEn. If only one is non-empty (legacy edge case), show that one in both views with a small "(translation pending)" caption. - Per-topic: every text field has an AR variant and an EN variant (see §3.1). AR view reads title, brief, narrative, actor_analysis, sentiment_summary, etc.; EN view reads the _en-suffixed siblings. Both come from the same merged topic object — you iterate topics once and pick the right side per view, you do not pick a different array.

4.3 Sectioning order on a topic card

1. Title (title / title_en). 2. Brief (brief / brief_en) — one paragraph directly under the title. 3. Headline stat — postCount. 4. Engaged sections chips (engaged_sections). 5. Key voices cards (key_voices / key_voices_en) — one quoted card per {account, quote}. 6. Viewpoints accordion (viewpoints / viewpoints_en) — one item per {perspective, description}, keyed by perspective. 7. Narrative (narrative / narrative_en) — long-form prose, expandable. 8. Six narrative-analysis sections: actor_analysis / actor_analysis_en, sentiment_summary / sentiment_summary_en (one-line headline), geographic_analysis / geographic_analysis_en (fallback to location_analysis if empty), temporal_analysis / temporal_analysis_en, platform_analysis / platform_analysis_en, source_analysis / source_analysis_en. 9. Visualisation cards — keywords, locations, actors_typed, sentiment, emotions, hashtags, source_analytics (with its by_section sub-object surfacing the dashboard-section split), mentions, sentiment_trend. 10. Sample posts (posts) — up to 5 cards, each showing text, platform, posted_at, engagement counters, and a link to external_url / url. 11. Source URLs (source_urls) — collapsed list of representative URLs.

4.4 Run-quality caption (above the topic grid)

Render a small caption strip showing:

- reportDate - id (mono, small) - coverage_metadata.embedding_model_id + .embedding_model_version if present (e.g. text-embedding-3-small · v1) - approved_at as a relative timestamp ("Generated 4 h ago")

The richer "shipping N of M topics" rollup and the factuality_summary chips cannot be rendered yet — those keys are not on the wire (§3.4). Do not invent them client-side.

4.5 Headline stats row

- total_posts — large number, "Posts analysed" label. - Per-platform breakdown from unique_posts_by_platform — one tile per platform. Skip platforms with 0 counts. Lowercase keys are expected; capitalise for display (e.g. twitter → "X / Twitter").

4.6 Must-show vs nice-to-show

Must show (do not silently drop):

- Envelope: reportDate, total_posts, unique_posts_by_platform, overallNarrative / overallNarrativeEn. - Per topic: title / title_en, brief / brief_en, narrative / narrative_en, the six narrative analyses, sentiment_summary / sentiment_summary_en, key_voices / key_voices_en, viewpoints / viewpoints_en, engaged_sections, postCount, sentiment, sentiment_trend, posts.

Nice to show (operator / power-user surfaces):

- pipeline_run_id, coverage_metadata, domain_routing_method, actors_structured, mentions, source_urls, detailed_analysis*.

4.7 Empty-state handling

- Envelope-level no_data == true → render a friendly "No topics today" empty state instead of the grid; still show the headline strip. - Per-topic empty strings (title, brief, narrative, any of the six narrative analyses) → render the section with a muted "(not available)" placeholder rather than collapsing it. This makes a degraded-LLM topic visible to the operator instead of pretending it doesn't exist. - Empty objects / arrays (keywords == {}, viewpoints == [], etc.) → hide that specific card; do not render an empty container.

4.8 Forward-only compatibility

- Treat any unknown additive key as informational and ignore it rather than erroring. New keys may appear in any release. - Treat any missing additive key as null / "" / [] / {} per its documented type. Do not throw if a field documented here is absent on a legacy row. - Never assume the order of keys in objects is meaningful — sort client-side where ordering matters (chip rows, accordion items).

4.9 Topic ordering in topics (server-set, do not re-sort)

The order of entries in the envelope's topics array is set by the server and is itself part of the contract (see §3 intro and Task #452). Reportable topics come first, ordered by reportable_rank ascending; any topic an operator promotes after the daily run finalised is appended at the tail. The client SHOULD render the topic grid in the order received and MUST NOT re-sort by postCount (or any other score) unless it has an explicit UX reason to do so — client-side re-sorting will silently break the "appended, not re-ranked" contract and a post-finalise promotion will stop landing at the bottom of the page where operators expect it.

---

5. "No drops" appendix — flat checklist

Use this checklist when reviewing the page: every key MUST be either rendered or have a deliberate "ignored because …" justification.

5.1 Envelope keys on the wire
  • [ ] id
  • [ ] reportDate
  • [ ] category
  • [ ] categoryName
  • [ ] category_name_ar
  • [ ] title
  • [ ] title_en
  • [ ] overallNarrative
  • [ ] overallNarrativeEn
  • [ ] total_posts
  • [ ] unique_posts_by_platform
  • [ ] topics
  • [ ] status
  • [ ] approved_at
  • [ ] bilingual
  • [ ] no_data
  • [ ] pipeline_run_id (additive — present when set upstream)
  • [ ] coverage_metadata (additive — present when set upstream)
  • [ ] forced_low_coverage (additive, Task #450 — present on Pipeline C)
  • [ ] coverage_at_trigger (additive, Task #450 — present on Pipeline C)
  • [ ] quota_floor (additive, Task #450 — present on Pipeline C)
  • [ ] quota_swap_count (additive, Task #450 — present on Pipeline C)
  • [ ] quota_swap_log (additive, Task #450 — present on Pipeline C)
5.2 Per-topic bilingual text pairs on the wire
  • [ ] title (AR) + title_en (EN)
  • [ ] brief (AR) + brief_en (EN)
  • [ ] narrative (AR) + narrative_en (EN)
  • [ ] detailed_analysis (AR) + detailed_analysis_en (EN)
  • [ ] temporal_analysis (AR) + temporal_analysis_en (EN)
  • [ ] platform_analysis (AR) + platform_analysis_en (EN)
  • [ ] source_analysis (AR) + source_analysis_en (EN)
  • [ ] location_analysis (AR) + location_analysis_en (EN)
  • [ ] geographic_analysis (AR) + geographic_analysis_en (EN)
  • [ ] actor_analysis (AR) + actor_analysis_en (EN)
  • [ ] sentiment_summary (AR) + sentiment_summary_en (EN)
  • [ ] key_voices (AR) + key_voices_en (EN)
  • [ ] viewpoints (AR) + viewpoints_en (EN)
5.3 Per-topic language-agnostic keys on the wire
  • [ ] original_topic
  • [ ] enabled
  • [ ] postCount
  • [ ] sentiment
  • [ ] sentiment_trend
  • [ ] keywords
  • [ ] locations
  • [ ] actors
  • [ ] actors_typed
  • [ ] actors_structured
  • [ ] hashtags (= the .trending sub-object)
  • [ ] emotions
  • [ ] source_analytics (incl. by_section)
  • [ ] mentions
  • [ ] source_urls
  • [ ] posts (renamed from example_posts; capped to 5)
  • [ ] domain_routing_method
  • [ ] engaged_sections
5.4 Upstream fields known to be absent on the wire (do NOT depend on)

- topics_ar, topics_en (replaced by merged topics) - executive_summary_ar, executive_summary_en, executive_summary (only overallNarrative / overallNarrativeEn survive) - pipeline, pipeline_run_status, total_topic_count, reportable_topic_count, preview_includes_unreportable, factuality_summary - Per-topic: domain_primary, momentum, is_carried_forward, topic_stable_id, source_influence_tiers, actors_analysis (plural), sentiment_analysis, total_reactions, total_shares, total_comments, total_views, stats, admin_edited, visualization

---

6. Client implementation prompt (ready to paste into an AI builder)

Build a page in our client app called "Pipeline C Daily Reports". Data shape and transport are fully specified in docs/client-pipeline-c-spec.md — read it end-to-end before writing any code. Implement against §2 (envelope), §3 (per-topic), §1 (transport), and §4 (rendering rules). Use §5 as the review checklist — every listed wire key must be either rendered on the page or have an explicit "ignored because …" comment in the code. Requirements: 1. Ingest. Accept the webhook at POST /api/receive-report with x-api-key header (§1.1). Dedupe on (id, status). Return 2xx on accept. The sender retries any non-2xx (including 4xx) up to MAX_RETRIES; the only exception is HTTP 413, which the sender treats as permanent. Do not retry yourself. 2. Store. Persist the full envelope verbatim. Upsert on id. Re-deliveries must overwrite in place. Tolerate unknown additive keys (do not strip them in storage). 3. List page. Show all stored reports where category == "unified-pipeline-c", newest first by reportDate. Each row: date, EN title, AR title, count of topics (topics.length). 4. Detail page. Render one report end-to-end per §4. Provide an AR (RTL) and EN (LTR) view toggle. Iterate the single topics array once; in AR view read the unsuffixed keys (title, brief, narrative, …), in EN view read the _en-suffixed siblings. 5. Bilingual fields. Show the per-language strings from the matching side of each topic. Do NOT render location_analysis* as a separate section when geographic_analysis* is non-empty — location_analysis* is a legacy alias of the same content. Same goes for actor_analysis* (which on Pipeline C wires mirrors the upstream actors_analysis text). 6. Backfill. There is no public pull endpoint that returns the Pipeline C envelope (§1.2). To recover a missed delivery, ask an operator to trigger an admin redeliver (POST /api/admin/pipeline-c/runs/{report_id}/redeliver) or a date-range replay (POST /api/reports/backfill-webhook); both push through the normal §1.1 webhook path and the client's receive handler upserts as usual. 7. Empty states. Honour §4.7: render placeholders for empty per-language strings, hide cards backed by empty objects / arrays, and render the envelope-level empty state when no_data == true. 8. Styling. IBM Plex Sans for both languages, AR set RTL. Palette: Teal / Orange / Dark Blue / Yellow / Green / Red-Orange. Match the existing admin dashboard. 9. Forward-only. Tolerate unknown keys without erroring. Treat documented-but-missing keys as null / "" / [] / {} per their type. 10. Verification. Before shipping, run through the checklist in §5 and confirm every wire key is either rendered or has an explicit "ignored because …" code comment. 11. Known wire gaps (do NOT invent these client-side). Per §3.4, the Pipeline-C identity fields (domain_primary, momentum, is_carried_forward, topic_stable_id), the Task #396 engagement totals (total_reactions, total_shares, total_comments, total_views), and the envelope-level factuality_summary / pipeline_run_status / total_topic_count / reportable_topic_count are produced upstream but not on the wire today. Leave hooks in the UI for "Continuing" badges, domain pills, engagement tiles, and a run-quality strip; do not fabricate the data. A follow-up will widen _merge_topic and build_webhook_payload and update this spec in the same change. Do not invent fields that are not in this spec. If a behaviour is ambiguous, prefer the conservative rendering choice (show, do not hide).

22 / 28 · Client Spec — Pipeline C wire contract Source: client-pipeline-c-spec.md · 2026-05-21 · companion to §19 Pipeline C Architecture
23 / Data & Quality

Labeling & integrity.

We can sharpen the pipeline all we want, but if the upstream labeling delivers fields whose contracts don't match what the pipeline expects, or whose semantics drift from what they're called, we are tightening downstream of a leaky source. Source: labeling-quality-note.

Methodology connection · Bias 7 — NLP & Language

The methodology prescribes "language-specific NLP models where available (CAMeL-Tools for Arabic, dialect-aware BERT models); minimum 5% stratified random sample human validation (10% for Arabic-primary reports); document model name and version in every report." The product currently uses general-purpose OpenAI / xAI Grok models (no CAMeL-Tools); validation rate is not published per-report; model name and version are recorded in LLM_CALL_REGISTRY but not surfaced in user output. The findings below — sentiment_label casing/language drift, multi-label encoding ambiguity, mis-named topic taxonomy — are precisely the failure modes the methodology predicts when these mitigations are not in place.

What's good in the labeling

Worth saying first, because the rest of this section is critical.

  • nlp_analysis.geo_locations — 81.7% of posts populated; 991 records covering 391 distinct locations; 93.3% have country_en. Schema is consistent. strong
  • nlp_analysis.actors in input CSVs — 72.9% populated; 100% with normalized_name_en; clean type vocabulary (organization, person, entity, country, group, nationality). No “unknown” values at this stage. The 81.57%-unknown problem is downstream. strong
  • keywords / keywords_en / keywords_stem — three parallel views; 99.8% populated. strong
  • profile_classification + profile_affiliation together carry meaningful publisher signal. strong

Findings, ranked by impact

1 · nlp_analysis.topic is mislabeled

The downstream pipeline reads this field as if it were a topic identifier and groups posts by it. It isn't a topic — it's a fixed 13-theme taxonomy of the kind a manual content classifier would emit. In one examined file, 60% of posts share “Politics & Governance” (alone or in combination with other themes). A 15-post random sample tagged exactly “Politics & Governance” contained 9+ distinct events: local elections, IDF raids, an op-ed about Trump and Iran, Mahmoud Abbas's diplomacy in Amman, Israeli air-raid sirens, Hebron mayoral handover, settler clashes, salary policy, traffic enforcement.

2 · Multi-label encoding is a comma-joined string with no schema

56.7% of posts carry comma-joined multi-label values like Advocacy & Activism, Human Rights, Politics & Governance. There is no schema spec — the consumer has to guess whether to treat the whole string as a single value or split on comma. In profile_classification, three different conventions appear in the same column.

3 · nlp_analysis.sentiment.confidence is categorical when the consumer expects numeric

The methodology PDF describes a Stage 3 filter that drops posts with nlp_confidence < 0.3. The actual values: high (495), medium (120), null (1) — three string values, no low seen at all. The numeric comparison silently fails (or gets coerced in some way nobody documented).

4 · Casing/language drift on topics.sentiment_label

LabelCount%
سلبي3,40726.69
Negative3,39726.61
محايد2,11016.53
Neutral2,10616.50
إيجابي8726.83
Positive8716.82

Client code that compares sentiment_label === "negative" (lowercase) misses every row. ~83% of rows are typed/cased differently from the lowercase form a programmer would naturally guess.

5 · Trend-direction four-way split

Four casings/decorations for three concepts (stable, Stable, ➡️ Stable). Any client filter using equality on one casing misclassifies the other three.

Six dead-or-near-dead fields

FieldPopulationStatus
topics.viewpoints0dead
topics.sentiment_confidence0dead
topics.detailed_analysis4.07%effectively dead
topics.actors_analysis99.6%unread by client
topics.sentiment_analysis99.7%unread by client
topics.geographic_analysis99.6%unread by client

What we want back from the labeling side

The note's “suggested first wave for the labeling side” — the smallest set of fixes that would unblock the most downstream features:

  • Document the nlp_analysis.topic taxonomy and stop using it as a topic ID; rename or repurpose.
  • Schema-spec the multi-label encoding (comma-join vs array) for every field that uses it.
  • Make nlp_confidence numeric, or document the categorical mapping with thresholds.
  • Pick one casing/language convention for sentiment_label and trend_direction; surface the other in display only.
23 / 28 · Labeling & Integrity Source: Labeling Quality Note · Data Inventory §2, §5
24 / Research & Prototypes

Topic Capturing Pipeline. Experimental

A prospective end-to-end pipeline for automatic topic detection, clustering, and labelling. Applies multilingual NLP to embed posts as semantic vectors, then uses hierarchical density-based clustering to organize content into a three-level taxonomy — Topics → Subtopics → Events. Fully reproducible: fixed random seeds at every stochastic step, guaranteeing identical outputs given identical input data. Source: Topic_Capturing_Pipeline_Documentation.docx.

EXPERIMENTAL · In testing phase · Not in production

This pipeline is not currently implemented anywhere in production. Pipeline C (§17) remains the in-flight production clustering pipeline; the segmented "Pipeline B" path (see §08 Admin Portals) remains the legacy path. The decision whether this prototype's three-level hierarchy ever lands in production is open and unmade, and is independent of the Pipeline C republishing-readiness verdict.

Authorship

Authored by the project's data analyst as a standalone prototype. This is not work produced by the Replit-based build agent, not work produced by Claude, not part of the FastAPI data-app codebase, and not part of the Express / React client-app codebase. The pipeline lives outside both production repositories. The content below faithfully reproduces the analyst's source document; framing, comparisons, and methodology connections are documentation-portal additions.

Methodology connection · cluster-quality discipline

Stages 6 and 7 of this prototype (the HDBSCAN parameter sweep, and the post-clustering quality metrics: Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin, Calinski-Harabasz) are direct expressions of the methodology's cluster-cohesion discipline. Pipeline C currently computes some cohesion diagnostics but does not gate publication on them (Pipeline C §5.2). This prototype foregrounds parameter selection as an explicit sweep-then-pick decision rather than a fixed-constant choice — closing one of the gaps the Pipeline C audit surfaced. Comparison at §20 below.

§1 — Pipeline at a glance

The table below summarises all 10 stages of the processing pipeline, their types, and their primary function. Types are one of: I/O, Transform, Clean, ML, Reduce, Tune.

StageNameTypeSummary
1Data Ingestion & SamplingI/ORead CSV, filter nulls, sample 3,000 posts
2Embedding Text ConstructionTransformBuild weighted composite text per post
3Text CleaningCleanStrip URLs, mentions, normalize charset
4Sentence EmbeddingMLEncode posts to 768-dim dense vectors
5UMAP Dimensionality ReductionReduceReduce to 30-dim (cluster) + 2-dim (viz)
6HDBSCAN Parameter SweepTuneGrid-search min_cluster_size values
7Main Topic ClusteringMLHDBSCAN on 30-dim space → topic labels
8Subtopic ClusteringMLLocal UMAP + HDBSCAN per main topic
9Event ClusteringMLAgglomerative clustering within subtopics
10CSV ExportI/OWrite output CSV with full hierarchy

§2 — Clustering hierarchy

The pipeline organizes content into a three-level taxonomy. Each level uses a different algorithm tuned to that level's granularity.

Level 1

Main Topics

Algorithm: Global HDBSCAN on 30-dim UMAP-reduced embeddings.

What it captures: Broad thematic groups across the entire sample.

ID format: integer (1-indexed in output CSV)

Level 2

Subtopics

Algorithm: Local UMAP + HDBSCAN within each main topic of ≥ 50 posts.

What it captures: Fine-grained sub-themes invisible in the global projection.

ID format: TOPIC.SUBTOPIC (e.g., 3.2)

Level 3

Events

Algorithm: Agglomerative clustering within each subtopic.

What it captures: Discrete, temporally or contextually specific incidents.

ID format: TOPIC.SUBTOPIC.EVENT (e.g., 2.1.3)

§3 — Stage-by-stage reference

Each stage faithfully reproduced from the source document, with its description and parameter table.

Stage 1 · Data Ingestion & Sampling I/O

The pipeline reads the source CSV file using pandas with UTF-8-BOM (utf-8-sig) encoding to correctly handle byte-order marks produced by Excel and other Windows tools. Rows where the content column is null are removed before any further processing to prevent downstream errors.

Input fileposts - 2026-05-07T111357.906.csv
Encodingutf-8-sig (UTF-8 with BOM — Excel-compatible)
Null filterRows with null content column are dropped
Sample size3,000 posts
Random seed42 (fixed — ensures reproducibility across runs)
Key outputssample_df, display_posts, display_platforms
Stage 2 · Embedding Text Construction Transform

For each post, a single composite string is assembled from multiple NLP-enriched metadata fields. Fields are repeated to increase their weight in the embedding space, biasing the model to cluster by theme and topic rather than surface-level lexical overlap.

The weighting scheme is as follows (most-weighted first):

  1. Topic
  2. Keywords
  3. Geo locations
  4. Raw content
  5. Actors

Note: the specific repetition counts per field are not given in the source document — only the relative ordering.

Stage 3 · Text Cleaning Clean

Before embedding, each composite string is cleaned with a regex-based pipeline to remove noise that would degrade embedding quality. The cleaning steps are applied sequentially:

  • URL removal: All http:// and www. patterns are stripped entirely
  • Mention removal: All @username mentions are stripped
  • Hashtag normalisation: The # symbol is removed, converting #hashtag → hashtag (retaining the word)
  • Charset filtering: Non-word characters outside Latin, Arabic (U+0600–U+06FF), and Hebrew (U+0590–U+05FF) scripts are replaced with spaces
  • Whitespace collapse: Multiple consecutive spaces are reduced to a single space
  • Minimum length filter: Posts with fewer than 5 words after cleaning are dropped from the sample
URL patternhttp/www patterns → removed
Mention pattern@username → removed
Hashtag pattern#word → word (# stripped)
Charset filterLatin + Arabic (U+0600–U+06FF) + Hebrew (U+0590–U+05FF)
Min length≥ 5 words required to retain a post
Key outputsclean_posts (for embedding), clean_display_posts (for output), clean_platforms
Stage 4 · Sentence Embedding ML

Each cleaned post is transformed into a dense 768-dimensional numerical vector using the paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2 model from the Sentence Transformers library. This model was selected for its strong multilingual coverage (50+ languages), making it appropriate for corpora that contain both Arabic and English content.

Embeddings are L2-normalised to unit vectors on the hypersphere. This normalisation step is critical: it makes cosine similarity equivalent to the dot product, which is required by the cosine metric used in the subsequent UMAP dimensionality reduction stage.

Modelparaphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2 (Sentence Transformers)
Languages50+ languages including Arabic and English
Output dims768 dimensions per post
Batch size32 posts per forward pass
NormalisationL2 unit vectors (cosine similarity ≡ dot product)
Model size~280 MB (downloaded and cached on first run)
Key outputsembeddings (numpy array, shape N × 768)
Stage 5 · UMAP Dimensionality Reduction Reduce

Raw 768-dimensional embeddings are far too high-dimensional for density-based clustering algorithms to work effectively (the curse of dimensionality). UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) is used to reduce the embeddings to a lower-dimensional space that preserves the global semantic structure while making clusters more compact and separable.

Two separate UMAP projections are computed for different purposes:

  • 30-dimensional UMAP (clustering): Used as input to HDBSCAN. A high n_neighbors value (50) ensures the global topic structure is captured. min_dist=0.0 creates tightly packed clusters, which is ideal for HDBSCAN's density estimation.
  • 2-dimensional UMAP (visualisation): Used only to generate scatter-plot visualisations. min_dist=0.1 spreads points apart for readability. This projection is never used for clustering.
Clustering dims30
Visualisation dims2
n_neighbors50 (high value to capture global structure)
min_dist (cluster)0.0 (tight packing — ideal for HDBSCAN)
min_dist (viz)0.1 (spread for readability)
metriccosine
random_state42 (reproducible)
Key outputsembeddings_reduced (N×30), embeddings_2d (N×2)
Stage 6 · HDBSCAN Parameter Sweep Tune

Before the main clustering run, an automated parameter sweep explores 8 candidate values for min_cluster_size, the most impactful HDBSCAN hyperparameter. For each candidate value, HDBSCAN is run on the 30-dimensional UMAP embeddings and three diagnostic metrics are recorded:

  • Number of topics found: How many clusters HDBSCAN identified
  • Noise ratio: The fraction of posts assigned label -1 (no cluster)
  • Silhouette score: Cluster cohesion and separation quality (cosine metric; computed only on non-noise posts)

The sweep produces three diagnostic charts to guide selection of the final min_cluster_size parameter.

Values tested10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 70, 100
min_samplesmax(3, int(mcs × 0.25)) — auto-derived per value
metriceuclidean (on UMAP-reduced space)
cluster_selectioneom (Excess of Mass)
Silhouette metriccosine (on non-noise posts only)
Key outputssweep_df (DataFrame), 3 diagnostic charts
Stage 7 · Main Topic Clustering — HDBSCAN ML

The main topic clustering step applies HDBSCAN to the 30-dimensional UMAP embeddings using the parameters selected from the sweep (min_cluster_size=30, min_samples=5). Posts that do not fit any cluster receive the noise label -1 and are retained in all outputs but not assigned a topic title.

Three clustering quality metrics are computed after clustering:

  • Silhouette Score: Measures intra-cluster cohesion versus inter-cluster separation (higher is better)
  • Davies-Bouldin Index: Ratio of within-cluster scatter to between-cluster separation (lower is better)
  • Calinski-Harabasz Index: Ratio of between-cluster dispersion to within-cluster dispersion (higher is better)
AlgorithmHDBSCAN
min_cluster_size30
min_samples5
metriceuclidean
cluster_selectioneom (Excess of Mass)
Noise label-1 (posts that fit no cluster)
Topic ID schemeInternal: 0-indexed. Output CSV: 1-indexed (offset +1)
Key outputsbest_labels (array), main_topic_titles (dict), cluster_sizes (Counter)
Stage 8 · Subtopic Clustering — Local UMAP + HDBSCAN ML

For each main topic with 50 or more posts, a dedicated local UMAP + HDBSCAN pipeline is run using only the embeddings belonging to that topic. This local approach reveals within-topic structure that is invisible in the global 30-dimensional space because the global projection compresses fine-grained distinctions.

Topics with fewer than 50 posts are assigned a single subtopic (ID = 1) and are not sub-clustered.

  • Local n_neighbors is capped at min(15, topic_size - 1) to handle small clusters
  • Local n_components is capped at min(10, topic_size - 2) for the same reason
  • sub_min_cluster_size is auto-derived as max(10, int(topic_size × 0.08))
  • sub_min_samples is auto-derived as max(3, int(sub_mcs × 0.40))

Subtopic IDs use a dot notation: TOPIC.SUBTOPIC (e.g., 3.2 = main topic 3, subtopic 2). Posts with noise label -1 within their subtopic are retained in the output.

Min main topic size50 posts (smaller → single subtopic, no sub-clustering)
Local n_neighborsmin(15, topic_size - 1)
Local n_componentsmin(10, topic_size - 2)
sub_min_cluster_sizemax(10, int(topic_size × 0.08))
sub_min_samplesmax(3, int(sub_mcs × 0.40))
Subtopic ID formatTOPIC.SUBTOPIC (e.g. 3.2)
Key outputssubtopic_labels (list), subtopic_titles (dict), subtopic_summary_df
Stage 9 · Event Clustering — Agglomerative ML

Within each subtopic that has X or more posts (where X is unspecified in the source — see §5 below), Agglomerative Clustering groups posts into discrete events. Unlike HDBSCAN, agglomerative clustering produces no noise — every post is assigned to a group. However, groups smaller than MIN_EVENT_GROUP_SIZE (3 posts) are discarded and their posts are labelled -1 (event noise).

Agglomerative clustering with a cosine distance threshold of 0.25 and average linkage was chosen for this level because it reliably partitions dense subtopic clusters into coherent, actionable event groups without requiring a pre-specified number of clusters.

Event IDs follow the three-level dot notation: TOPIC.SUBTOPIC.EVENT (e.g., 2.1.3).

AlgorithmAgglomerativeClustering (sklearn)
Distance threshold0.25 (cosine distance)
Linkageaverage
Min event size3 posts (smaller groups → noise label -1)
Min subtopic sizeX — unspecified in source
Event ID formatTOPIC.SUBTOPIC.EVENT (e.g. 2.1.3)
Key outputsevent_labels (list), event_metrics_df
Stage 10 · CSV Export I/O

The source document has no Stage 10 detail entry in Section 3. The only mention of Stage 10 is the row in Section 2's "Pipeline at a Glance" table, which reads in full: "Write 1 output CSVs with full hierarchy" (likely a typo for "Write 1 output CSV"). The output filename, the column set, the row ordering, and whether noise-labelled posts are retained in the export are not specified.

Reasonable defaults can be inferred from the hierarchy IDs documented in Stages 7–9 (each post carries a main-topic ID, a TOPIC.SUBTOPIC subtopic ID, and a TOPIC.SUBTOPIC.EVENT event ID, with -1 at any level indicating noise at that level) — but the export schema itself awaits analyst confirmation.

§4 — How this differs from Pipeline C

Side-by-side comparison of the prototype against the in-flight production pipeline. The two share an upstream input shape (the same daily posts.csv format) but take fundamentally different architectural approaches downstream.

DimensionPipeline C (production, in-flight)Topic Capturing (experimental)
StatusProduction code; audit verdict NOT READY (§17)Testing phase; not in any production codebase
AuthorshipBuilt by Replit + Claude; audited by ClaudeAuthored by the project data analyst
CodebaseFastAPI data app (pipeline_c_*.py)Standalone — outside both production repos
Input scopeWhole-day corpus (entire posts.csv)3,000-post sample (random seed 42)
Embedding modeltext-embedding-3-small (OpenAI, 1536-dim)paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2 (Sentence Transformers, 768-dim)
LanguagesDepends on OpenAI multilingual coverageExplicit 50+ language support; Latin + Arabic + Hebrew charset filter
Pre-clustering reductionNone at clustering step (1536-dim direct)UMAP to 30 dimensions (cosine, n_neighbors=50, min_dist=0.0)
Clustering levels1 (flat HDBSCAN)3 (Topic → Subtopic → Event)
Level-1 paramsmin_cluster_size=8, min_samples=3min_cluster_size=30, min_samples=5
Level-2 algorithmn/aLocal UMAP + HDBSCAN per topic ≥ 50 posts
Level-3 algorithmn/aAgglomerative (cosine, threshold 0.25, average linkage)
Parameter selectionFixed constants in codeSweep over 8 candidate min_cluster_size values
Cluster-quality metricsComputed but not gatingThree diagnostic metrics produced by the sweep (number of topics found, noise ratio, Silhouette score); the selection rule that picks the final value from the diagnostics is not specified in source
Domain routing8 anchor-based domain prompts (political / financial / social / health / security / religious / educational / unclassified)Not specified in source
Bilingual LLM narrativeYes — per-domain prompts produce AR + ENNot in source (this prototype is a clustering pipeline; narrative-labelling layer not documented)
Cross-day continuitypgvector + HNSW, threshold 0.30Not in source
ReproducibilityStochastic in placesFixed seeds at every stochastic step (deterministic by design)

§5 — Gaps in the source document

Honest framing of what the source document does not specify. None of these are blockers; each is a follow-up question for the analyst before the prototype could be evaluated against production criteria.

GapWhere it appearsQuestion to resolve
Field repetition counts in stage 2Stage 2 — Embedding Text ConstructionThe relative ordering is given (Topic > Keywords > Geo > Content > Actors), but the exact repetition counts that produce those weights are not specified.
Min subtopic size in stage 9Stage 9 — Event Clustering ("Within each subtopic that has X or more posts")The threshold X is left as a placeholder. Required to know which subtopics get split into events vs. emit a single event.
Output CSV schema in stage 10Stage 10 — CSV Export ("Write 1 output CSVs with full hierarchy")The exact column set, ordering, and naming for the export are not enumerated. Phrasing also appears typo'd.
How main_topic_titles and subtopic_titles are derivedStages 7 and 8 list these as key outputsThe pipeline produces titles, but the title-generation method (keyword extraction, LLM, centroid-nearest, etc.) is not described.
Sample weighting / stratificationStage 1 — SamplingThe 3,000-post sample is described as random (seed 42). Whether the sample stratifies by platform, language, or date is not specified.
How parameters from stage-6 sweep are selectedStages 6 → 7The sweep produces diagnostics but the source does not specify the decision rule (manual analyst pick? highest silhouette? Pareto frontier on (topics-found, noise-ratio, silhouette)?). Stage 7 names min_cluster_size=30 as "the parameters selected from the sweep" without naming the selection criterion.

§6 — Why this is worth evaluating

Three reasons the prototype is interesting relative to the existing production stack, framed neutrally as evaluation criteria — not as a recommendation to adopt.

Hierarchy

Three-level structure

Pipeline C produces one level of topics. This prototype produces Topic → Subtopic → Event. If the additional granularity surfaces structure analysts currently can't see, it may justify a structurally larger output payload. If it produces splits that don't track real analyst intuition, it adds complexity without value. Evaluation question: do the Level 2 and Level 3 splits match the way analysts already mentally subdivide a topic?

Reproducibility

Deterministic by design

Pipeline C is stochastic in places. This prototype fixes random seeds at every stochastic stage — re-running on identical input produces identical output. For audit, regression testing, and "what changed since last run?" investigations, deterministic output is a measurable improvement.

Parameter discipline

Sweep-then-pick

Pipeline C uses fixed-constant clustering parameters. This prototype foregrounds parameter selection as an explicit sweep over 8 candidates with three diagnostic metrics. Aligns with the methodology's confidence-tier discipline (§5 and §7) — measure cluster quality before committing.

Reasons against: sample-based (3,000 posts is < 2% of a typical day's raw_posts volume); does not document a narrative-labelling layer; does not document cross-day continuity; lives outside both production repos so adopting it means writing new integration code. The decision is not made here.

§7 — Decision status

For the avoidance of doubt:

  • The prototype has been reviewed and documented in this portal.
  • No decision has been taken to adopt, integrate, or schedule it.
  • The §5 gaps are open questions for the analyst, not blockers in the production sense.
  • If this work proceeds, the natural next step is an apples-to-apples comparison run against the same posts.csv Pipeline C consumes, on a stable date with completed Pipeline C output for reference.
24 / 28 · Topic Capturing Pipeline (Experimental) Source: Topic_Capturing_Pipeline_Documentation.docx · authored by data analyst · received 2026-05-13
25 / Operations

Tasks, audit findings & security.

Snapshot of recent task closures, the standing strategic audit findings, and the unresolved security/architecture issues. The task corpus is the durable record; this page picks out the consequential entries. Source: System Audit Tasklist · Task #50.

Recent closure — Task #50

Closed

Platform parity & unique post counts in report payload

Asked: Diagnose why Facebook is ~10–18% of /api/client/posts/raw but only ~1% of per-platform totals summed across stored reports. Add a non-breaking unique_posts_by_platform field to the report payload. Add a daily-summary client endpoint.

Changed: New diagnostic scripts/diagnose_platform_parity.py; new db.py helper get_raw_posts_platform_counts_for_date; build_webhook_payload now carries unique_posts_by_platform at top level; format_report_response calls webhook._unique_posts_by_platform_from_topics directly so the report endpoint and webhook payload can never drift; new GET /api/client/reports/daily-summary?date=YYYY-MM-DD.

Root cause: The per-category fixed_window_* CSVs themselves are FB-light; no platform filter exists in processing.py. Upstream and explicitly out of scope.

Verification: End-to-end check on report_PS_Youth_2026-04-23 produced unique_posts_by_platform={"telegram": 3} on both format_report_response and webhook payload.

Strategic audit — built logically

  • Dual-server architecture (FastAPI for API + admin; Streamlit for internal analysis UI). Clean separation.
  • Bilingual design with Arabic-first RTL and parallel English output. Language validation with retry mechanisms.
  • Smart topic consolidation — Jaccard similarity at 40% threshold prevents redundant micro-topics. Profile-weighted scoring prioritizes authoritative sources. Engagement + NLP confidence + similarity scoring is well-balanced.
  • Comprehensive logging — JobLogger with AI call tracking, cost estimation, error logging. Thread-safe context propagation for parallel workers. Structured JSON logs.
  • Three-state report workflow — draft → approved → rejected with predefined rejection reasons and unreject capability.
  • Structured visualization data — separated visualization namespace for client rendering, with legacy fields preserved for backward compatibility.

Strategic audit — built sub-optimally

Critical · duplicate OpenAI clients

processing.py:17-21 has a 60s timeout. app.py:19-22 has no timeout. The Streamlit app can hang indefinitely on AI calls. Fix: consolidate to single client, or add timeout to app.py.

Critical · two separate processing pipelines

FastAPI (api.py) uses processing.py, gets JobLogger, has timeout fix, supports 6 categories. Streamlit (app.py) duplicates logic locally, has no logging, missing timeout, no category support. Recommendation: deprecate Streamlit processing — use it only as API consumer or read-only viewer.

Duplicate constants

MIN_TOPICS = 8 / MAX_TOPICS = 14 in both processing.py:26-27 and app.py:27-28.

Critical security issues — eight findings (S1–S8)

Each finding names the specific endpoint, file:line, and concrete attack scenario. None of these is a hypothetical; every one was verified during the audit.

S1 · Mass AI Regeneration Without Auth routes.ts:1593 · CRITICAL

POST /api/weekly-reports/regenerate-all — no auth whatsoever. Any anonymous caller can trigger regeneration of ALL weekly reports. Each one makes a paid AI call. This is both a financial attack surface (LLM cost amplification) and destructive (overwrites completed reports with regenerated versions).

S2 · Weekly Settings Writable Without Auth routes.ts:1631 · CRITICAL

PUT /api/weekly-settings — no auth. Anyone can change the day-of-week for weekly report generation.

S3 · Raw Posts Sync Triggerable Without Auth routes.ts:2866 · CRITICAL

POST /api/raw-posts/sync — no auth. Anyone can trigger a full raw posts sync from the external data app — a long-running operation pulling tens of thousands of records.

S4 · Internal Data App URL Exposed routes.ts:860 · CRITICAL

GET /api/data-app/status — no auth, returns url: dataAppUrl. This exposes the value of the DATA_APP_URL environment variable to any anonymous caller.

S5 · Wrong Middleware Level on Destructive Routes CRITICAL

Six routes use requireAuth (any logged-in user) instead of requireAdmin. A trial user with an active session can currently:

  • DELETE /api/reports/:id — delete any report
  • DELETE /api/weekly-reports/:id — delete any weekly report
  • GET /api/sync-reports — trigger a full report sync from the data app
  • POST /api/data-app/sync-latest — trigger a latest-date sync
  • POST /api/data-app/sync/:reportId — sync a specific report
  • POST /api/data-app/sync-all-for-date — sync all reports for a date
S6 · Monitoring Profiles Fully Unprotected routes.ts:3264–3323 · CRITICAL

GET/POST/PUT/DELETE /api/monitoring-profiles — no auth on any of the four methods. Any anonymous user can read, create, update, and delete all monitoring profiles.

S7 · Watched Keywords Fully Unprotected routes.ts:3582–3645 · CRITICAL

GET/POST/DELETE /api/watched-keywords — no auth on any of the three methods. Anyone can read, add, and delete watched keywords.

S8 · Custom Reports Readable Without Auth routes.ts:3521–3542 · CRITICAL

GET /api/custom-reports and GET /api/custom-reports/:id — no auth. Any anonymous user can read all saved custom reports, which contain user-generated analyses and saved configurations.

Medium security issues (M1–M3)

M1 · Research Session History Publicly Readable routes.ts:1655–1708, 1975–2030 · MEDIUM

X Monitor: GET /api/x-monitor/sessions, GET /api/x-monitor/sessions/:id/messages, POST /api/x-monitor/sessions, DELETE /api/x-monitor/sessions/:id — no auth on session CRUD.

AI Assistant: same pattern — no auth on session CRUD.

These sessions contain the complete history of Palestinian media analysis research queries — sensitive internal organizational work. The actual AI query endpoints DO correctly use requireActiveTrial (protecting against financial abuse), but session data is readable by anyone.

M2 · Resend Verification Code Has No Rate Limit auth.ts:227 · MEDIUM

POST /api/auth/resend-code — no rate limiter applied. An attacker who knows a target user's email can flood their inbox with verification code emails indefinitely.

M3 · Webhook Health Leaks Configuration Status routes.ts:850 · MEDIUM

GET /api/webhook/health returns apiKeyConfigured: !!process.env.WEBHOOK_API_KEY. This tells any caller whether the server has API key protection enabled — unnecessary operational detail.

Bugs (B1–B2)

B1 · Trial Expiry Doesn't Persist to DB server/auth.ts:328–336

When a trial has expired, login correctly returns 403 with code ACCOUNT_DEACTIVATED — but storage.deactivateUser() is never called. The account stays non-deactivated in the database. Consequences:

  • Studio Admin shows the user as active, not deactivated — no visibility into expired trial users.
  • The "Reactivate" workflow in Studio Admin doesn't apply (user doesn't appear deactivated).
  • Login history records failureReason: "account_deactivated" but the DB says active — misleading audit trail.
B2 · Custom Report Delete Has a Null Ownership Bypass (Fragile) routes.ts:3563

DELETE /api/custom-reports/:id: ownership check is report.userId !== req.session?.userId. If report.userId is null and the caller is unauthenticated (req.session?.userId === undefined), then null !== undefined is true — so the delete correctly fails. But this is fragile by design: the route should require authentication before the ownership check runs.

Logical / design gaps (L1–L5)

Noted, not blocking — but each is worth a Studio Admin or product-roadmap conversation.

#GapDescription
L1Sessions shared globallyX Monitor and AI Assistant sessions have no user ownership — all logged-in users see each other's research sessions. Significant confidentiality concern for a political analysis tool.
L2No admin alert for new waitlist signupsAdmins must manually check Studio Admin. Industry standard is an email when a new user registers for the waitlist.
L32FA shares email channel with password resetEmail-only 2FA provides no protection if the inbox is compromised. True 2FA would use a TOTP app like Google Authenticator.
L4Role-change side effectsPromoting a user to admin doesn't clear their trialEndsAt. Admin accounts technically have expired trials, which could cause issues if trial checks are ever applied to them.
L5Invitation + waitlist simultaneous stateA user can exist on the waitlist and have a pending invitation at the same time.

Feature requests assessed

All three are industry-standard and worth building:

  • Downloadable Recent Prompts — Standard ops tooling. Useful for query analysis and content auditing.
  • Downloadable Survey Responses — Standard user research export. Useful for analyzing signup trends.
  • Email control panel — Entirely standard for SaaS ops dashboards. Being able to pause scheduled emails (e.g., to stop birthday emails during a system incident) without redeploying is a genuine operational need.

Auth implementation review — clean bill of health

After the security wave (introducing bcrypt-hashed passwords in admin_users, rate limiting, CORS hardening), the changed files were reviewed end-to-end. All four security enhancements are correctly implemented, properly secured, backward compatible, and non-destructive to the existing data pipeline.

auth.py — verdicts
Line(s)AreaVerdict
5-8New imports (bcrypt, time, threading)Correct — all necessary
21-24Rate limit constants + thread-safe storeCorrect — 5 attempts / 300s window is reasonable
27-32_cleanup_rate_limit_store()Correct — removes expired entries, prevents memory leak
35-48check_rate_limit()Correct — properly locked, double-checks window expiry, returns retry_after
51-58record_failed_attempt()Correct — resets window if expired, otherwise increments
61-63reset_rate_limit()Correct — clears on successful login
66-74hash_password / verify_password_hashCorrect — bcrypt with salt, exception-safe verification
84-94verify_password()Correct — backward-compatible: only allows the env-var fallback for emails not yet provisioned in the database
db.py + api.py — verdicts
db.py
  • Table schema (admin_users) — SERIAL PK, unique email, password_hash NOT NULL, is_active default TRUE, timestamps. Correct.
  • Index on email — fast lookups for login. Correct.
  • get_admin_user_by_email — parameterized query, lowercases email. Correct.
  • create_admin_user — UPSERT (ON CONFLICT updates hash and reactivates). Re-creating a deactivated user reactivates them — intentional and useful.
  • list_admin_users — does NOT return password_hash (safe for API exposure).
  • update_admin_last_login — simple timestamp update. Correct.
  • deactivate_admin_user — soft-delete (sets is_active=FALSE), returns success/failure.
api.py
  • DB init + admin seed (lines 37-52) — Only seeds if user doesn't already exist; uses ADMIN_PASSWORD env var.
  • CORS (lines 76-98) — Auto-detects Replit domains, explicit methods/headers, no wildcard fallback.
  • require_admin (lines 115-130) — After JWT validation, fetches DB user and checks is_active. If user is not in DB, passes through (preserves backward compatibility).
  • Login endpoint (lines 133-175) — Rate limit check first → domain validation → deactivation check → password verification → rate limit reset on success → last_login update.
  • Log endpoints (lines 6976-7017) — All 6 endpoints now have Depends(require_admin).
  • Admin user management (lines 7120-7159) — List, create (with 8-char minimum password and domain validation), deactivate (with self-deactivation prevention).
Scenarios traced — all pass
  • Hassan logs in → DB user found → bcrypt verified → last_login updated → token issued. Works.
  • Someone with @socialstudio.me email but no DB account → DB user not found → falls back to ADMIN_PASSWORD env var → if correct, token issued. Works (backward compatible).
  • Deactivated user with valid JWT → require_admin checks DB → is_active=FALSE → 403 returned. Token effectively invalidated.
  • Deactivated user tries to log in → Caught at line 156 before password check. Blocked.
  • Brute force attack → 5 failures → 6th attempt gets 429 for ~5 minutes. Working.
  • Public endpoints (reports, categories) → No require_admin dependency → still accessible. No regression.

Original audit gaps (the data app)

From the original strategic audit, before the security wave landed:

  • No data retention policy — historical CSVs and DB rows accumulate indefinitely.
  • No scheduled/automated processing at the data-app layer beyond client-app cron — manual triggers required for legacy pipeline.
  • No comparative analysis between today vs prior days at the data-app level (client-side topic-connections is same-day only by design).
  • No email/push notifications for the data-app side.
  • Missing export capabilities on certain admin surfaces.
  • No topic override / manual control — once a topic is generated, manual rebalancing requires a re-analysis run.
  • Two admin interfaces (FastAPI dashboard + Streamlit) — overlapping capabilities, distinct paths.
25 / 28 · Tasks & Audit Findings Source: System Audit Tasklist · Task #50 · S1–S8 · M1–M3 · B1–B2 · L1–L5 — full inventory
26 / Operations

Open questions & tensions.

Unresolved threads. Some are awaiting an answer from the data-app team; some are calls the product team has not yet made; some are off-limits redesigns explicitly preserved.

Five live tensions

1 · Actor-enrichment limitation gates entity-driven features

81.57% of actor records come back as type: "unknown" and most normalizedNameEn values are empty (.local/docs/data-app-ticket-actor-enrichment.md). This blocks the topic-connections synthesis sentence and shared-entities chip row (gated behind ENABLE_ENTITY_FEATURES), and it degrades the “Key Actors” card and “Actor Influence” list — currently rendering with everything lumped under “Unknown”. The remediation is upstream; the client cannot fix this without inventing data.

2 · Sentiment-comparison and confidence-gated features not currently feasible

topics.sentiment_confidence is 100% empty and sentiment_label is mixed-language and mixed-case; any sentiment-confidence-thresholding or naive sentiment-equality feature would silently drop most rows.

3 · social_media_posts is still receiving inserts

Despite Task #71 being merged to stop them. 50,571 inserts in last 30 days. We don't yet know whether the change failed to cover a code path, another writer exists, or verification was incomplete. Until resolved, any feature decision that depends on “the legacy table is dead” is on uncertain footing.

4 · Multi-platform UI vs Telegram-only ingestion

raw_posts is 100% Telegram; non-Telegram content reaches the user only through pre-baked viral_posts and dominant_platform. Posts-Explorer and Analytics-Board UIs that imply platform diversity are accurate-but-thin in practice. Whether to surface this honestly (e.g. a “Telegram only” affordance on Posts Explorer) is an open product call.

5 · Topic-connections held polish

Visual-polish work on topic-connections (warm-beige token, items #5–#8 from the held polish pass — proposed Task #76) was deliberately held until option-D landed. That has happened; the tension now is between resuming the held polish and the broader open question of whether the topic-connections card should be rethought given how much of its planned surface (synthesis, shared entities) is gated off. Not yet called.

Open questions for the data-app / NLP pipeline team

  • actorsStructured.type vocabulary: is the canonical set documented? At least 15 distinct values appear in production. Which are intended and which are NLP errors? (Notably: person vs people, organization vs entity vs group.)
  • raw_posts.nlp_actors shape: JSON-encoded array stored as a string, comma-joined name, or actual JSON array column? Today it's universally a JSON string.
  • raw_posts.nlp_sentiment_label = '{}' (1,345 rows): what produces a literal '{}' string in a label field? Likely a serializer error.
  • trend_direction casing/decoration: four variants for three concepts. Intentional (two pipelines emitting differently) or pipeline drift?
  • sentiment_label language coupling: is the data-app-side localization intentional? If so, the client needs a per-language equality table; if not, it should always be lowercase English with a separate display-localization step on the client.
  • viewpoints, sentiment_confidence, detailed_analysis: declared but never (or rarely) populated. Planned, deprecated, or never wired up?
  • Non-Telegram content provenance: Facebook + Twitter content reaches topics.viral_posts and topics.dominant_platform but never raw_posts. What is that ingestion path?
  • social_media_posts writes: Task #71 was merged to stop writes. 50,571 arrived in the last 30 days. Where are they coming from? (raw-posts-sync.ts? narrative-report-service.ts? legacy code path?)
  • dashboard_section taxonomy: mixes registry slugs (PS_Media, INT_Media) with looser legacy values (News, Government) and the typo INT_Verificaiton. Authoritative list?
  • daily_reports.category vs topics.category: are these supposed to agree? 126 reports use legacy slugs vs 2,763 topics — different numerators suggest the data-app emits each independently.

Open questions from the Pipeline C audit (§10)

  • profile_affiliation consumption: the spec lists it as a source-distinguishing input; Pipeline C does not branch on it.
  • Source-distinguishing columns at the door: should they be added to required_columns at processing.py:1508, or is silent degradation the intended behaviour?
  • Stage 7 dispatch failures: the webhook gate skips delivery silently when topics == 0 after pruning; is the synthetic failure row in webhook_deliveries reliable enough to alert on?
  • Dev fixture vs production: when does Pipeline C's first production posts.csv ingestion happen? The dev environment has not yet seen it.

Six dimensions of the audit

The structuring framework for the upcoming audit engagement, organized by priority. Each dimension carries its own set of open questions. Source: Audit Briefing §9.

Dim 1 · highest

System architecture & data flow

  • Push vs. pull balance. The push (webhook) path is primary; pull is admin-triggered. Right split, or should pull become automated? What's the cost of a missed webhook today?
  • Two connection pools (reads vs. sync). Still the right partitioning, or has Neon's pooler made it obsolete?
  • The social_media_posts legacy table. Inserts still arriving despite stop-writes intent. Find the writer or drop the table?
  • Raw posts sync — Telegram-only. Non-Telegram content reaches topics but not raw_posts. Contract bug or by design?
  • Boundaries. "Client doesn't add work to data app" is load-bearing. Are there cases where pushing computation upstream (e.g. IDF-weighted Jaccard) is actually better for caching and consistency?
Dim 2

Data quality & integrity practices

  • actors_structured.type vocabulary — 15 distinct values, undocumented. Canonical set? Which are NLP errors vs intentional?
  • Casing variants in dominant_platform, sentiment_label, trend_direction. Pipeline drift between two emitters? Path to a single canonical contract?
  • raw_posts.nlp_actors shape. Always a JSON string in practice; client expects array. Pick one and update the contract.
  • Dead fields (viewpoints, sentiment_confidence, six *_analysis text fields). Plan, deprecate, or wire up?
  • The 81% unknown actor type. What would it take upstream to bring this down? Model, prompt, or source material?
  • Telegram-only raw_posts. What ingestion path produces non-Telegram viral_posts content?
Dim 3

Prompt engineering & LLM call quality

  • Single call_llm wrapper. Good or load-bearing? Is the registry pattern still the right shape as call types grow?
  • Prompt assembly layers (system + preset + category + hardcoded + conditional). Right factoring? Should category-specific behavior move into preset variants?
  • JSON-mode handling. topic_analysis uses response_format: json_object; fallback retry triggers on JSONDecodeError. Failure rate today? Is structured-output (function-calling-style) better?
  • Language-mix retry. IMPORTANT suffix added when an English response contains Arabic. How often does this fire? Structural fix?
  • Model split: gpt-4.1-mini for topic analysis, gpt-4.1 for executive summary. Why this split? Re-evaluated against newer models?
  • Three client-side AI surfaces (AI Assistant, X-Monitor, Workbench Finisher). Model choice per surface deliberate? Routing layer?
Dim 4

Pipeline reliability & failure modes

  • Retry semantics. tenacity 4 attempts on transient errors in call_llm. Failure-mode taxonomy beyond transient-API? (Token limit, content filter, schema violation, language-mix, latency timeout.)
  • Idempotency. Webhook is push-from-data-app. Duplicate deliveries? Partial deliveries (some topics succeed, others fail)?
  • Job-run observability. job_runs and prompt_logs tables exist. Actual instrumentation surface? Queryable for SLO-style monitoring?
  • The sync rewrite. Timestamp-windowed cursors with a sync_ranges table, advisory locks, batch checkpointing. Living up to its design under load?
  • spending_logs. Actual token-spend trend? Cost SLO?
Dim 5 · the big one

Agentic / autonomous capability roadmap

  • AI Assistant. Today: chat surface backed by Grok/OpenAI with topics and raw_posts as grounding. Path to a tool-using agent that can pull from the database, run aggregations, and assemble cited responses? Right tool surface — direct SQL, named-query layer, or MCP-style tools?
  • X-Monitor. Today: session-scoped keyword run. Could it become an autonomous monitoring agent flagging emergent patterns within the same-day scope (without crossing the cross-day rejection)?
  • Report Workbench AI Finisher. Today: takes serialized canvas and finishes prose. Could it become an authoring agent proposing blocks based on the day's topics? Integrity guardrails (no inventing entities, every claim cited)?
  • Sub-agent architecture for the data app. Currently flat call_llm wrapper. Would a sub-agent pattern (topic-analysis agent that re-prompts itself, requests more context, or escalates) materially improve quality vs cost?
  • Where is agentic appropriate and where is it not? The product's hard rejection of LLM narrative generation in v1 means agentic work has to live within the integrity boundary. Safe envelope?
  • Tooling & framework. Made framework choices yet (LangGraph, vanilla orchestration, Anthropic's tool-use, OpenAI Assistants API)? Should we?
Dim 6

Cost / token economics / model choice

  • Per-topic token cost. With max_completion_tokens=8192 and one analysis call per language per topic plus retries, what's the actual per-day spend on the data-app side?
  • Model split economics. gpt-4.1-mini vs gpt-4.1. Cost-quality trade-off been measured?
  • Client-side AI cost. Grok + OpenAI across three surfaces. Per-session and per-DAU spend?
  • Embedding strategy. Are we doing embeddings anywhere? Should we be?
  • Caching. Anything cached at the LLM layer? Topic-analysis results persist in topics.*_analysis, but retries and within-session calls?
  • Rate limits. Hit any provider rate-limits in production? Headroom?

Proposed agenda for the audit meeting

A 90-minute slot, working backwards from the priority list:

SlotTopicNotes
0–10 minProduct framing + non-goalsUse §1 + §8 as anchors. The negative definitions are load-bearing.
10–25 minSystem architecture walk-through§2 + §3, the two Mermaid diagrams. Confirm consultant has mental model before deeper.
25–45 minData quality & integrity§7. Walk the unknown-actor worked example. Where most architectural nuance lives.
45–65 minPrompt engineering & LLM call quality§4 LLM call inventory + §9 Dim 3 questions.
65–80 minAgentic roadmap scoping§9 Dim 5 — the framing question, then specific surfaces.
80–90 minReliability + cost (lightning round)§9 Dims 4 and 6. Likely follow-up async.

Off-limits redesigns

Per the audit briefing §8, the AI integration / agentic systems consultant will not entertain redesigns of the following:

  • The contractual boundary that “the client does not add work to the data app”.
  • The rejection of cross-day topic linkage in v1.
  • The rejection of LLM-generated narrative reporting in v1.
  • OTPs and security mail staying on the transactional pipeline (Resend).
  • The “honesty over polish” data integrity principle.
26 / 28 · Open Questions & Tensions Source: Audit Briefing §8–§9 · System Reference §7 · Data Inventory §7 · Pipeline C §10
27 / Operations

Glossary & reference.

Disambiguating terms that appear across the documentation set, plus pointers to the canonical reference documents this site is derived from.

Glossary

Report (daily)
A per-day, per-category envelope produced by the data app. One day generates many reports (one per category; bilingual — language is ar or en, ~50/50). Stored in daily_reports.
Report (weekly)
A locale-filtered (PS / IL / INT) aggregation of a week's daily reports. Stored in weekly_reports.
Topic
The data app's unit of NLP-derived grouping within a daily report. Linked to one daily report, one category, one language. Lifespan: same as its report.
Source
Disambiguate by context. Publishing source — the upstream account/outlet. Data source — the data app, as the system that provides payloads. Signal source — the field a chart is reading from (e.g. topics.source_analytics_data).
Post (raw)
raw_posts — the live ingestion surface, currently Telegram-only, ~168k rows, with NLP fields. Backs Posts Explorer, Analytics Board, AI Assistant context.
Post (legacy)
social_media_posts — residue of two earlier pivots (CSV upload + in-process LLM, then per-report posts payload). Currently still receiving inserts despite Task #71's intent to stop them.
Viral post
A pre-baked, per-topic snippet (viral_posts jsonb on topics) chosen by the data app. The only place Facebook/Twitter content surfaces, since raw_posts is Telegram-only.
Distinctive keyword
In topic-connections, a keyword that appears in one topic but not the other being compared.
Watched keyword
A user-tracked term that drives filtering on Posts Explorer and influences report-generation context.
Evidence block
A typed unit collected into the workbench (ai_response, x_monitor_result, post, chart_snapshot, topic_discovery, narrative_text).
Category
A taxonomy slug like PS_Media, IL_Government, INT_Solidarity, plus legacy slugs that route through LEGACY_CATEGORY_MAP in shared/categories.ts. ~21.65% of topics use legacy slugs.
Dashboard section
A separate, looser taxonomy on raw_posts.dashboard_section. Mixes registry slugs with values not in the registry (News, Government) and a known typo (INT_Verificaiton).
Pipeline B
The legacy per-category processing pipeline. Writes to topics.
Pipeline C
The unified, all-source same-day pipeline. Writes to unified_topics with separate viewpoints_ar / viewpoints_en.
call_llm
The data app's single LLM entry point at processing.py:818. Routes by call_type through LLM_CALL_REGISTRY.
Preset
A named LLM configuration in seed_presets.py: Factual Reporter (default) · Media Monitor · Analyst · Political Analyst.
Methodology terms (added v2.0)
Methodology
The discipline of social-listening as a research practice — what it can capture reliably, what it cannot capture without correction, and how to communicate findings honestly. See section 04.
Ecological validity
How accurately data reflects real-world cultural dynamics, agenda-setting, and narrative formation within online communities. Social listening has high ecological validity. Methodology §2.
External validity
How accurately findings generalize from the study population (social media users who post) to the target population of interest (the general public). Social listening has limited external validity by default. Methodology §2.
Convenience sample
A non-random sample drawn from whatever is accessible. Social media data is by definition a convenience sample of the vocal, connected, platform-active subset of any group.
Lurker rule
The empirical observation that the active-vs-passive user ratio on social platforms is roughly 9:1 — about 91% of registered users never post publicly.
Population bias / Self-selection bias / Temporal bias / Platform bias / Algorithmic amplification bias / Content moderation bias / NLP & language bias
The seven structural biases inventoried in the methodology. Each must be identified and disclosed for every engagement. Methodology §3.
Triangulation
Combining social-listening data with at least one probability-based source (a survey, a published poll, an official statistic) to elevate confidence. Methodology §6 method 3; Denzin (1978).
MRP — Multilevel Regression with Post-stratification
A two-stage statistical technique for converting non-representative datasets into population-representative estimates by reweighting against known demographic distributions. Methodology §6 method 4; Gelman & Little (1997); Wang et al. (2015).
Stratified purposive sampling
Dividing the potential data into meaningful strata (platform × language × account type × region) and sampling proportionally rather than collecting all available data from a keyword stream. Methodology §6 method 1.
MoE — Margin of Error
For social listening, only the statistical floor is calculable (Z × √(p̂(1−p̂)/n)); the systematic-bias component cannot be reduced to a single number. Methodology §5.
AAPOR
American Association for Public Opinion Research. AAPOR's Transparency Initiative is the closest formally endorsed disclosure standard applicable to social-listening data. Methodology §9; AAPOR (2023).
Signal Confirmed / Signal Detected / Signal Emerging / Artifact–Verify
The four-tier confidence label system. Every quantitative finding in a research-grade report carries one of these labels. The product does not currently use this taxonomy — see the "What this means in this product" callout in methodology §7.
Descriptive vs inferential
The most important language rule: descriptive language for what the data shows; inferential language only when cross-validated. Methodology §8.
Olteanu et al. (2019) / Hargittai (2020) / Wang et al. (2015)
The foundational methodology references. Olteanu identified five classes of social-data bias; Hargittai demonstrated systematic demographic skew in digital trace data; Wang et al. (the "Xbox study") showed MRP can recover population estimates from severely biased samples. Methodology §10.
Jaccard similarity
Set-overlap measure: |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B|. Used in the client-side Topic Connections panel as IDF-weighted Jaccard against keyword sets and (gated-off) actor sets.
HDBSCAN
Density-based clustering algorithm. Pipeline C's whole-day clustering uses HDBSCAN with min_cluster_size=8, min_samples=3 on L2-normalized embeddings.
IDF
Inverse Document Frequency. Weights keywords by rarity across the corpus; used in the topic-overlap scorer to downweight common terms.
ADR 0010
The architectural decision record formalizing the strict-coexistence rule that Pipeline C never mutates Pipeline B state. Cited inline in the dispatcher, orchestrator, and webhook hook.
Cohesion diagnostics
Per-topic computed metrics (cohesion score, single-author share, low-similarity posts) that Pipeline C records for every cluster but does not use to gate publication. Future "Phase 2" reject thresholds candidate.

Update cadence

This documentation is a living reference but requires a human trigger to stay current. Revisit when:

  • A major new surface ships (something a user would name when describing what the app does).
  • A core product principle changes or a new constraint is adopted.
  • The system architecture changes materially — new component, removed component, new external integration, or a meaningful shift in the data-app ↔ client contract.
  • Otherwise, quarterly review for staleness.

If you read this and notice it has drifted from current reality, flag it rather than silently working around it.

27 / 28 · Glossary & Reference End of documentation
